options.D: Set up Studio client user endpoint validation by using the aws:PrincipalTag IAM policy condition.

questions.explanation: The company is using Amazon SageMaker Studio notebooks. Access is allowed through a VPN, meaning users are coming from known, fixed IP ranges. The concern is unauthorized access via presigned URLs, which could potentially be used outside the trusted network. Why aws:sourceIp is the right choice: The aws:sourceIp condition in IAM policies allows you to restrict access based on the client's IP address. This is perfect for VPN- based setups where you know the IP range. It ensures that only users accessing from allowed IPs (e.g., your VPN subnet) can access SageMaker Studio resources, even if they have a valid presigned URL. This directly mitigates the risk of URL misuse from outside the VPN.

questions.question: An ML engineer needs to merge and transform data from two sources to retrain an existing ML model. One data source consists of .csv files that are stored in an Amazon S3 bucket. Each .csv file consists of millions of records. The other data source is an Amazon Aurora DB cluster. The result of the merge process must be written to a second S3 bucket. The ML engineer needs to perform this merge-and-transform task every week. Which solution will meet these requirements with the LEAST operational overhead?

options.A: Create a transient Amazon EMR cluster every week. Use the cluster to run an Apache Spark job to merge and transform the data.
options.B: Create a weekly AWS Glue job that uses the Apache Spark engine. Use DynamicFrame native operations to merge and transform the data.
options.C: Create an AWS Lambda function that runs Apache Spark code every week to merge and transform the data. Configure the Lambda function to connect to the initial S3 bucket and the DB cluster.
options.D: Create an AWS Batch job that runs Apache Spark code on Amazon EC2 instances every week. Configure the Spark code to save the data from the EC2 instances to the second S3 bucket.

questions.explanation: create a weekly AWS Glue job that uses the Apache Spark engine. Use DynamicFrame native operations to merge and transform the data. AWS Glue for ETL with Low Overhead AWS Glue is a fully managed, serverless ETL (Extract, Transform, and Load) service. It is specifically designed for tasks like the weekly merge and transform operation described in the prompt. Its features contribute to minimal operational overhead: Serverless Execution: You don't need to provision, manage, or scale any servers. AWS Glue handles all the underlying infrastructure, including the Apache Spark cluster, automatically. This eliminates the operational burden of managing EC2 instances, EMR clusters, or Lambda function environments. Scheduling: AWS Glue jobs can be scheduled directly from the console or API to run on a weekly basis, perfectly matching the required frequency. DynamicFrame: AWS Glue's DynamicFrame is an extension of Apache Spark DataFrames that is optimized for ETL tasks on semi-structured data. It can automatically infer schema and handle schema drift, which is common in data coming from different sources, making the transformation process easier to implement and less prone to errors. This reduces the development effort required for the transformation logic.

questions.question: An ML engineer has deployed an Amazon SageMaker model to a serverless endpoint in production. The model is invoked by the InvokeEndpoint API operation. The model's latency in production is higher than the baseline latency in the test environment. The ML engineer thinks that the increase in latency is because of model startup time. What should the ML engineer do to confirm or deny this hypothesis?

options.A: Schedule a SageMaker Model Monitor job. Observe metrics about model quality.
options.B: Schedule a SageMaker Model Monitor job with Amazon CloudWatch metrics enabled.
options.C: Enable Amazon CloudWatch metrics. Observe the ModelSetupTime metric in the SageMaker namespace.
options.D: Enable Amazon CloudWatch metrics. Observe the ModelLoadingWaitTime metric in the SageMaker namespace.

questions.explanation: enable Amazon CloudWatch metrics and observe the ModelLoadingWaitTime metric in the SageMaker namespace. When an ML engineer suspects that an increase in latency is due to a model's startup time, they need a way to measure the time it takes for the model to be ready to serve requests. Amazon SageMaker automatically publishes a variety of metrics to Amazon CloudWatch, which can be used to diagnose performance issues.

questions.question: An ML engineer needs to ensure that a dataset complies with regulations for personally identifiable information (PII). The ML engineer will use the data to train an ML model on Amazon SageMaker instances. SageMaker must not use any of the PII. Which solution will meet these requirements in the MOST operationally efficient way?

options.A: Use the Amazon Comprehend DetectPiiEntities API call to redact the PII from the data. Store the data in an Amazon S3 bucket. Access the S3 bucket from the SageMaker instances for model training.
options.B: Use the Amazon Comprehend DetectPiiEntities API call to redact the PII from the data. Store the data in an Amazon Elastic File System (Amazon EFS) file system. Mount the EFS file system to the SageMaker instances for model training.
options.C: Use AWS Glue DataBrew to cleanse the dataset of PII. Store the data in an Amazon Elastic File System (Amazon EFS) file system. Mount the EFS file system to the SageMaker instances for model training.
options.D: Use Amazon Macie for automatic discovery of PII in the data. Remove the PII. Store the data in an Amazon S3 bucket. Mount the S3 bucket to the SageMaker instances for model training.

questions.explanation: A. Use the Amazon Comprehend DetectPiiEntities API call to redact the PII from the data. Store the data in an Amazon S3 bucket. Access the S3 bucket from the SageMaker instances for model training. This solution minimizes operational overhead by leveraging fully managed, serverless AWS services that integrate seamlessly. Amazon Comprehend: The DetectPiiEntities API is a ready-to-use, pre-trained service. It requires no model building, training, or infrastructure management to redact PII from text data. You simply make an API call, and the service handles the detection and redaction automatically. Amazon S3: S3 is the native and most common data source for Amazon SageMaker. SageMaker is optimized to pull data directly from S3, which eliminates the need to manage a separate file system or data pipeline. S3's high scalability and durability make it an ideal and cost-effective place to store large datasets. Seamless Integration: The workflow of using Comprehend to process data and then storing it in S3 for SageMaker to consume is a standard and well-supported pattern on AWS. It avoids the complexities of mounting network file systems or managing custom scripts on compute instances, which are required by other options.

questions.question: A company must install a custom script on any newly created Amazon SageMaker notebook instances. Which solution will meet this requirement with the LEAST operational overhead?

options.A: Create a lifecycle configuration script to install the custom script when a new SageMaker notebook is created. Attach the lifecycle configuration to every new SageMaker notebook as part of the creation steps.
options.B: Create a custom Amazon Elastic Container Registry (Amazon ECR) image that contains the custom script. Push the ECR image to a Docker registry. Attach the Docker image to a SageMaker Studio domain. Select the kernel to run as part of the SageMaker notebook.
options.C: Create a custom package index repository. Use AWS CodeArtifact to manage the installation of the custom script. Set up AWS PrivateLink endpoints to connect CodeArtifact to the SageMaker instance. Install the script.
options.D: Store the custom script in Amazon S3. Create an AWS Lambda function to install the custom script on new SageMaker notebooks. Configure Amazon EventBridge to invoke the Lambda function when a new SageMaker notebook is initialized.

questions.explanation: create a lifecycle configuration script to install the custom script when a new SageMaker notebook is created and attach it to every new SageMaker notebook as part of the creation steps. How Lifecycle Configurations Minimize Overhead SageMaker lifecycle configuration scripts are shell scripts that you can attach to a notebook instance. They run automatically during specific events, such as when the instance is created or started. This makes them the perfect tool for the company's requirement. Automation: The "on-create" script runs only once when a new notebook instance is created. This ensures the custom script is installed from the very beginning. By attaching this configuration to every new notebook, you automate the installation process without manual intervention. Low Management: This is a built-in feature of SageMaker. You don't have to manage external resources like a custom Docker image registry (ECR) or a separate serverless function (Lambda) and its associated event triggers. The script and its configuration are managed directly within SageMaker. Purpose-Built: Lifecycle configurations are designed specifically for tasks like customizing the notebook environment, installing packages, or configuring settings. This makes it a purpose-built solution that is simpler and more direct than other approaches.

questions.question: A company is building a real-time data processing pipeline for an ecommerce application. The application generates a high volume of clickstream data that must be ingested, processed, and visualized in near real time. The company needs a solution that supports SQL for data processing and Jupyter notebooks for interactive analysis. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Use Amazon Data Firehose to ingest the data. Create an AWS Lambda function to process the data. Store the processed data in Amazon S3. Use Amazon QuickSight to visualize the data.
options.B: Use Amazon Kinesis Data Streams to ingest the data. Use Amazon Data Firehose to transform the data. Use Amazon Athena to process the data. Use Amazon QuickSight to visualize the data.
options.C: Use Amazon Managed Streaming for Apache Kafka (Amazon MSK) to ingest the data. Use AWS Glue with PySpark to process the data. Store the processed data in Amazon S3. Use Amazon QuickSight to visualize the data.
options.D: Use Amazon Managed Streaming for Apache Kafka (Amazon MSK) to ingest the data. Use Amazon Managed Service for Apache Flink to process the data. Use the built-in Flink dashboard to visualize the data.

questions.explanation: use Amazon Managed Streaming for Apache Kafka (Amazon MSK) to ingest the data, use Amazon Managed Service for Apache Flink to process the data, and use the built-in Flink dashboard for visualization. This solution combines purpose-built, fully managed AWS services to address each requirement of the real- time data processing pipeline. Ingestion (High-Volume Clickstream Data): Amazon MSK is a fully managed service that makes it easy to build and run applications that use Apache Kafka to process streaming data. It's a highly scalable and durable solution, making it an excellent choice for ingesting a high volume of clickstream data. Processing (SQL): Amazon Managed Service for Apache Flink is a powerful, fully managed service for real- time data processing. It has native support for Flink SQL, which allows you to perform complex stream processing tasks using standard SQL syntax. This directly meets the company's requirement for using SQL for data processing. Interactive Analysis (Jupyter Notebooks): Managed Service for Apache Flink provides Jupyter-based notebooks (specifically, Apache Zeppelin notebooks) that are fully integrated into the service. This allows for interactive analysis of the streaming data, enabling developers to prototype and explore their data in real time, directly fulfilling the Jupyter notebook requirement. Visualization: While Flink itself offers a built-in dashboard for monitoring, the data processed by Flink can be easily sent to a sink like Amazon S3 or a database, which can then be visualized by services like Amazon QuickSight. This provides flexibility for visualization needs.

questions.question: A medical company needs to store clinical data. The data includes personally identifiable information (PII) and protected health information (PHI). An ML engineer needs to implement a solution to ensure that the PII and PHI are not used to train ML models. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Store the clinical data in Amazon S3 buckets. Use AWS Glue DataBrew to mask the PII and PHI before the data is used for model training.
options.B: Upload the clinical data to an Amazon Redshift database. Use built-in SQL stored procedures to automatically classify and mask the PII and PHI before the data is used for model training.
options.C: Use Amazon Comprehend to detect and mask the PII before the data is used for model training. Use Amazon Comprehend Medical to detect and mask the PHI before the data is used for model training.
options.D: Create an AWS Lambda function to encrypt the PII and PHI. Program the Lambda function to save the encrypted data to an Amazon S3 bucket for model training.

questions.explanation: use Amazon Comprehend to detect and mask the PII and Amazon Comprehend Medical to detect and mask the PHI before the data is used for model training. This approach leverages two specialized, fully managed AWS services designed for natural language processing (NLP) tasks, which makes them perfect for handling clinical data. Amazon Comprehend is a service that uses machine learning to find insights in unstructured text. It includes a built-in feature to detect and redact (mask) Personally Identifiable Information (PII) such as names, addresses, credit card numbers, and social security numbers. Amazon Comprehend Medical is a HIPAA-eligible version of Comprehend, specifically trained to understand and process medical text. It can detect and mask Protected Health Information (PHI), which includes medical conditions.

questions.question: An ML engineer is developing a classification model. The ML engineer needs to use custom libraries in processing jobs, training jobs, and pipelines in Amazon SageMaker. Which solution will provide this functionality with the LEAST implementation effort?

options.A: Manually install the libraries in the SageMaker containers.
options.B: Build a custom Docker container that includes the required libraries. Host the container in Amazon Elastic Container Registry (Amazon ECR). Use the ECR image in the SageMaker jobs and pipelines.
options.C: Create a SageMaker notebook instance to host the jobs. Create an AWS Lambda function to install the libraries on the notebook instance when the notebook instance starts. Configure the SageMaker jobs and pipelines to run on the notebook instance.
options.D: Run code for the libraries externally on Amazon EC2 instances. Store the results in Amazon S3. Import the results into the SageMaker jobs and pipelines.

questions.explanation: The best approach for incorporating custom libraries into SageMaker jobs and pipelines with minimal effort is to build a custom Docker container. Option B stands out because it leverages Docker's containerization capabilities to encapsulate the environment and dependencies needed for the ML model. By creating a custom Docker image that includes the necessary libraries and hosting it in Amazon ECR (Elastic Container Registry), the ML engineer can ensure that the exact same environment is used consistently across all processing, training, and pipeline stages. SageMaker natively supports using ECR images, making integration seamless. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/your-algorithms-containers.html Option A requires manual installation of libraries in SageMaker containers. This is not practical, scalable or maintainable as the ML engineer must configure each container individually. This option would create inconsistencies and potential errors. Option C suggests using a SageMaker notebook instance and Lambda function. Notebook instances are primarily intended for interactive development and exploration, not for hosting production workloads. Relying on Lambda functions for installing libraries on notebook instance startup adds unnecessary complexity and overhead. Option D involves running code externally on EC2 instances and transferring data to S3. It unnecessarily introduces extra complexity, increases latency, and makes the model building process less streamlined. It also adds extra cost. Docker containerization (option B) is the most suitable approach because it is portable, reproducible, and simplifies deployment. It ensures consistency across environments and is the recommended way to include custom libraries in SageMaker jobs. https://docs.docker.com/get-started/

questions.question: An ML engineer is deploying a trained model to an Amazon SageMaker endpoint. The ML engineer needs to receive alerts when data quality issues occur in production. Which solution will meet this requirement?

options.A: Configure an Amazon CloudWatch metric alarm and a corresponding action to send an Amazon Simple Notification Service (Amazon SNS) notification.
options.B: Integrate the SageMaker endpoint with a SageMaker Clarify processing job. Configure an Amazon CloudWatch alarm to provide alerts.
options.C: Configure a monitoring job in SageMaker Model Monitor. Integrate Model Monitor with Amazon CloudWatch to provide alerts.
options.D: Configure a data flow in SageMaker Data Wrangler. Integrate Data Wrangler with Amazon CloudWatch to provide alerts.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is C because it utilizes SageMaker's built-in capabilities for model monitoring, providing a comprehensive solution for detecting and alerting on data quality issues in production. Here's a detailed justification: Option C leverages SageMaker Model Monitor: This SageMaker feature is specifically designed to detect data drift and other data quality issues in real-time. It allows you to continuously monitor the performance and characteristics of your model's input data and predictions. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model-monitor.html Integration with Amazon CloudWatch: SageMaker Model Monitor automatically captures metrics related to data quality, such as drift scores, feature statistics, and more. These metrics are then sent to Amazon CloudWatch. [https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model-monitor-cloudwatch.html] CloudWatch Alarms for Alerting: By integrating with CloudWatch, you can set up alarms based on these metrics. These alarms trigger when a specific threshold is crossed, indicating a data quality problem (e.g., significant drift in a feature's distribution). The alarm can then send notifications via Amazon SNS to the ML engineer or other relevant personnel. Why the other options are less suitable: Option A: Configuring CloudWatch directly without SageMaker Model Monitor requires you to manually instrument your application code to publish custom metrics. This approach is less scalable and more complex than using Model Monitor, which automates the process. This approach would also not give insights into data quality, as it is not directly assessing the input/output of the model. Option B: SageMaker Clarify is primarily used for bias detection and explainability before deployment. While it can be helpful for identifying potential issues, it doesn't provide continuous monitoring of data quality in production. The approach would also require additional jobs to evaluate the model outputs over time. Option D: SageMaker Data Wrangler is used for data preparation and feature engineering before training. It is not designed for continuous monitoring of data quality in production. It is typically a one-time or periodic data preparation process, and not a continuous service. In summary, using SageMaker Model Monitor, integrating with CloudWatch, and configuring alarms is the most effective and direct way to monitor data quality issues in a SageMaker endpoint and receive alerts, because it is the intended tool provided by AWS for monitoring model data quality in production.

questions.question: A company needs to use Amazon SageMaker to train a model on more than 300 GB of data. The training data is composed of files that are 200 MB in size. The data is stored in Amazon S3 Standard storage and feeds a dashboard tool. Which SageMaker training ingestion mechanism is the MOST cost-effective solution for this scenario?

options.A: Amazon Elastic File System (Amazon EFS) file system
options.B: Amazon FSx for Lustre file system
options.C: Amazon S3 in fast file mode while using S3 Express One Zone
options.D: Amazon S3 in fast file mode without using S3 Express One Zone

questions.explanation: The correct answer is D. Amazon S3 in fast file mode without using S3 Express One Zone. Here's why: Let's analyze each option and its suitability for this scenario: A. Amazon Elastic File System (Amazon EFS) file system: EFS is a network file system. While it allows multiple instances to access the same data, it's generally more expensive than S3 for large datasets and is not optimized for high-throughput data ingestion for training. Also, it will require copying the data from S3 to EFS which adds to cost and time. B. Amazon FSx for Lustre file system: FSx for Lustre is a high-performance file system designed for workloads that require extremely fast read and write speeds, like HPC. While suitable, it's the most expensive option and provides performance overkill for a scenario primarily focused on cost-effectiveness for SageMaker training with relatively large files but not extreme performance requirements. As with EFS, the data must be copied from S3 to FSx. C. Amazon S3 in fast file mode while using S3 Express One Zone: S3 Express One Zone provides extremely low latency and high performance, but this comes at a premium price. While beneficial for latency-sensitive applications, it's not the most cost-effective solution when the priority is processing a 300GB dataset for model training. D. Amazon S3 in fast file mode without using S3 Express One Zone: SageMaker's "fast file mode" utilizes parallel data transfer from S3 to the training instance's EBS volumes. This significantly accelerates the data ingestion process compared to the standard "File Mode" without the additional cost of S3 Express One Zone or managed file systems like EFS or FSx for Lustre. This parallelization improves throughput while still leveraging the cost-effectiveness and scalability of S3 Standard. Since the data consists of many small files (200MB), the parallel data loading is important. S3 Standard is already a very cost-effective option and the parallel data loading will make a big difference. Therefore, using S3 in fast file mode (without S3 Express One Zone) provides the best balance between cost and performance for ingesting a large dataset (300 GB) composed of moderate-sized files (200 MB) from S3 into SageMaker for training. Relevant Documentation: Amazon SageMaker Data Input/Output Configuration: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model-managed-spot-training-data-input.html Amazon S3: https://aws.amazon.com/s3/ Amazon EFS: https://aws.amazon.com/efs/ Amazon FSx for Lustre: https://aws.amazon.com/fsx/lustre/ Amazon S3 Express One Zone: https://aws.amazon.com/s3/storage-classes/express-one-zone/

questions.question: A company has an ML model that is deployed to an Amazon SageMaker endpoint for real-time inference. The company needs to deploy a new model. The company must compare the new model’s performance to the currently deployed model’s performance before shifting all traffic to the new model. Which solution will meet these requirements with the LEAST operational effort?

options.A: Deploy the new model to a separate endpoint. Manually split traffic between the two endpoints.
options.B: Deploy the new model to a separate endpoint. Use Amazon CloudFront to distribute traffic between the two endpoints.
options.C: Deploy the new model as a shadow variant on the same endpoint as the current model. Route a portion of live traffic to the shadow model for evaluation.
options.D: Use AWS Lambda functions with custom logic to route traffic between the current model and the new model.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is C: Deploy the new model as a shadow variant on the same endpoint as the current model. Route a portion of live traffic to the shadow model for evaluation. Here's why this solution is optimal and why the others are not: SageMaker endpoints with shadow variants are specifically designed for A/B testing and model performance comparison in a live environment with minimal operational overhead. Shadow variants allow you to deploy a new model to an existing endpoint without fully replacing the currently serving model. Real-world traffic can be mirrored to the shadow variant without impacting the existing model's performance or availability for the majority of users. This enables you to observe the new model's behavior and metrics using the same live data as the existing model, facilitating an apples-to-apples comparison. Option A (Deploying to a separate endpoint and manually splitting traffic) is highly manual and error-prone. It requires custom scripting and constant monitoring to adjust traffic distribution, leading to increased operational complexity. Option B (Deploying to a separate endpoint and using CloudFront) adds unnecessary complexity. CloudFront is primarily a CDN for caching static and dynamic content, not specifically designed for routing real-time inference traffic based on model performance needs. While technically feasible, it involves more configuration and management than necessary. Option D (Using Lambda functions with custom logic) introduces significant operational overhead. Lambda functions would need to be designed, deployed, and maintained to handle traffic routing based on model health and performance. This solution increases the complexity of the system and requires custom error handling. Shadow variants within SageMaker endpoints provide the most straightforward and efficient method for A/B testing and model performance comparison in a real-time inference setup. They leverage SageMaker's built- in capabilities for traffic mirroring and performance monitoring, resulting in the least operational effort. The other options involve manual intervention, additional infrastructure components, or custom coding, increasing complexity and management burden. For further research, refer to the official AWS documentation on SageMaker Endpoint Variants: Deploy multiple models to serve predictions - Amazon SageMaker Inference with Multiple Models and Variants - Amazon SageMaker

questions.question: A company runs an ML model on Amazon SageMaker. The company uses an automatic process that makes API calls to create training jobs for the model. The company has new compliance rules that prohibit the collection of aggregated metadata from training jobs. Which solution will prevent SageMaker from collecting metadata from the training jobs?

options.A: Opt out of metadata tracking for any training job that is submitted.
options.B: Ensure that training jobs are running in a private subnet in a custom VPC.
options.C: Encrypt the training data with an AWS Key Management Service (AWS KMS) customer managed key.
options.D: Reconfigure the training jobs to use only AWS Nitro instances.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is A: Opt out of metadata tracking for any training job that is submitted. SageMaker by default collects metadata from training jobs to help improve its services and provide insights to users. However, if a company has compliance rules prohibiting the collection of this aggregated metadata, the most direct and effective solution is to opt out of metadata tracking. AWS provides mechanisms to disable this feature. Option B (Running training jobs in a private subnet) doesn't prevent metadata collection. A private subnet restricts inbound and outbound traffic to resources within the VPC and prevents direct internet access but doesn't control the metadata that SageMaker itself sends to AWS services. Option C (Encrypting training data with KMS) is primarily a security measure that focuses on data protection and confidentiality. While encryption is crucial for compliance with many data privacy regulations, it doesn't directly address the collection of aggregated metadata by SageMaker. Option D (Using AWS Nitro instances) relates to the underlying infrastructure used for the training jobs. Nitro instances provide enhanced performance and security but do not directly impact the metadata collection practices of SageMaker. Therefore, opting out of metadata tracking is the only solution that directly addresses the compliance requirement of preventing SageMaker from collecting aggregated metadata from training jobs. This is typically done through API parameters or configurations within the SageMaker training job definition. For further research, consult the official AWS SageMaker documentation, specifically focusing on configurations related to privacy and data collection: AWS SageMaker Documentation - Focus on the "Configure a Training Job" section and search for privacy- related configurations or opt-out options. While the exact documentation detailing a global "opt-out" may not be explicitly present, understanding the configuration of Training Jobs will allow the administrator to discover settings that limit metadata aggregation. Look for API parameters related to logging and monitoring, which can indirectly reduce the metadata transmitted.

questions.question: A company is exploring generative AI and wants to add a new product feature. An ML engineer is making API calls from existing Amazon EC2 instances to Amazon Bedrock. The EC2 instances are in a private subnet and must remain private during the implementation. The EC2 instances have an assigned security group that allows access to all IP addresses in the private subnet. What should the ML engineer do to establish a connection between the EC2 instances and Amazon Bedrock?

options.A: Modify the security group to allow inbound and outbound traffic to and from Amazon Bedrock.
options.B: Use AWS PrivateLink to access Amazon Bedrock through an interface VPC endpoint.
options.C: Configure Amazon Bedrock to use the private subnet where the EC2 instances are deployed.
options.D: Link the existing VPC to Amazon Bedrock by using an AWS Direct Connect connection.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is B: Use AWS PrivateLink to access Amazon Bedrock through an interface VPC endpoint. Here's why: Private Subnet Requirement: The core constraint is that the EC2 instances must remain in a private subnet. This immediately rules out solutions that involve exposing them to the public internet. Internet Access Alternatives (Avoided): While NAT Gateways or NAT Instances could provide internet access from the private subnet, they are not ideal for secure, private communication within AWS. They also add cost and operational overhead. Security Group Limitations: Security Groups control traffic flow based on IP addresses, protocols, and ports. Modifying them (Option A) would not magically make Amazon Bedrock accessible without exposing the EC2 instances to the internet or other network configurations. Bedrock is a service accessible via API endpoints, not directly through IP address whitelisting. Amazon Bedrock Deployment Location (Not Possible): Amazon Bedrock (Option C) is a fully managed service. You cannot "deploy" it into your private subnet. It's a service offered by AWS that you access through APIs. AWS Direct Connect (Overkill): AWS Direct Connect (Option D) establishes a dedicated network connection between your on-premises environment and AWS. This is for hybrid scenarios and is unnecessary and overly complex for communication within the same AWS Region, especially when the goal is to keep EC2 instances private. AWS PrivateLink & Interface VPC Endpoints: AWS PrivateLink provides private connectivity between VPCs, AWS services, and on-premises networks, without exposing traffic to the public internet. By creating an interface VPC endpoint for Amazon Bedrock within the VPC where the EC2 instances reside, the EC2 instances can securely make API calls to Bedrock using private IP addresses. The traffic stays within the AWS network. The interface endpoint provides a private endpoint within your VPC, allowing the EC2 instances to connect to Bedrock as if it were within the same network. This method satisfies the requirement that EC2 instances remain private while allowing them to use Bedrock. In conclusion, using AWS PrivateLink and an interface VPC endpoint is the most secure and efficient way to connect EC2 instances in a private subnet to Amazon Bedrock. Supporting Documentation: AWS PrivateLink: https://aws.amazon.com/privatelink/ Interface VPC Endpoints (AWS PrivateLink): https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc- endpoints-interface.html Amazon Bedrock: https://aws.amazon.com/bedrock/

questions.question: An ecommerce company is using Amazon SageMaker Clarify Foundation Model Evaluations (FMEval) to evaluate ML models. Select the correct model evaluation task from the following list for each ecommerce use case. Each model evaluation task should be selected one time. •Classification evaluation •Open-ended generation •Question answering •Text summarization

answer.statement: Categorize customer reviews as positive, neutral, or negative sentiment.
Create concise product descriptions from complete manufacturer details.
Recommend products based on a user's browsing history.
Respond to specific customer queries about product features.

questions.explanation: 1) “Categorize customer reviews as positive, neutral, or negative sentiment.” → Classification evaluation (i.e., classification) Why correct: Sentiment labeling is a classic supervised classification problem: assign each review one label from a discrete set (positive / neutral / negative). “Classification evaluation” here means using a classification model and then evaluating it — it’s the only option that matches a label assignment task. Why the others don’t fit: Text summarization: produces shorter text, not a discrete sentiment label. Open-ended generation: generates free text, not a categorical label. Question answering: returns answers to direct questions, not a sentiment class. 2) “Create concise product descriptions from complete manufacturer details.” → Text summarization Why correct: This is a classic summarization / abstractive compression task: convert verbose product specs into a concise description. Text summarization models are designed to compress and rephrase input text into a shorter, coherent output. Why the others don’t fit: Classification returns labels, not a human-readable description. Open-ended generation could produce copy, but when you condense existing material into a concise description the standard approach is summarization (and the option explicitly labeled “Text summarization” matches best). Question answering expects question/answer pairs, not general summarization. 3) “Recommend products based on a user's browsing history.” → Open-ended generation (from the provided options) Why this selection is marked: Given the available options, “Open-ended generation” is the closest match because recommendation involves producing a list of suggested product items (a generated set of results) that aren’t a single label or a text summary. The prompt likely intends “generate recommendations” as an output, which is generative in nature. Caveat / better real-world mapping: In real ML systems, recommendations are usually produced by a recommendation algorithm (collaborative filtering, matrix factorization, factorization machines, or a ranking model), not a generic “open-ended generation” model. If the exam question restricts you to the four listed options, “Open-ended generation” is the best fit because the output is a list of items (generated suggestions). But strictly speaking, a recommender is a specialized ranking/retrieval model rather than a plain text generator. Why classification / summarization / QA don’t fit: Classification: would only give a label, not a ranked list of products. Summarization: compresses text — irrelevant. Question answering: answers factoid questions, not produce recommendation lists. 4) “Respond to specific customer queries about product features.” → Question answering Why correct: Question answering systems are designed to take a user question and return a precise answer drawn from product documentation or knowledge sources. This maps exactly to “respond to specific customer queries about product features.” Why the others don’t fit: Classification returns a label, not an answer. Open-ended generation could produce an answer, but QA systems are specialized to retrieve factual answers and to ground responses in documents. Text summarization condenses text, not directly answer user questions.

questions.question: A company wants to launch a new internal generative AI interface to answer user questions. The interface will be based on a popular open source large language model (LLM). Which combination of steps will deploy the interface with the LEAST operational overhead? (Choose two.)

options.A: Use Amazon SageMaker JumpStart to deploy the LLM.
options.B: Download the LLM as a .zip file. Deploy the LLM on a GPU-based Amazon EC2 instance.
options.C: Create a frontend HTML interface that uses an Amazon API Gateway WebSocket API with AWS Lambda functions to handle the user interaction.
options.D: Use Amazon QuickSight to create a UI to handle the user interaction.
options.E: Use Amazon Lex to create a UI to handle the user interaction.

questions.explanation: A. Use Amazon SageMaker JumpStart to deploy the LLM. JumpStart provides many pre-built open-source LLMs (LLaMA, Falcon, Flan-T5, etc.). No need to manage GPU servers manually. Automatic scaling and secure endpoints. C. Create a frontend HTML interface that uses Amazon API Gateway WebSocket API with AWS Lambda functions. Allows real-time conversational interaction. Lambda is fully managed → minimal overhead. Works smoothly with the SageMaker inference endpoint.

questions.question: A company wants to build a real-time analytics application that uses streaming data from social media. An ML engineer must implement a solution that ingests and transforms 5 GB of data each minute. The solution also must load the data into a data store that supports fast queries for the real-time analytics. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Use Amazon EventBridge to ingest the social media data. Use AWS Glue to transform the data. Store the transformed data in Amazon ElastiCache (Memcached).
options.B: Use Amazon Simple Queue Service (Amazon SQS) to ingest the social media data. Use AWS Lambda to transform the data. Store the transformed data in Amazon S3.
options.C: Use Amazon Simple Notification Service (Amazon SNS) to ingest the social media data. Use Amazon EMR to transform the data. Store the transformed data in Amazon RDS.
options.D: Use Amazon Kinesis Data Streams to ingest the social media data. Use Amazon Managed Service for Apache Flink to transform the data. Store the transformed data in Amazon DynamoDB.

questions.explanation: Use Amazon Kinesis Data Streams to ingest the social media data. Use Amazon Managed Service for Apache Flink to transform the data. Store the transformed data in Amazon DynamoDB. Amazon Kinesis Data Streams: Designed specifically for high-throughput ingestion of streaming data, such as social media feeds. It can handle gigabytes of data per second by scaling the number of shards. A volume of 5 GB per minute (~83 MB/s) is well within its capabilities, especially with the On-demand Advantage mode for automatic scaling. Amazon Managed Service for Apache Flink: This service (formerly Kinesis Data Analytics) is the industry standard for high-performance, low-latency transformation of streaming data. It integrates natively with Kinesis and can process gigabytes per second with sub-second latencies, making it ideal for the real-time transformation requirement. Amazon DynamoDB: A serverless NoSQL database that provides consistent, single-digit millisecond response times at any scale. It supports the fast queries required for real-time analytics dashboards and can handle the high write throughput generated by the Flink transformation.

questions.question: A company stores training data as a .csv file in an Amazon S3 bucket. The company must encrypt the data and must control which applications have access to the encryption key. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Create a new SSH access key. Use the AWS Encryption CLI with a reference to the new access key to encrypt the file.
options.B: Create a new API key by using the Amazon API Gateway CreateApiKey API operation. Use the AWS CLI with a reference to the new API key to encrypt the file.
options.C: Create a new IAM role. Attach a policy that allows the AWS Key Management Service (AWS KMS) GenerateDataKey action. Use the role to encrypt the file.
options.D: Create a new AWS Key Management Service (AWS KMS) key. Use the AWS Encryption CLI with a reference to the new KMS key to encrypt the file.

questions.explanation: The requirement is to encrypt data stored in an S3 bucket and control access to the encryption key. Option D, which involves creating an AWS KMS key and using the AWS Encryption CLI, is the most appropriate solution because it directly addresses both requirements. AWS KMS is a managed service that allows you to easily create and control the encryption keys used to encrypt your data. It provides centralized key management and cryptographic services, ensuring secure storage and controlled access. The AWS Encryption CLI is a command-line tool that simplifies the process of encrypting and decrypting data using KMS keys. By creating a new KMS key, the company maintains full control over the key's usage and permissions. They can explicitly grant access to specific applications or IAM roles that need to encrypt or decrypt the data. This ensures that only authorized entities can access the key and, consequently, the data. Options A and B are incorrect because SSH access keys and API keys are not designed for data encryption. SSH keys are for secure shell access to instances, and API keys are for authenticating API requests. Using them for encryption would be a misuse of their intended purpose and would not provide the level of security and control required. Option C is insufficient on its own. While granting the GenerateDataKey action to an IAM role allows the role to generate data keys using KMS, it doesn't directly encrypt the file or provide a straightforward method for doing so. The AWS Encryption CLI (used with a KMS key as in option D) provides the direct mechanism for encryption. Additionally, generating a data key requires an additional step of encrypting data with the data key and then encrypting the data key itself with the KMS key, which adds complexity unnecessarily compared to using the AWS Encryption CLI. Option D is the most direct and secure solution. Therefore, Option D provides the best balance between security, control, and ease of implementation. Relevant links for further research: AWS Key Management Service (KMS): https://aws.amazon.com/kms/ AWS Encryption CLI: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/encryption-sdk/latest/developer-guide/getting- started.html Protecting Data Using Server-Side Encryption with Amazon S3-Managed Keys (SSE-S3): https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/UsingEncryption.html Protecting Data Using Server-Side Encryption with AWS KMS-Managed Keys (SSE-KMS): https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/UsingKMSEncryption.html

questions.question: A company needs to perform feature engineering, aggregation, and data preparation. After the features are produced, the company must implement a solution on AWS to process and store the features. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Use Amazon SageMaker Feature Processing to process and ingest the data. Use SageMaker Feature Store to manage and store the features.
options.B: Use Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor to automatically ingest and transform the data. Create an Amazon S3 bucket to store the features in JSON format.
options.C: Use Amazon Managed Service for Apache Flink to transform the data and to ingest the data directly into Amazon SageMaker Feature Store. Use Feature Store to manage and store the features.
options.D: Use an Amazon SageMaker batch transform job to analyze, transform, and ingest the data. Create an Amazon DynamoDB table to store the features.

questions.explanation: Option A is the most suitable solution for feature engineering, aggregation, data preparation, processing, storage, and management within AWS, according to the requirements. Amazon SageMaker Feature Processing provides a scalable and managed environment specifically designed for data transformation and feature engineering tasks. This service simplifies the process of cleaning, normalizing, and transforming raw data into features suitable for machine learning models. It's directly built to work within the SageMaker ecosystem, facilitating seamless integration with other components. SageMaker Feature Store acts as a centralized repository for storing, managing, and serving machine learning features. It ensures that features are consistently available for both training and inference, addressing a critical need for organizations needing organized and retrievable feature data. This allows for easy retrieval of features for training or real-time inference. Options B, C, and D have limitations. Option B's suggestion of using SageMaker Model Monitor is incorrect because Model Monitor is used to detect deviations in model performance, not for data transformation. Using Amazon S3 for storing features directly lacks crucial management capabilities like versioning, metadata management, and online/offline feature serving. Option C's use of Amazon Managed Service for Apache Flink is suitable for stream processing, but it's overly complex for simple feature engineering and doesn't provide the dedicated feature management capabilities of Feature Store. Option D's use of batch transform jobs is a valid option for processing but lacks the managed storage of features. DynamoDB, while powerful, is not designed for the analytical workloads commonly associated with machine learning feature stores. Feature Store's purpose-built design provides benefits not available in general-purpose storage solutions like S3 or DynamoDB. In summary, the combination of SageMaker Feature Processing and SageMaker Feature Store provides a well-integrated, scalable, and manageable solution for the entire feature engineering and management lifecycle on AWS.Amazon SageMaker Feature Store DocumentationAmazon SageMaker Feature Processing Documentation

questions.question: A company is developing a new online application to gather information from customers. An ML engineer has developed a new ML model that will determine a score for each customer. The model will use the score to determine which product to display to the customer. The ML engineer needs to minimize response-time latency for the model. How should the ML engineer deploy the application in Amazon SageMaker to meet these requirements?

options.A: Configure batch transform.
options.B: Configure a real-time inference endpoint.
options.C: Configure a serverless inference endpoint.
options.D: Configure an asynchronous inference endpoint.

questions.explanation: The requirement is to minimize response-time latency for a machine learning model that scores customers and determines which product to display. This necessitates immediate results to provide a personalized experience. Therefore, a real-time inference endpoint is the most suitable solution. Option A, batch transform, is designed for processing large datasets offline or at scheduled intervals. It's unsuitable because it doesn't provide immediate predictions. Customers need to see product recommendations in real-time as they interact with the application. Option C, serverless inference endpoints (using SageMaker Serverless Inference), scales compute resources automatically based on traffic. While it can handle unpredictable traffic, it may still introduce cold start latency, especially when there's no recent traffic, making it less ideal for applications demanding consistently low latency. Option D, asynchronous inference endpoints are appropriate for requests that can tolerate some delay, where immediate responses aren't crucial. It stores requests and processes them later. This wouldn't work because the application needs the score instantly to personalize product recommendations. Real-time inference endpoints, also known as SageMaker Hosting Services, are explicitly designed for low- latency, high-throughput predictions. They deploy the model behind a REST endpoint that can be queried on demand. The model is continuously available, ensuring minimal latency for each prediction request. This approach is best for applications where immediate responses are crucial for a good user experience. By using a dedicated endpoint, the ML engineer can optimize it for the model, ensuring a good tradeoff between cost and latency. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/deploy- model.htmlhttps://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/realtime- endpoints.htmlhttps://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/serverless- endpoints.htmlhttps://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/async-inference.html

questions.question: A company is using Amazon EMR. The company has a large dataset in Amazon S3 that needs to be ingested into Amazon SageMaker Feature Store. The dataset contains historical data and real-time streaming data. The company must ensure that the Feature Store online store is updated with the most recent data as soon as the data becomes available. The company also must maintain a complete Feature Store offline store for batch processing. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Use the PutRecord API in Feature Store Runtime to ingest all the data into the online store.
options.B: Use the PutRecord API in Feature Store Runtime to ingest all the data into the offline store.
options.C: Use the Feature Store Spark connector to ingest the data as Spark DataFrames with the online store and offline store enabled.
options.D: Use the Feature Store Spark connector to ingest the data as Spark DataFrames with only the online store enabled.

questions.explanation: Here's a detailed justification for why option C is the correct solution, incorporating relevant cloud computing concepts and links for further research: The core requirement is to ingest both historical (batch) and real-time streaming data into Amazon SageMaker Feature Store, ensuring the online store reflects the most recent data while maintaining a complete offline store. Option A is incorrect because the PutRecord API is primarily designed for writing individual records to the online store. It's not suitable for bulk loading historical data for the offline store. Manually inserting millions/billions of historical records with PutRecord would be extremely inefficient and costly. Option B is incorrect because the PutRecord API doesn't directly support writing to the offline store. The offline store is typically populated through batch processing jobs. Also, this option fails to address the need to keep the online store updated with streaming data. Option D is incorrect because it only focuses on the online store and neglects the requirement of maintaining a complete offline store for batch processing. The offline store is crucial for model training, backtesting, and other analytical workloads. Option C, using the Feature Store Spark connector with both online and offline stores enabled, is the most appropriate solution. Here's why: Spark Connector: The SageMaker Feature Store Spark connector allows you to ingest data into Feature Store using Spark DataFrames. Spark is a powerful distributed data processing engine, well-suited for handling large datasets in parallel. This allows efficient ingestion of both historical and streaming data. Online and Offline Store Support: The Spark connector can be configured to simultaneously write data to both the online and offline stores. This addresses the requirements for real-time data in the online store and complete historical data in the offline store. Scalability and Performance: Spark's distributed processing capabilities allow you to scale your data ingestion pipeline to handle large datasets efficiently. The Spark connector can leverage EMR's compute resources to parallelize the data ingestion process. Simplified Data Engineering: The Spark connector simplifies the data engineering process by providing a high-level API for writing data to Feature Store. This reduces the need for complex data transformation and loading logic. Real-time Updates: While the initial load might be a batch process, Spark Streaming or Structured Streaming (integrated into Spark) can be used to consume real-time data streams from sources like Apache Kafka and write them continuously to the online store through the Spark connector, ensuring the most recent data is always available. The Spark connector handles the necessary data formatting and API calls to Feature Store. In summary, option C leverages the power of Spark for efficient and scalable data ingestion, and it satisfies both the real-time online store update requirement and the complete offline store requirement. The Spark connector simplifies the process and enables a robust and maintainable solution. Authoritative Links: Amazon SageMaker Feature Store: https://aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/feature-store/ SageMaker Feature Store Spark Connector: https://sagemaker-spark- sdk.readthedocs.io/en/latest/feature_store.html Amazon EMR: https://aws.amazon.com/emr/ Apache Spark: https://spark.apache.org/ Spark Structured Streaming: https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/structured-streaming-programming- guide.html

questions.question: An ML engineer needs to deploy four ML models in an Amazon SageMaker inference pipeline. The models were built with different frameworks. The ML engineer also needs to give clients the ability to use the invoke_endpoint call to perform inference for each model. Which solution will meet these requirements MOST cost-effectively?

options.A: Create a SageMaker multi-model endpoint.
options.B: Create a SageMaker multi-container endpoint.
options.C: Create multiple SageMaker single-model endpoints.
options.D: Run a SparkML job to generate multiple endpoints.

questions.explanation: Here's a detailed justification for why option B (Create a SageMaker multi-container endpoint) is the most cost-effective solution for deploying multiple ML models built with different frameworks in an inference pipeline, allowing clients to selectively invoke each model through invoke_endpoint calls: A multi-container endpoint leverages SageMaker's capabilities to host multiple Docker containers, each running a model built using a different framework (e.g., TensorFlow, PyTorch, Scikit-learn) under a single endpoint. This architecture reduces infrastructure overhead compared to deploying individual single-model endpoints for each model. With multi-container endpoints, all models share the same underlying compute resources (EC2 instance), optimizing resource utilization and minimizing costs. The invoke_endpoint call can then route requests to the appropriate container based on a specified target model. This eliminates the need to provision and manage separate instances for each model, which would be required with single-model endpoints (option C). Multi-model endpoints (option A) are designed for a scenario where a single model server loads and unloads models dynamically based on inference requests. This is suitable for large numbers of models sharing the same framework but less suitable for models built with entirely different frameworks intended to operate simultaneously in a pipeline. Running a SparkML job (option D) is not directly relevant to inference deployment. SparkML is primarily used for large-scale data processing and model training, not for real-time inference. Therefore, using Spark to create endpoints is not efficient. Because the models need to work as an inference pipeline and be available through invoke endpoint, Multi-container endpoints allow defining a sequence of models through the containers. This supports the construction of complex inference pipelines. The solution achieves high utilization of the compute instance, leading to significant cost savings. Authoritative links for further research: Amazon SageMaker Multi-Container Endpoints: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/deploy- multi-container-endpoints.html Amazon SageMaker Inference Pipelines: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/inference- pipelines.html Amazon SageMaker Multi-Model Endpoints: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/multi- model-endpoints.html

questions.question: An ML engineer wants an Amazon SageMaker notebook to automatically stop running after 1 hour of idle time. How can the ML engineer accomplish this goal?

options.A: Create a lifecycle configuration in SageMaker. Copy the auto-stop-idle script from GitHub to the Start Notebook section.
options.B: Create a lifecycle configuration in SageMaker. Copy the auto-stop-idle script from GitHub to the Create Notebook section.
options.C: Track the notebook's CPU metric by using Amazon CloudWatch Logs. Invoke an AWS Lambda function from CloudWatch Logs to shut down the notebook instance if CPU utilization becomes zero.
options.D: Track the notebook's memory metric by using Amazon CloudWatch Logs. Invoke an AWS Lambda function from CloudWatch Logs to shut down the notebook instance if memory utilization becomes zero.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is A because SageMaker Lifecycle Configurations are designed to automate notebook instance customization and management. Lifecycle configurations provide shell scripts that run automatically when a notebook instance is created (Create Notebook) or started (Start Notebook). The auto-stop-idle script, readily available on GitHub from AWS, is specifically crafted to monitor notebook idle time and automatically shut down the instance after a specified duration, conserving resources and costs. By placing the script in the Start Notebook section, the script will execute every time the notebook is started or restarted, ensuring consistent idle-time monitoring. Option B is incorrect because putting the script in the Create Notebook section only executes the script when the notebook instance is initially created. It wouldn't continuously monitor idle time after each start or restart, defeating the purpose of automatically stopping the notebook after each idle period. Option C and D are less efficient and more complex. While CloudWatch Logs and Lambda can be used to monitor metrics and take actions, they require configuring custom CloudWatch metric filters, Lambda functions, and IAM roles for the notebook instance to allow CloudWatch Logs to access notebook metrics and to allow CloudWatch to invoke Lambda. This approach involves significantly more setup and maintenance compared to the straightforward Lifecycle Configuration solution. Also, tracking CPU or memory utilization alone might not be an accurate indicator of idle time. A notebook could have low CPU or memory utilization while actively performing background tasks, leading to premature shutdowns. Lifecycle Configurations offer a simple, managed, and more appropriate method to accomplish the automatic stop-idle functionality directly within SageMaker's capabilities. The AWS-provided script within a lifecycle configuration directly monitors activity and stops the instance based on actual idle time, making it a more reliable solution. For further research, refer to the official AWS documentation on SageMaker Lifecycle Configurations: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/notebook-lifecycle-config.htmland the example auto- stop-idle notebook configuration: https://github.com/aws-samples/amazon-sagemaker-notebook-instance- lifecycle-config-samples/tree/master/scripts.

questions.question: A company wants to provide services to help other businesses label images. The company wants its labeling specialists to complete human labeling tasks on AWS. How should the company register the labeling specialists to receive tasks on AWS?

options.A: Use AWS Data Exchange.
options.B: Create and use an internal workforce in Amazon SageMaker Ground Truth.
options.C: Create and use Amazon Mechanical Turk entities in an Amazon SageMaker human loop.
options.D: Use the Amazon Mechanical Turk website.

questions.explanation: B. Create and use an internal workforce in Amazon SageMaker Ground Truth. Amazon SageMaker Ground Truth: This is the primary AWS service for managing data labeling workflows. It allows you to create labeling jobs for various data types, including images, video, and text. Internal Workforce: This feature allows a company to create its own team of labeling specialists (employees or contractors). Specialists are managed through an Amazon Cognito user pool or an OIDC identity provider.

questions.question: A company wants to use Amazon SageMaker to host an ML model that runs on CPU for real-time predictions. The model will have intermittent traffic during business hours and will have periods of no traffic after business hours. The company needs a solution that will serve inference requests in the most cost-effective manner. Which hosting option will meet these requirements?

options.A: Deploy the model to a SageMaker real-time endpoint. Add a schedule-based auto scaling policy to handle traffic surges during business hours.
options.B: Deploy the model to a SageMaker Serverless Inference endpoint. Configure increased provisioned concurrency during business hours.
options.C: Deploy the model to a SageMaker Asynchronous Inference endpoint. Configure an auto scaling policy that scales in to zero outside business hours.
options.D: Deploy the model to a SageMaker real-time endpoint. Create a scheduled AWS Lambda function that activates the endpoint during business hours only.

questions.explanation: B. Deploy the model to a SageMaker Serverless Inference endpoint. Configure increased provisioned concurrency during business hours. Traffic has predictable surges (e.g., during business hours). The company wants to avoid paying for compute when traffic is low (e.g., outside business hours). The solution should require low operational overhead.

questions.question: An ML engineer needs to train a supervised deep learning model. The available dataset is a large number of unlabeled images that only employees should access. The ML engineer needs to implement a solution that labels the dataset with the highest possible accuracy. Which combination of steps should the ML engineer take to meet these requirements? (Choose two.)

options.A: Use Amazon Rekognition to automatically label the dataset.
options.B: Train the deep learning model directly on the raw data. Let the model infer the labels by itself.
options.C: Use Amazon SageMaker Ground Truth to create an annotation job that specifies the labeling task and requirements.
options.D: Set up workforce teams to access a private workforce to run and review the annotation job created by Amazon SageMaker Ground Truth.
options.E: Use Amazon Mechanical Turk to complete the annotation job created by Amazon SageMaker Ground Truth.

questions.explanation: C. Use Amazon SageMaker Ground Truth to create an annotation job . Ground Truth allows you to: Set up a formal labeling workflow for image classification/detection/segmentation. Define labeling rules, instructions, validations. Manage large-scale labeling with quality controls. D. Set up workforce teams to access a private workforce . Ground Truth supports three workforce types: Private workforce (employees) Vendor workforce Mechanical Turk Since only employees should access the images: The private workforce is mandatory. Allows internal reviewers, secure access, and high-quality labeling.

questions.question: A company is using an Amazon S3 bucket to collect data that will be used for ML workflows. The company needs to use AWS Glue DataBrew to clean and normalize the data. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Create a DataBrew dataset by using the S3 path. Clean and normalize the data by using a DataBrew profile job.
options.B: Create a DataBrew dataset by using the S3 path. Clean and normalize the data by using a DataBrew recipe job.
options.C: Create a DataBrew dataset by using a Java Database Connectivity (JDBC) driver to connect to the S3 bucket. Clean and normalize the data by using a DataBrew profile job.
options.D: Create a DataBrew dataset by using a Java Database Connectivity (JDBC) driver to connect to the S3 bucket. Clean and normalize the data by using a DataBrew recipe job.

questions.explanation: Create a DataBrew dataset by using the S3 path. Clean and normalize the data by using a DataBrew recipe job.

questions.question: A company is developing a new ML model that uses the XGBoost algorithm. The company will train the model on data that is stored in an Amazon S3 bucket. The data is in a nested JSON format. An ML engineer needs to convert the JSON files into a tabular format. Which solution will meet this requirement with the LEAST operational overhead?

options.A: Create an AWS Glue PySpark job that uses the Relationalize transform to convert the files.
options.B: Write custom Scala code to convert the files. Use Amazon EMR Serverless to run the Scala code.
options.C: Create an AWS Lambda function that uses a Python runtime and invokes the reduce() function to convert the files. Invoke the Lambda function.
options.D: Create an Amazon Athena database that is based on the JSON files. Use the Athena flatten function to convert the data.

questions.explanation: A. Create an AWS Glue PySpark job that uses the Relationalize transform to convert the files. The data is in nested JSON format and needs to be converted into a tabular structure for XGBoost training. The objective is to do this with the least operational overhead.

questions.question: A medical company ingests streams of data from devices that monitor patients’ vital signs. The company uses Amazon SageMaker and plans to prepare ML models to predict adverse events for patients. The dataset is large with thousands of features. An ML engineer needs to run several hundred training iterations with different sets of features, different algorithms, and many potential parameters. The ML engineer must implement a solution to log the characteristics and results of each training iteration. Which solution will meet these requirements with the LEAST implementation effort?

options.A: Use Amazon CloudWatch to create custom metrics for the characteristics of each iteration.
options.B: Write the characteristics of each iteration to logs in Amazon S3. Use AWS Glue and Amazon Athena to search the logs.
options.C: Use the SageMaker Model Registry to track the characteristics and results of each iteration.
options.D: Use SageMaker Experiments to track the characteristics and results of each iteration.

questions.explanation: D. Use SageMaker Experiments to track the characteristics and results of each iteration. Run many training iterations Vary features, algorithms, and hyperparameters Log both characteristics and results for comparison Minimize implementation effort SageMaker Experiments is specifically designed for this use case. It automatically organizes and tracks: Training configurations Hyperparameters Metrics and evaluation results Artifacts (models, logs) And allows easy comparison across hundreds of runs.

questions.question: A company is planning to create an internal-only chat interface to help employees handle customer queries. Currently, the employees need to refer to a massive knowledge base of internal documents to address customer issues. The new solution must be serverless. Which combination of steps will meet these requirements?

options.A: Set up Amazon Bedrock with the Anthropic Claude foundation model.
options.B: Set up Amazon SageMaker JumpStart with the Llama foundation model.
options.C: Use Amazon EC2 instances with Amazon API Gateway to invoke the model API.
options.D: Use AWS Lambda functions with Amazon API Gateway to invoke the model API.
options.E: Use an Amazon S3 bucket to store vector database dumps and embeddings. F. Use Amazon RDS for MySQL to store vector database dumps and embeddings.

questions.explanation: A. Set up Amazon Bedrock with the Anthropic Claude foundation model. Bedrock provides fully managed foundation models without needing model hosting or infrastructure. This meets the goal of minimal overhead. D. AWS Lambda + API Gateway A serverless approach to invoke the Bedrock model → meets low overhead requirement. E. Amazon S3 for embeddings/vector store dumps Storing vector embeddings in S3 is cost-effective and serverless. Suitable if using external vector DB or Bedrock Knowledge Base pipelines.

questions.question: An ML engineer needs to deploy a trained model that is based on a genetic algorithm. The algorithm solves a complex problem and can take several minutes to generate predictions. When the model is deployed, the model needs to access large amounts of data to process requests. The requests can involve as much as 100 MB of data. Which deployment solution will meet these requirements with the LEAST operational overhead?

options.A: Deploy the model to Amazon EC2 instances in an Auto Scaling group behind an Application Load Balancer.
options.B: Deploy the model to an Amazon SageMaker real-time endpoint.
options.C: Deploy the model to an Amazon SageMaker Asynchronous Inference endpoint.
options.D: Package the model as a container. Deploy the model to Amazon Elastic Container Service (Amazon ECS) on Amazon EC2 instances.

questions.explanation: C. Deploy the model to an Amazon SageMaker Asynchronous Inference endpoint. The model has two key characteristics: Inference takes several minutes This means the inference is long-running. Real-time endpoints are designed for low-latency requests and will timeout for multi-minute executions. Requests may carry up to 100 MB of input data Real-time SageMaker endpoints limit payload size (typically up to 6–10 MB). SageMaker Asynchronous Inference supports much larger payload sizes and stores input/output in Amazon S3. SageMaker Asynchronous Inference is specifically built for: Long-running inference workloads Large payload processing Fully managed scaling & low operational overhead

questions.question: An ML engineer wants to use a set of survey responses as training data for an ML classifier. All the survey responses are either “yes” or “no.” The ML engineer needs to convert the responses into a feature that will produce better model training results. The ML engineer must not increase the dimensionality of the dataset. Which methods will meet these requirements? (Choose two.)

options.A: Binary encoding
options.B: Label encoding
options.C: One-hot encoding
options.D: Statistical imputation

questions.explanation: A. Binary EncodingHow it works: This method first assigns an integer to each category, and then converts the integer into its corresponding binary code. Each bit of the binary code is then represented as a separate feature column.Dimensionality: While it does increase the dimensionality compared to the original single categorical column, the number of new columns is equal to $\log_2(N)$, where $N$ is the number of unique categories. This is a logarithmic increase, making it much smaller than the linear increase of one-hot encoding, and thus it's often acceptable when trying to constrain dimensionality. For example, 32 categories require only 5 new columns ($\log_2(32) = 5$). B. Label EncodingHow it works: This is the simplest method, where each unique categorical response is assigned a unique integer value. For example, 'Red' $\rightarrow 1$, 'Green' $\rightarrow 2$, 'Blue' $\rightarrow 3$.Dimensionality: The number of features does not increase; one categorical column is replaced by one numerical column. This strictly meets the requirement to not increase dimensionality. This method is best used for ordinal data (where the order of categories matters).

questions.question: An ML engineer must choose the appropriate Amazon SageMaker algorithm to solve specific AI problems. Select the correct SageMaker built-in algorithm from the following list for each use case. Each algorithm should be selected one time. •Random Cut Forest (RCF) algorithm •Semantic segmentation algorithm •Sequence-to-Sequence (seq2seq) algorithm
questions.explanation: Sequence-to-Sequence (seq2seq) algorithm. Seq2Seq models are designed for natural language understanding and generation. They convert one sequence of text into another, making them ideal for: Text summarization Language translation Chatbots and conversational AI Semantic Segmentation algorithm. Semantic segmentation performs pixel-level image classification, assigning a label to every pixel in an image. This allows: Object detection in self-driving cars Medical imaging analysis Scene understanding. Random Cut Forest (RCF) algorithm. Random Cut Forest (RCF) is used for: Anomaly detection Detecting unusual behavior in time-series or tabular data Example use cases: Fraud detection Fault detection Outlier detection in sensor data

questions.question: A company is planning to use an Amazon SageMaker prebuilt algorithm to create a recommendation model. The algorithm must be able to make predictions on high-dimensional sparse data. Which SageMaker algorithm should the company choose for the recommendation model?

options.A: K-nearest neighbors (k-NN)
options.B: Factorization Machines
options.C: Principal component analysis (PCA)
options.D: Sequence-to-Sequence (seq2seq)

questions.explanation: B. Factorization Machines. Designed specifically for sparse data (such as one-hot encoded user and item IDs). Works well for collaborative filtering and recommendation tasks. Efficient in high-dimensional feature spaces.

questions.question: A company has several teams that have developed separate prediction models on their own laptops. The teams developed the models by using Python with scikit-learn and TensorFlow frameworks. The company must rebuild the models and must integrate the models into an ML infrastructure that the company manages by using Amazon SageMaker. The company also must incorporate the models into a model registry. Which solution will meet these requirements with the LEAST operational overhead?

options.A: Export the models from the laptops to an Amazon S3 bucket. Use an Amazon API Gateway REST API and AWS Lambda functions with SageMaker endpoints to access the models. Register the models in the SageMaker Model Registry.
options.B: Import the models into the SageMaker Model Registry. Use SageMaker to run the imported models.
options.C: Use code from the laptops to create containers for the models. Use the bring your own container (BYOC) functionality of SageMaker to import and use the models. Register the models in the SageMaker Model Registry.
options.D: Import the Python-based models into SageMaker. Rebuild the scikit-learn and TensorFlow models in SageMaker. Register all the models in the SageMaker Model Registry.

questions.explanation: D. Import the Python-based models into SageMaker. Rebuild the scikit-learn and TensorFlow models in SageMaker. Register all the models in the SageMaker Model Registry. Rebuilds the models directly in SageMaker using built-in frameworks (like TensorFlow / scikit-learn). Registering them in SageMaker Model Registry fits seamlessly with managed ML workflow. This is lowest operational overhead and meets all requirements.

questions.question: A company is training a large language model (LLM) by using on-premises infrastructure. A live conversational engine uses the LLM to help customers find real-time insights in credit card data. An ML engineer must implement a solution to train and deploy the LLM on Amazon SageMaker. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Use SageMaker Training Compiler to train the LLM. Deploy the LLM by using SageMaker real-time inference.
options.B: Use SageMaker with deep learning containers for large model inference to train the LLM. Deploy the LLM by using SageMaker real-time inference.
options.C: Use SageMaker Notebook Jobs to train the LLM. Deploy the LLM by using SageMaker Asynchronous Inference.
options.D: Use SageMaker Studio to train the LLM. Deploy the LLM by using SageMaker batch transform.

questions.explanation: A. Use SageMaker Training Compiler to train the LLM. Deploy the LLM by using SageMaker real-time inference. This accelerates large-model training and supports conversational real-time deployment. SageMaker Training Compiler: This tool optimizes deep learning models to accelerate training by up to 50% through infrastructure-aware compilation. For an LLM, which typically requires significant compute resources, this compiler reduces both the time and cost of training on SageMaker. Real-time Inference: A live conversational engine requires sub-second or millisecond latency to provide immediate responses to users. SageMaker real-time inference is specifically designed for these low-latency, interactive use cases

questions.question: A company has an existing Amazon SageMaker model (v1) on a production endpoint. The company develops a new model version (v2) and needs to test v2 in production before substituting v2 for v1. The company needs to implement a solution to minimize the risk of v2 generating incorrect output in production. The solution must prevent any disruption of production traffic during the change to v2. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Create a second production variant for v2. Assign 1% of the traffic to v2 and 99% of the traffic to v1. Collect all the output of v2 in an Amazon S3 bucket. If v2 performs as expected, switch all the traffic to v2.
options.B: Create a second production variant for v2. Assign 10% of the traffic to v2 and 90% of the traffic to v1. Collect all the output of v2 in an Amazon S3 bucket. If v2 performs as expected, switch all the traffic to v2.
options.C: Deploy v2 to a new endpoint. Turn on data capturing for the production endpoint. Write a script to pass 100% of input data to v2. If v2 performs as expected, deactivate the v1 endpoint and direct the traffic to v2.
options.D: Deploy v2 into a shadow variant that samples 100% of the inference requests. Collect all the output in an Amazon S3 bucket. If v2 performs as expected, promote v2 to production.

questions.explanation: D. Deploy v2 into a shadow variant that samples 100% of the inference requests. Collect all the output in an Amazon S3 bucket. If v2 performs as expected, promote v2 to production.
