Authoritative Links: Amazon SageMaker Model Registry: https://aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/model-registry/ Amazon SageMaker Inference Recommender: https://aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/inference-recommender/ SageMaker Model Registry Documentation: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model- registry.html SageMaker Inference Recommender Documentation: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/inference-recommender.html

questions.question: A company is using Amazon SageMaker AI to develop a credit risk assessment model. During model validation, the company finds that the model achieves 82% accuracy on the validation data. However, the model achieved 99% accuracy on the training data. The company needs to address the model accuracy issue before deployment. Which solution will meet this requirement?

options.A: Add more dense layers to increase model complexity. Implement batch normalization. Use early stopping during training.
options.B: Implement dropout layers. Use L1 or L2 regularization. Perform k-fold cross-validation.
options.C: Use principal component analysis (PCA) to reduce the feature dimensionality. Decrease model layers. Implement cross-entropy loss functions.
options.D: Augment the training dataset. Remove duplicate records from the training dataset. Implement stratified sampling.

questions.explanation: The scenario clearly indicates a classic case of overfitting, where the model performs exceptionally well on the training data (99% accuracy) but poorly on unseen validation data (82% accuracy). The goal is to improve the model's generalization ability and reduce this discrepancy. Option B provides a highly effective and comprehensive set of strategies to address overfitting: 1. Implement dropout layers: Dropout is a powerful regularization technique primarily used in neural networks. During training, it randomly sets a fraction of neuron activations to zero at each update step. This prevents neurons from co-adapting too much, forcing the network to learn more robust and independent features, thereby significantly reducing overfitting and improving generalization. 2. Use L1 or L2 regularization: These techniques add a penalty term to the loss function, discouraging large weight values. L1 regularization (Lasso) encourages sparsity by pushing some weights to exactly zero, effectively performing feature selection. L2 regularization (Ridge) shrinks all weights towards zero. Both methods reduce model complexity by penalizing excessive reliance on specific features or complex relationships, leading to a simpler model that generalizes better. 3. Perform k-fold cross-validation: While not a direct regularization technique, k-fold cross-validation is crucial for robust model evaluation and hyperparameter tuning. It involves splitting the dataset into k folds, training the model k times (each time using a different fold for validation), and averaging the results. This provides a more reliable estimate of the model's true performance on unseen data, helps detect overfitting patterns, and guides the selection of optimal hyperparameters (like dropout rate or regularization strength) that promote better generalization. In contrast, other options are less suitable or even counterproductive: Option A's suggestion to "add more dense layers to increase model complexity" would likely exacerbate overfitting. Option C's "implement cross-entropy loss functions" is about choosing an appropriate loss function for classification, not a direct solution for overfitting, although "decrease model layers" and "PCA" are good steps. Option D's "remove duplicate records" is good data hygiene but less impactful than regularization for severe overfitting, and "stratified sampling" primarily ensures balanced evaluation rather than preventing overfitting during training. Therefore, the combination of dropout, L1/L2 regularization, and k-fold cross-validation in Option B offers the most direct and effective approach to combat overfitting and improve the model's generalization capability before deployment. Authoritative Links for further research: Dropout: https://www.cs.toronto.edu/~hinton/absps/JMLRdropout.pdf (Original paper by Hinton et al.) L1/L2 Regularization: https://web.stanford.edu/~hastie/Papers/ESLII.pdf (Chapter 3, "The Elements of Statistical Learning" by Hastie, Tibshirani, Friedman) K-fold Cross-Validation: https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/cross_validation.html (Scikit-learn documentation)

questions.question: A company collects customer data every day. The company stores the data as compressed files in an Amazon S3 bucket that is partitioned by date. Every month, analysts download the data, process the data to check the data quality, and then upload the data to Amazon QuickSight dashboards. An ML engineer needs to implement a solution to automatically check the data quality before the data is sent to QuickSight. Which solution will meet these requirements with the LEAST operational overhead?

options.A: Run an AWS Glue crawler every month to update the AWS Glue Data Catalog. Use AWS Glue Data Quality rules to check the data quality.
options.B: Use an AWS Glue trigger to run an AWS Glue crawler every month to update the AWS Glue Data Catalog. Create an AWS Glue job that loads the data into a PySpark DataFrame. Configure the job to apply custom functions and to evaluate the data quality.
options.C: Run Python scripts on an AWS Lambda function every month to evaluate data quality. Configure the S3 bucket to invoke the Lambda function when objects are added to the S3 bucket.
options.D: Configure the S3 bucket to send event notifications to an Amazon Simple Queue Service (Amazon SQS) queue when objects are uploaded. Use Amazon CloudWatch insights every month for the SQS queue to evaluate the data quality.

questions.explanation: Option A is the most suitable solution with the LEAST operational overhead because it leverages native AWS Glue services specifically designed for data discovery, cataloging, and quality assessment. First, the requirement is to automatically check data quality on compressed files stored in Amazon S3, partitioned by date, on a monthly basis. Option A starts by running an AWS Glue crawler. An AWS Glue crawler is a serverless service that automatically discovers the schema, data types, and partitions of data stored in S3, updating the AWS Glue Data Catalog. This eliminates the manual effort of defining schemas for varied and evolving datasets. Second, the core of the data quality check is addressed by AWS Glue Data Quality rules. AWS Glue Data Quality is a managed feature that allows users to define, evaluate, and monitor data quality directly on tables in the AWS Glue Data Catalog or within AWS Glue ETL jobs. These rules can check for various data quality dimensions like completeness, accuracy, consistency, validity, and uniqueness using a declarative language, removing the need to write complex custom code for standard checks. The combination of Glue crawlers and Glue Data Quality significantly minimizes operational overhead. Both are fully managed services, meaning AWS handles the underlying infrastructure, scaling, and maintenance. Defining rules is a configuration task rather than a development effort, reducing the need for engineers to write, test, and maintain custom Python/PySpark scripts as in Option B. The monthly execution can be easily scheduled using AWS Glue workflows or Amazon CloudWatch Events. In contrast, Option B, while using Glue, introduces significant overhead by requiring an AWS Glue job with custom PySpark functions. This demands more development, testing, and ongoing maintenance of the code. Option C proposes using AWS Lambda functions, which would necessitate extensive custom code for reading, decompressing, parsing, and quality-checking potentially large datasets, likely exceeding Lambda's execution limits or becoming complex to manage for monthly batch processing. Furthermore, triggering Lambda on every object upload is inefficient for a monthly quality check. Option D is fundamentally flawed as Amazon CloudWatch Insights is for analyzing logs and metrics, not for evaluating the content-based data quality of customer data residing in S3 files via SQS messages. Therefore, Option A provides a highly integrated, serverless, and declarative approach to data discovery and quality checks, leading to the least operational overhead while effectively meeting the requirements. Authoritative Links: AWS Glue Data Catalog: https://aws.amazon.com/glue/features/data-catalog/ AWS Glue Data Quality: https://aws.amazon.com/glue/features/data-quality/ AWS Glue Crawlers: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/components-crawlers.html What is AWS Glue Data Quality?: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/dq-rules.html

questions.question: A company has an ML model in Amazon SageMaker AI. An ML engineer needs to implement a monitoring solution to automatically detect changes in the input data distribution of model features. Which solution will meet this requirement with the LEAST operational overhead?

options.A: Configure SageMaker Model Monitor. Establish a data quality baseline. Ensure that the emit_metrics option is enabled in the baseline constraints file. Configure an Amazon CloudWatch alarm to notify the company about changes in specific metrics that are related to data quality.
options.B: Configure SageMaker Model Monitor. Establish a model quality baseline. Ensure that the comparison_method option is set to Robust in the baseline constraints file. Configure an Amazon CloudWatch alarm to notify the company about changes in model quality metrics.
options.C: Use SageMaker Debugger with custom rules to track shifts in feature distributions. Configure Amazon CloudWatch alarms to notify the company when the rules detect significant changes.
options.D: Use Amazon CloudWatch to directly observe the SageMaker AI endpoint’s performance metrics. Manually analyze the CloudWatch logs for indicators of data drift or shifts in feature distribution.

questions.explanation: Option A is the most appropriate solution because it directly leverages Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor, a purpose-built, managed service designed for exactly this kind of monitoring requirement with the least operational overhead. Here's a detailed justification: 1. SageMaker Model Monitor's Purpose: SageMaker Model Monitor is specifically engineered to detect data quality, model quality, bias, and explainability issues in ML models deployed on SageMaker endpoints. Its primary function includes automatically detecting deviations in input data distributions. 2. Data Quality Baseline: The core of detecting changes in input data distribution is establishing a "data quality baseline." This baseline is generated by analyzing a representative dataset (often the training data) and capturing its statistical properties, such as feature distributions, missing values, and data types. Model Monitor then continuously compares incoming inference data to this established baseline. 3. Automatic Detection of Drift: When Model Monitor runs monitoring jobs, it performs statistical tests (e.g., Chi-squared, Kolmogorov-Smirnov, Jensen-Shannon Divergence) to compare the statistics of the incoming data with the baseline. If significant deviations are detected, it signals potential data drift. 4. emit_metrics Option: Enabling the emit_metrics option in the baseline constraints file is crucial. This ensures that Model Monitor publishes detailed data quality violation metrics (e.g., FeatureDrift, DataQualityViolation) to Amazon CloudWatch. These metrics quantify the degree of change detected, making them ideal for automated alarming. 5. CloudWatch Alarms for Notification: Once these data quality metrics are emitted to CloudWatch, an ML engineer can easily configure CloudWatch alarms. These alarms can be set to trigger when specific metrics (like a FeatureDrift score or a DataQualityViolation count) exceed predefined thresholds, automatically notifying the company about changes in the input data distribution. 6. Least Operational Overhead: This solution offers the least operational overhead because SageMaker Model Monitor is a fully managed service. Engineers only need to configure the monitoring schedule and baseline; Amazon handles the underlying infrastructure, computation, and statistical analysis. Integrating with CloudWatch alarms is also a standard, low-overhead configuration. 7. Why other options are less suitable: Option B (Model Quality Baseline): This focuses on the model's performance (e.g., accuracy) based on ground truth, not directly on input data distribution changes. While data drift can impact model quality, monitoring model quality doesn't proactively identify the cause as data drift. Option C (SageMaker Debugger): SageMaker Debugger is primarily for analyzing tensor data during training to identify common ML issues like vanishing gradients or overfitting. While custom rules could theoretically be written to track feature distributions, this would require significant custom development and maintenance, greatly increasing operational overhead compared to Model Monitor's out-of-the-box capabilities for production data drift. Option D (Manual CloudWatch Log Analysis): This is a highly manual and reactive approach. It involves parsing inference logs, extracting features, and then manually performing statistical analysis, which is the opposite of an "automatically detect" requirement with "least operational overhead." In summary, Option A provides a robust, automated, and low-overhead solution by utilizing SageMaker Model Monitor's native data drift detection capabilities, seamlessly integrating with CloudWatch for proactive alerting. Authoritative Links for Further Research: Monitor Models for Data and Model Quality (Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor):https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model-monitor.html SageMaker Model Monitor - Create a Baseline Job:https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model-monitor-create-baseline.html CloudWatch Alarms:https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/monitoring/AlarmThatSendsEmail.html

questions.question: A company is using Amazon SageMaker AI to deploy a new recommendation model for its ecommerce website. The model must use data from all client website interactions as input. Traffic is variable throughout the day. The company needs to create an inference endpoint for the model. Which type of inference endpoint will meet these requirements MOST cost-effectively?

options.A: Batch transform inference endpoint
options.B: Asynchronous inference endpoint
options.C: Real-time inference endpoint
options.D: Serverless inference endpoint

questions.explanation: D is correct because SageMaker Serverless Inference eliminates baseline instance costs by scaling to zero and charging per invocation/duration, making it the most cost-effective choice for highly variable, per-interaction web traffic. From a cost-model and systems perspective, converting fixed infrastructure costs into purely variable costs (cost = Σ invocations × duration × memory rate) minimizes wasted spend during idle periods and aligns expenses with actual user interaction patterns. Serverless also removes operational overhead (no instance provisioning, patching, or capacity planning), supports synchronous real-time invocations suitable for per-interaction recommendations, and autos-scales concurrency to match bursty traffic. Architecturally it enables an event-driven inference pattern where each website interaction can be submitted immediately without introducing batch latency or holding resources idle. The primary tradeoffs—memory, payload size, timeout constraints and potential cold starts—are generally acceptable for recommendation workloads and are outweighed by cost savings unless the application requires sustained, ultra-low-latency at very high throughput. Critical evaluation of other choices: A. Batch transform — Incorrect: built for offline, high-throughput batch jobs with high latency and no synchronous per-interaction responses; not suitable for live recommendation needs. B. Asynchronous inference — Incorrect: intended for long-running or large-payload requests with response polling/queuing; still relies on provisioned endpoint resources and is suboptimal for many small, synchronous web requests. C. Real-time inference — Incorrect: provides low latency but requires provisioned instances (or a nonzero minimum autoscaling capacity) that incur continuous costs during idle periods, making it less cost-effective under variable traffic. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/serverless- inference.htmlhttps://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/inference- endpoints.htmlhttps://aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/pricing/serverless-inference/

questions.question: A company runs an Amazon SageMaker AI domain in a public subnet of a newly created VPC. The network is configured properly, and ML engineers can access the SageMaker AI domain. Recently, the company discovered suspicious traffic to the domain from a specific IP address. The company needs to block traffic from the specific IP address. Which update to the network configuration will meet this requirement?

options.A: Create a security group inbound rule to deny traffic from the specific IP address. Assign the security group to the domain.
options.B: Create a network ACL inbound rule to deny traffic from the specific IP address. Assign the rule to the default network ACL for the subnet where the domain is located.
options.C: Create a shadow variant for the domain. Configure SageMaker Inference Recommender to send traffic from the specific IP address to the shadow endpoint.
options.D: Create a VPC route table to deny inbound traffic from the specific IP address. Assign the route table to the domain.

questions.explanation: B is correct because an Amazon VPC network ACL (NACL) can explicitly deny traffic by source IP at the subnet boundary, allowing you to block the offending address before it reaches the SageMaker domain. Reasoning: Network ACLs operate at the subnet level and support explicit deny rules evaluated in rule- number order, so adding a deny entry for the specific IP CIDR on the subnet that contains the domain immediately blocks ingress (and, if required, egress) from that source; NACLs are evaluated before security group checks and therefore provide an effective perimeter filter. Security groups are stateful allow-only constructs and cannot express explicit deny semantics, so they cannot be used to block a particular source IP. Route tables perform destination-based routing (they forward traffic to targets) and have no capability to filter or deny by source IP. The SageMaker Inference/“shadow” workflow is an application-level testing mechanism and does not provide network-layer access control or IP blocking. Critical evaluation of incorrect choices: A — Security group inbound rule to deny: incorrect because security groups do not support deny rules; they only allow traffic and are evaluated after NACLs. You cannot create an explicit “deny” security group entry to block a specific source IP. C — Shadow variant / Inference Recommender: incorrect because shadowing is for model evaluation/traffic mirroring at the inference layer, not for network filtering; it cannot prevent traffic from reaching the domain or drop connections from a specific IP. D — Route table to deny inbound traffic: incorrect because route tables are destination-driven and do not provide source-IP filtering or deny semantics; they control routing targets, not packet acceptance. Operational notes: implement the deny with a low-numbered NACL rule for the offending /32 (or appropriate CIDR), and remember NACLs are stateless—if you need to fully block two-way flows, ensure corresponding outbound rules are also configured (or left to default denies). References:https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc-network- acls.htmlhttps://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/VPC_SecurityGroups.htmlhttps://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/VPC_Route_Tables.html

questions.question: A company’s ML engineer is creating a classification model. The ML engineer explores the dataset and notices a column that is named day_of_week. The column’s data consists of the following values: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, and Sunday. Which technique should the ML engineer use to convert this column’s data to binary values?

options.A: Binary encoding
options.B: Label encoding
options.C: One-hot encoding
options.D: Tokenization

questions.explanation: The most appropriate technique to convert the day_of_week column, which contains nominal categorical data like Monday, Tuesday, etc., into a format suitable for a classification model is One-Hot Encoding. This method is crucial because the days of the week have no inherent ordinal relationship; for example, "Monday" is not numerically "less" or "greater" than "Tuesday." One-Hot Encoding addresses this by creating new binary columns, one for each unique category in the original column. For instance, instead of a single day_of_week column, there would be seven new columns: day_is_Monday, day_is_Tuesday, ..., day_is_Sunday. For each row, the column corresponding to the actual day would have a value of 1, and all other new day columns would have a value of 0. This direct representation using binary values (0s and 1s) prevents the machine learning model from inferring misleading numerical relationships or magnitudes between categories. This technique is widely used in feature engineering pipelines, including those within AWS SageMaker, to prepare categorical data for model training. Label Encoding (B) would assign a unique integer to each day (e.g., Monday=0, Tuesday=1). This introduces an artificial ordinal relationship that can misguide models, implying that Tuesday is somehow "greater" than Monday, which is incorrect for nominal data. Binary Encoding (A) first maps categories to integers, then converts these integers into binary code, creating multiple new columns. While it uses binary values, for a small number of nominal categories like days of the week, One-Hot Encoding is often preferred for its explicit, direct representation without introducing an intermediate integer mapping that might still imply some arbitrary ordering. Tokenization (D) is primarily used for processing unstructured text data by breaking it into smaller units (tokens) and is entirely irrelevant for a structured categorical column like day_of_week. Therefore, One-Hot Encoding provides a clear, unambiguous, and robust way to represent nominal categorical data using binary values without imposing unintended numerical interpretations, making it the superior choice for preparing this feature for an ML classification model. For further reading on categorical encoding techniques: Scikit-learn documentation on preprocessing: https://scikit- learn.org/stable/modules/preprocessing.html#encoding-categorical-features AWS SageMaker Feature Engineering best practices: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/feature-engineering.html

questions.question: An ML engineer wants to use Amazon SageMaker AI to prepare data for training. During exploratory data analysis, the ML engineer notices that several categorical features are missing values. How can the ML engineer use SageMaker AI to solve this problem?

options.A: Use SageMaker Clarify to impute categorical features with the mean value.
options.B: Use SageMaker Clarity to impute categorical features with the mode value.
options.C: Use SageMaker Data Wrangler to impute categorical features with the mean value.
options.D: Use SageMaker Data Wrangler to impute categorical features with the mode value.

questions.explanation: The problem involves imputing missing values in categorical features during data preparation for machine learning. The most suitable service for this task within Amazon SageMaker AI is SageMaker Data Wrangler. SageMaker Data Wrangler is a dedicated service designed for data aggregation, preparation, and transformation, offering a visual interface to streamline the data cleaning process. It provides numerous built- in transformations, including specific options for handling missing values across different data types. This makes it an ideal tool for an ML engineer to perform exploratory data analysis and prepare datasets for training efficiently. When dealing with categorical features, using the mean value for imputation is statistically incorrect and inappropriate. The mean is a measure of central tendency applicable only to numerical data, where arithmetic operations are meaningful. Categorical data, such as "color" or "product type," does not have a mathematical average. Conversely, the mode represents the most frequently occurring value in a dataset. For categorical features, imputing missing values with the mode ensures that the imputed data maintains the statistical properties and distribution of the existing categories. This method replaces missing categories with the dominant category, which is a common and robust approach for categorical missing value imputation. SageMaker Clarify, mentioned in options A and B, serves a different purpose within the SageMaker ecosystem. Its primary function is to detect potential bias in machine learning models and data, and to provide model explainability. It is not designed for data preparation, cleaning, or imputation of missing values. Therefore, using SageMaker Clarify for this task would be incorrect. By combining the appropriate service (SageMaker Data Wrangler for data preparation) with the correct statistical method for categorical features (imputing with the mode), an ML engineer can effectively address the problem of missing categorical values. SageMaker Data Wrangler allows users to apply this transformation easily as part of their data flow, ensuring high-quality data for subsequent model training. Authoritative Links: Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler: https://aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/data-wrangler/ Amazon SageMaker Clarify: https://aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/clarify/ Using Data Wrangler to prepare data (AWS Documentation): https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/data-wrangler.html

questions.question: A company stores user clickstream data in an Amazon S3 bucket in AWS Account A. The company needs to use the data to train an ML model in Amazon SageMaker AI in AWS Account B. The training will take 10 days. The company needs to use only private IP addresses in the training. The company also must make sure that no training metadata is shared with AWS. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Set up VPC peering between Account A and Account B. Contact AWS by email to opt out of metadata collection.
options.B: Set up a VPC endpoint for the S3 bucket. Set the SageMaker AI OPT_OUT_TRACKING environment variable to 1 in the training job.
options.C: Configure a security group policy that is assigned to the S3 bucket in Account A to allow access from only Account B. Create AI services opt-out policies.
options.D: Generate presigned URLs with expiration times for the objects that are stored in the S3 bucket. Access the data by using the presigned URLs. Set the SageMaker AI OPT_OUT_TRACKING environment variable to 1 in the training job.

questions.explanation: The solution involves two key components to address the company's requirements: private IP address usage and prevention of metadata sharing. First, to ensure that the Amazon SageMaker AI training job in AWS Account B uses only private IP addresses to access the Amazon S3 bucket in AWS Account A, a VPC endpoint for S3 is the correct approach. A Gateway VPC Endpoint for S3 allows instances within a VPC (in this case, the VPC where SageMaker trains) to access S3 buckets entirely within the AWS network using private IP addresses, bypassing the public internet. When a SageMaker training job is configured to run in a specific VPC that has an S3 Gateway Endpoint, all S3 traffic originating from the training instances will automatically route through this private endpoint. This satisfies the requirement for private IP address communication and is suitable for a long-running training job over 10 days, providing persistent and reliable private access. Second, to ensure that no training metadata is shared with AWS, setting the SageMaker AI OPT_OUT_TRACKING environment variable to 1 in the training job configuration is the direct and authoritative method. SageMaker, by default, may collect certain diagnostic and usage metadata to improve the service. This environment variable explicitly signals SageMaker to disable such collection for that specific training job, thereby meeting the strict data privacy requirement. Let's evaluate why other options are not suitable: A. VPC peering alone does not privatize S3 access; S3 is a regional service accessed via endpoints. While peering enables private communication between VPCs, S3 access still requires a private endpoint in the calling VPC for full privacy. Contacting AWS by email is not a standard or programmatic way to manage metadata collection for a specific job. C. Security group policy for S3 bucket is incorrect because S3 buckets do not have security groups; they use bucket policies for access control. Also, "AI services opt-out policies" is too vague and doesn't specify the precise SageMaker control. D. Presigned URLs primarily handle authentication for S3 objects, not the network path. They typically access S3 via public endpoints unless combined with a private endpoint configuration, which would make the presigned URL itself less relevant for the private path. For a 10-day training job, continuously managing presigned URLs for potentially many objects would be complex and prone to expiration issues, making it operationally unfeasible compared to a persistent VPC endpoint. While OPT_OUT_TRACKING is correct here, the presigned URL approach fails the private IP and operational longevity requirements. Therefore, setting up an S3 VPC endpoint for private network access and using the OPT_OUT_TRACKING environment variable for metadata privacy directly addresses all specified requirements in a robust and AWS- native manner. Authoritative Links for further research: Amazon S3 Gateway VPC Endpoints: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc-endpoints- s3.html Configuring SageMaker Training Jobs with VPCs: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/train- vpc.html Controlling metadata collection in Amazon SageMaker (OPT_OUT_TRACKING): Search for "OPT_OUT_TRACKING" in the Amazon SageMaker developer guide for specific service versions or API references for the exact parameter usage. Generally, this setting is passed as an environment variable to the training job. (Note: Direct authoritative link for OPT_OUT_TRACKING as a single, static page is less common as it's often part of broader documentation or SDK examples. The concept is documented within SageMaker's privacy features.) A good starting point would be the SageMaker Python SDK documentation for Estimator or TrainingJob classes which often detail environment variables. For example, within the SageMaker Python SDK sagemaker.estimator.Estimator class, environment parameter allows setting these variables.

questions.question: A music streaming company constantly streams song ratings from an application to an Amazon S3 bucket. The company wants to use the ratings as an input for training and inference of an Amazon SageMaker AI model. The company has an AWS Glue Data Catalog that is configured with the S3 bucket as the source. An ML engineer needs to implement a solution to create a repository for this data. The solution must ensure that the data stays synchronized during batch training and real-time inference. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Ingest data into SageMaker Feature Store from the S3 bucket. Apply tags and indexes.
options.B: Use Amazon Athena. Create tables by using CREATE TABLE AS SELECT (CTAS) queries to group data.
options.C: Use AWS Lake Formation. Apply tag-based control on the data.
options.D: Use the Generate Data Insights function in SageMaker Data Wrangler.

questions.explanation: The correct solution is A. Ingest data into SageMaker Feature Store from the S3 bucket. Apply tags and indexes. Here's a detailed justification: 1. SageMaker Feature Store Purpose: Amazon SageMaker Feature Store is a purpose-built service for machine learning (ML) engineers to create, store, and share features for training and inference. Its core design addresses the exact challenge of maintaining feature consistency across different ML stages. 2. Synchronization for Batch and Real-time: The primary requirement is that the data stays synchronized during both batch training and real-time inference. SageMaker Feature Store achieves this by maintaining two distinct but synchronized stores: Online Store: A low-latency store optimized for real-time inference. When an ML model needs to make a prediction, it can quickly fetch the latest features from the online store, ensuring that real- time inference uses the most up-to-date information. Offline Store: A store optimized for batch processing and historical data analysis, typically stored in Amazon S3 (and cataloged by AWS Glue). This store is ideal for retrieving large datasets for model training, backfilling features, or debugging. 3. Data Ingestion and Consistency: When data (like song ratings from the S3 bucket) is ingested into a Feature Group in SageMaker Feature Store, it is automatically written to both the online and offline stores. This dual-write mechanism guarantees that features used for batch training (from the offline store) and real-time inference (from the online store) are consistent and synchronized, preventing training-serving skew. 4. Integration with S3 and Glue Data Catalog: The problem statement mentions data in an S3 bucket and an AWS Glue Data Catalog. SageMaker Feature Store can easily ingest data from S3, and its offline store is typically integrated with the Glue Data Catalog, allowing existing catalog configurations to be leveraged. 5. Tags and Indexes: Applying tags and indexes within Feature Store (as suggested in option A) is a standard practice to organize, discover, and efficiently query feature groups, improving manageability, especially as the number of features and models grows. Why other options are not suitable: B. Use Amazon Athena. Create tables by using CREATE TABLE AS SELECT (CTAS) queries to group data. While Athena is excellent for querying data in S3 using SQL and CTAS queries can organize data, it is primarily a query service, not a feature repository designed for low-latency real-time serving. Querying S3 directly via Athena for real-time inference would introduce unacceptable latency and would not inherently provide the synchronized online/offline store mechanism that Feature Store offers. C. Use AWS Lake Formation. Apply tag-based control on the data. AWS Lake Formation is a service that helps build, secure, and manage data lakes. It provides robust security and governance features (like tag- based access control) over data in S3 and Glue Data Catalog. However, Lake Formation itself does not provide the specialized online/offline storage and synchronization capabilities required for ML feature serving and training that SageMaker Feature Store does. It focuses on the data lake foundation rather than the specific ML feature repository requirement. D. Use the Generate Data Insights function in SageMaker Data Wrangler. SageMaker Data Wrangler is a tool for data preparation and feature engineering. The "Generate Data Insights" function specifically analyzes data characteristics to help users understand their datasets. It is a data exploration and transformation tool, not a data repository that stores and serves features for training and real-time inference in a synchronized manner. Therefore, SageMaker Feature Store is the most appropriate solution because it directly addresses the critical need for a synchronized repository that can efficiently serve features for both batch training and real- time inference with low latency. Authoritative Links for further research: Amazon SageMaker Feature Store: https://aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/feature-store/ Amazon SageMaker Feature Store Documentation: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/feature-store.html Prevent Training-Serving Skew with Amazon SageMaker Feature Store: https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/prevent-training-serving-skew-with-amazon-sagemaker- feature-store/

questions.question: A hospital is using an ML model to validate x-ray results. The hospital runs a nightly batch inference job. The hospital needs to produce a daily report about model data quality and model performance. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Schedule a monitoring job in Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor. Generate the monitoring results for the model and data.
options.B: Create an Amazon CloudWatch dashboard that includes the metrics for processing steps in the nightly batch inference job. Compare the baseline resource metrics. Share the dashboard link.
options.C: Use AWS Glue DataBrew to create a custom recipe job that uses the Numerical Statistics data quality check for the model file. Generate the results.
options.D: Create a SageMaker AI pipeline that includes a QualityCheck step to run monitoring jobs. Generate the monitoring results for the model and the data.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is A. Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor is specifically designed to address the requirements of monitoring machine learning models in production, including both data quality and model performance. Here's a detailed justification: Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor allows hospitals to automatically detect deviations in data quality and model performance metrics for models deployed in production, even with nightly batch inference jobs. It works by continuously analyzing the input data, output predictions, and potentially ground truth labels of the model to detect data drift, model drift, and other quality issues. For "model data quality," Model Monitor can compare the statistical properties of the incoming inference data (from the nightly batch job) against a baseline dataset (typically the training data). This comparison helps identify drifts in feature distributions, missing values, or changes in data types that could degrade model performance. For "model performance," Model Monitor can be configured to integrate with ground truth data. If the hospital can collect actual outcomes (e.g., confirmed diagnoses by a radiologist) after the x-ray predictions, Model Monitor can compare the model's predictions with these ground truth labels to calculate actual performance metrics like accuracy, precision, recall, or F1-score over time. Even in the absence of immediate ground truth, significant data drift detected by Model Monitor serves as an early warning indicator that model performance is likely to degrade. The monitoring results generated can be used to produce the required daily report, providing actionable insights into the model's health. Let's look at why the other options are less suitable: B. Create an Amazon CloudWatch dashboard... Compare the baseline resource metrics: While CloudWatch is excellent for monitoring infrastructure metrics (CPU, memory, network) of the underlying compute resources running the batch job, it does not directly assess the quality of the model's input data or the model's predictive performance. Resource metrics do not tell you if the x-ray images' features have drifted or if the model's accuracy has dropped. C. Use AWS Glue DataBrew to create a custom recipe job... Numerical Statistics data quality check for the model file: AWS Glue DataBrew is primarily a data preparation and profiling service. While it can perform data quality checks on datasets, it is not purpose-built for continuous, comparative monitoring of inference data drift against a training baseline in the context of an ML model, nor does it inherently assess model performance by comparing predictions to ground truth. It's more suited for pre-processing and initial data validation. D. Create a SageMaker AI pipeline that includes a QualityCheck step...: SageMaker Pipelines orchestrates ML workflows (training, model registration, deployment). A QualityCheck step within a pipeline is typically used for data validation before training or model evaluation after training to ensure a candidate model meets quality thresholds. While you could trigger a Model Monitor job from a pipeline, the pipeline itself is an orchestration tool, not the primary monitoring mechanism for ongoing production inference data and model performance drift. Model Monitor is the dedicated service for continuous monitoring of deployed models. Therefore, Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor is the most appropriate and direct solution for meeting the hospital's requirements for daily reports on model data quality and model performance from a nightly batch inference job. Authoritative Links for further research: Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor: https://aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/model-monitor/ Monitor Models with Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model-monitor.html

questions.question: A company needs to ingest data from data sources into Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler. The data sources are Amazon S3, Amazon Redshift, and Snowflake. The ingested data must always be up to date with the latest changes in the source systems. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Use direct connections to import data from the data sources into Data Wrangler.
options.B: Use cataloged connections to import data from the data sources into Data Wrangler.
options.C: Use AWS Glue to extract data from the data sources. Use AWS Glue also to import the data directly into Data Wrangler.
options.D: Use AWS Lambda to extract data from the data sources. Use Lambda also to import the data directly into Data Wrangler.

questions.explanation: The correct solution is A because Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler is specifically designed to establish direct connections to various data sources, including Amazon S3, Amazon Redshift, and external databases like Snowflake, to facilitate interactive data preparation. Here's a detailed justification: 1. Direct Connections in Data Wrangler: SageMaker Data Wrangler provides native connectors and capabilities to directly access data from numerous sources. For Amazon S3, users can browse buckets and select specific files or folders. For Amazon Redshift, Data Wrangler offers a direct connector where users can specify connection details and query tables. Similarly, for external databases like Snowflake, Data Wrangler supports connections via JDBC drivers or other dedicated connectors, allowing direct querying. 2. Ensuring "Up-to-date" Data: The core requirement is that the ingested data "must always be up to date with the latest changes in the source systems." When Data Wrangler uses a direct connection, it retrieves the data from the source system at the time of the import or preview. If the underlying data in S3, Redshift, or Snowflake changes, the next time Data Wrangler refreshes or processes the data from that direct connection, it will reflect the most current state. This immediacy is crucial for maintaining real-time freshness during interactive data preparation. 3. Limitations of Other Options: B. Cataloged connections (AWS Glue Data Catalog): While Data Wrangler can use AWS Glue Data Catalog tables as a source, the catalog primarily stores metadata (schema and location). For the catalog itself to be "up-to-date" with the source systems (S3, Redshift, Snowflake), AWS Glue Crawlers or custom ETL processes would need to run regularly to update the catalog. This introduces an extra layer of dependency and potential latency compared to a direct connection, as Data Wrangler would be reading from the catalog's view, not directly from the live source. C. AWS Glue to extract data and import directly into Data Wrangler: AWS Glue is an excellent ETL service for batch processing and data movement. However, Glue jobs typically extract data and write it to a destination (e.g., S3). Data Wrangler would then ingest from that destination, not directly from the running Glue job itself. This creates a snapshot of the data at the time the Glue job ran, rather than providing a live, up-to-date view directly from the source systems for interactive preparation. To get the "latest changes," the Glue job would need to run continuously or extremely frequently, and Data Wrangler would need to re-ingest repeatedly from its output. D. AWS Lambda to extract data and import directly into Data Wrangler: Similar to AWS Glue, AWS Lambda is a compute service that can extract data. However, Lambda functions are typically invoked for specific tasks and would likely output data to an intermediary storage like S3. Data Wrangler would then read from this S3 location. This method also involves an indirect, snapshot-based approach, requiring frequent Lambda invocations and subsequent re-ingestion by Data Wrangler to reflect the latest changes, making it inefficient and not suitable for the interactive, up-to-date requirement. In summary, direct connections allow SageMaker Data Wrangler to pull the most current data directly from S3, Redshift, and Snowflake as needed for interactive analysis and transformation, aligning perfectly with the requirement for "always up to date" data. Authoritative Links for Further Research: SageMaker Data Wrangler Overview: https://aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/data-wrangler/ Connect Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler to various data sources: https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/connect-amazon-sagemaker-data-wrangler-to-various- data-sources/ Prepare data with Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/data-wrangler.html (Refer to "Importing data" section)

questions.question: An ML engineer is using Amazon SageMaker Canvas to build a custom ML model from an imported dataset. The ML engineer wants the model to make continuous numeric predictions based on 10 years of data. Which metric should the ML engineer use to evaluate the model’s performance?

options.A: Accuracy
options.B: InferenceLatency
options.C: Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC)
options.D: Root mean square error (RMSE)

questions.explanation: The ML engineer is building a model to make "continuous numeric predictions." This critical detail immediately categorizes the problem as a regression task. In regression, the goal is to predict a numerical value, rather than a category or class. Let's evaluate the given options in the context of a regression problem: A. Accuracy: Accuracy is a common metric used for classification problems, where the model predicts discrete categories (e.g., spam/not spam, dog/cat). It measures the proportion of correct predictions out of the total. Since the model is making continuous numeric predictions, accuracy is not applicable. B. Inference Latency: Inference latency measures the time it takes for a model to make a prediction after receiving input. While an important operational metric for production systems, it does not evaluate the quality or correctness of the predictions themselves. It's a performance metric, not an error metric. C. Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC): AUC is predominantly used for binary classification problems to assess a model's ability to distinguish between two classes across various threshold settings. It plots the true positive rate against the false positive rate. Like accuracy, AUC is irrelevant for a model making continuous numeric predictions. D. Root mean square error (RMSE): RMSE is a standard and highly appropriate metric for evaluating models in regression tasks. It measures the average magnitude of the errors between the predicted values and the actual observed values. The "error" in this context is the difference between what the model predicted and the true numeric value. RMSE is calculated by taking the square root of the average of the squared errors. By squaring the errors, RMSE gives higher penalties to larger errors, which is often desirable when significant deviations are particularly problematic. Furthermore, RMSE provides an error value in the same units as the target variable, making it interpretable and easy to understand the typical magnitude of prediction errors for continuous outputs over the 10 years of data. Therefore, for a model designed for continuous numeric predictions, RMSE is the most suitable metric to evaluate its performance effectively. Authoritative Links for Further Research: Understanding Machine Learning Metrics (AWS Blog): This blog post provides a good overview of various ML metrics, including those for regression and classification.https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine- learning/understanding-your-machine-learning-metrics/ Model Evaluation Metrics (Amazon SageMaker Developer Guide): While SageMaker Canvas simplifies the process, the underlying metrics are consistent with general SageMaker capabilities. This documentation describes common metrics used.https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model-metrics.html Root Mean Square Error (Wikipedia): For a detailed mathematical and conceptual explanation of RMSE.https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Root-mean-square_deviation

questions.question: A company has built, trained, and tuned two new ML models: •Model A detects if a transaction is fraudulent based on the IP address, location, and user credentials. This model will be accessed every time a transaction occurs. •Model B forecasts sales totals for the next month based on historical sales data. This model will be accessed one time each month. The company must deploy both models to production by using Amazon SageMaker AI. Which hosting solution for the models should the company use to meet these requirements?

options.A: Host both models in one container behind one real-time endpoint.
options.B: Host Model A with an asynchronous endpoint. Host Model B with a real-time endpoint.
options.C: Host Model A with a real-time endpoint. Use batch transform for Model B.
options.D: Use batch transform for Model A. Host Model B with an asynchronous endpoint.

questions.explanation: The company's two ML models have distinct operational requirements that necessitate different Amazon SageMaker hosting solutions. Model A: Fraud DetectionThis model detects fraudulent transactions based on real-time inputs (IP address, location, user credentials) and "will be accessed every time a transaction occurs." This implies a critical need for low-latency, immediate inference responses. Amazon SageMaker's real-time endpoints are designed precisely for such scenarios. They provide persistent, always-on endpoints that can serve predictions with very low latency, making them ideal for interactive applications where immediate decisions, like flagging a fraudulent transaction, are essential. Model B: Sales ForecastingThis model forecasts sales totals for the next month based on historical sales data and "will be accessed one time each month." This scenario does not require an immediate, interactive response. Instead, it involves processing a batch of historical data periodically. Amazon SageMaker Batch Transform is the most suitable solution here. Batch Transform is designed for offline processing of large datasets where predictions are not required in real time. It processes the input data in batches, stores the output in Amazon S3, and then de-provisions the compute resources, making it highly cost-effective for infrequent, large-scale inference tasks. Deploying Model B on an always-on real-time or asynchronous endpoint would be inefficient and unnecessarily expensive given its monthly execution schedule. Why Option C is Optimal: Model A with a real-time endpoint perfectly addresses the need for instant, low-latency fraud detection, ensuring transactions can be processed without delay. Model B with Batch Transform provides an efficient and cost-effective way to generate monthly sales forecasts without the need for a continuously running, expensive endpoint. It scales compute resources only when needed for the monthly job. Why other options are suboptimal: A (Host both in one container behind one real-time endpoint): Model B would incur significant unnecessary costs for an always-on endpoint when only accessed monthly. B (Model A with asynchronous, Model B with real-time): Model A cannot use an asynchronous endpoint as it requires immediate, synchronous fraud detection. Model B would still be over-provisioned with a real-time endpoint. D (Batch transform for Model A, Asynchronous for Model B): Batch Transform for Model A would introduce unacceptable delays for fraud detection. While asynchronous could work for Model B, Batch Transform is generally more cost-effective for truly infrequent, large-batch processing, as it doesn't maintain an underlying persistent endpoint. Therefore, Option C provides the optimal balance of performance, latency, and cost-efficiency for both models according to their specific requirements. Authoritative Links for Further Research: Amazon SageMaker Real-time Endpoints: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/realtime- endpoints.html Amazon SageMaker Batch Transform: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/batch- transform.html Amazon SageMaker Asynchronous Endpoints (for comparison): https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/async-inference.html

questions.question: A bank needs to use Amazon SageMaker AI to create an ML model to determine which customers qualify for a new product. The bank must use algorithms that SageMaker AI directly supports. The model must be explainable to the bank’s regulators. Which modeling approach will meet these requirements?

options.A: Train the model by using the Object2Vec algorithm.
options.B: Train the model by using the linear learner algorithm.
options.C: Train a neural network.
options.D: Train the model by using the k-means algorithm.

questions.explanation: The bank's requirement to determine customer qualification for a new product is a classic supervised binary classification problem. The model must use algorithms directly supported by Amazon SageMaker and, critically, be explainable to bank regulators. Option B, training the model using the Linear Learner algorithm, directly addresses all these requirements. Amazon SageMaker's Linear Learner is a built-in, highly optimized algorithm capable of performing both classification and regression tasks. For this scenario, it would typically implement logistic regression for binary classification (qualify/not qualify). The primary advantage of linear models, such as those produced by Linear Learner, is their inherent interpretability. The model's coefficients directly indicate the weight and direction of influence each input feature has on the prediction. For instance, a positive coefficient for "credit score" would show that a higher score increases the likelihood of qualification. This transparent relationship allows regulators to easily understand why a particular customer was approved or denied based on their specific attributes. This level of explainability is crucial for financial institutions, which are subject to stringent regulatory oversight demanding transparency and fairness in decision-making processes. In contrast, other options fall short. Training a neural network (Option C) would typically result in a "black box" model. While powerful, the complex non-linear relationships and hidden layers make deep neural networks difficult to interpret, hindering the ability to explain individual predictions to regulators in a straightforward manner. Similarly, Object2Vec (Option A) is designed for learning embeddings of objects for similarity tasks, not directly for explainable classification predictions. K-means (Option D) is an unsupervised clustering algorithm, which is inappropriate for a supervised prediction task like determining customer qualification. Therefore, the Linear Learner algorithm stands out as the optimal choice due to its direct SageMaker support, suitability for classification, and superior inherent explainability, which is paramount for regulatory compliance. Authoritative Links: Amazon SageMaker Built-in Algorithms: This documentation lists and describes all algorithms natively supported by SageMaker, including Linear Learner.https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/algos.html Amazon SageMaker Linear Learner Algorithm: Specific details on how Linear Learner works and its capabilities.https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/linear-learner.html Machine Learning Interpretability & Explainability: A general concept crucial for understanding why linear models are preferred in regulated industries. (While not AWS specific, it underpins the decision.)https://aws.amazon.com/machine-learning/ml-explainability/

questions.question: A company is preparing data to train a new ML model on Amazon SageMaker AI. The data has not been used before for ML training. The data includes duplicates and is missing some values. The company needs to increase the data quality and detect any statistical bias in the data. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Use SageMaker Clarify to create data quality rules. Use SageMaker Model Monitor to detect bias.
options.B: Use SageMaker Data Wrangler to create data quality rules. Use SageMaker Clarify to detect bias.
options.C: Use SageMaker Debugger to create data quality rules. Use SageMaker Model Monitor to detect bias.
options.D: Use SageMaker Model Monitor to create data quality rules. Use SageMaker Clarify to detect bias.

questions.explanation: The company needs to address two distinct challenges: increasing data quality by handling issues like duplicates and missing values, and detecting statistical bias in the dataset. Amazon SageMaker offers specialized tools for each of these requirements. For increasing data quality and creating data quality rules:Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler is the ideal solution. Data Wrangler provides a unified interface to prepare data for machine learning. It allows users to connect to various data sources, visually explore data, apply over 300 built-in data transformations (including handling missing values, standardizing features, and removing duplicates), and even create custom transformations. Users can define data quality rules within Data Wrangler to validate data, identify anomalies, and ensure consistency before training. This direct functionality perfectly aligns with the need to improve data quality and create rules. For detecting statistical bias in the data:Amazon SageMaker Clarify is specifically designed for bias detection and explainability for ML models. It can analyze the raw input data (pre-training data) to detect statistical biases related to sensitive attributes (e.g., gender, age, race) before a model is even trained. Clarify helps identify potential fairness issues in the dataset itself by examining feature distributions and group disparities. This preemptive analysis is crucial for building fair and responsible AI models. Why Option B is correct and others are not: Option B: "Use SageMaker Data Wrangler to create data quality rules. Use SageMaker Clarify to detect bias." This accurately maps each requirement to the most appropriate SageMaker service. Data Wrangler excels at data preparation and quality rule definition, while Clarify is purpose-built for bias detection. SageMaker Model Monitor is primarily used for monitoring models in production for data drift, concept drift, and model quality, not for initial data preparation or detecting pre-training statistical bias in the raw dataset. SageMaker Debugger is used to inspect training job metrics and tensors during model training to identify issues like overfitting, underfitting, or vanishing/exploding gradients, not for data quality rules or statistical bias detection in the input data. Therefore, using SageMaker Data Wrangler for data quality and SageMaker Clarify for bias detection provides a comprehensive and effective solution for the company's requirements. Authoritative Links: Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler: https://aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/data-wrangler/ Amazon SageMaker Clarify: https://aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/clarify/ Prepare ML data with Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/data-wrangler.html Detect and mitigate bias and explain predictions with SageMaker Clarify: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/clarify.html

questions.question: An ML engineer is building a model to predict house and apartment prices. The model uses three features: Square Meters, Price, and Age of Building. The dataset has 10,000 data rows. The data includes data points for one large mansion and one extremely small apartment. The ML engineer must perform preprocessing on the dataset to ensure that the model produces accurate predictions for the typical house or apartment. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Remove the outliers and perform a log transformation on the Square Meters variable.
options.B: Keep the outliers and perform normalization on the Square Meters variable.
options.C: Remove the outliers and perform one-hot encoding on the Square Meters variable.
options.D: Keep the outliers and perform one-hot encoding on the Square Meters variable.

questions.explanation: A is correct because removing extreme leverage points and applying a log transform to Square Meters reduces skew and heteroscedasticity while preventing two anomalous observations from disproportionately biasing a model intended to predict typical prices. Removing the mansion and tiny apartment addresses influence and leverage (which can distort parameter estimates and loss gradients), and the log transform linearizes multiplicative size–price relationships and stabilizes variance, improving fit and generalization for the bulk of the distribution. From a statistical perspective, outlier exclusion (or winsorizing) plus a variance- stabilizing transform is a standard approach when the objective is to model the central tendency rather than rare extremes; robust diagnostics (e.g., Cook’s distance or OLS influence measures) should be used to identify such points before removal. Normalization (min–max or z-scoring) rescales features but does not reduce the leverage of extreme values or correct skewness, so it does not address the core problem here. One-hot encoding is inappropriate for a continuous, high-cardinality metric like square meters because it explodes dimensionality, removes ordinal information, and produces sparse representations that harm statistical efficiency and interpretability. Therefore: B. Incorrect — normalization keeps the outliers’ leverage and does not correct skew or heteroscedasticity, so learned coefficients and error metrics remain biased by the extremes. C. Incorrect — removing outliers is beneficial but one-hot encoding a continuous variable is a misuse that sacrifices order and yields massive, sparse feature expansion. D. Incorrect — keeping outliers plus one-hot encoding both preserves influential points and applies an unsuitable encoding for continuous size, compounding modeling problems. https://scikit- learn.org/stable/modules/preprocessing.htmlhttps://www.statsmodels.org/stable/generated/statsmodels.stats.outliers_influence.OLSInfluence.html

questions.question: A company is developing an ML model by using Amazon SageMaker AI. The company must monitor bias in the model and must display the results on a dashboard. An ML engineer creates a bias monitoring job. How should the ML engineer capture bias metrics to display on the dashboard?

options.A: Capture AWS CloudTrail metrics from SageMaker Clarify.
options.B: Capture Amazon CloudWatch metrics from SageMaker Clarify.
options.C: Capture SageMaker Model Monitor metrics from Amazon EventBridge.
options.D: Capture SageMaker Model Monitor metrics from Amazon Simple Notification Service (Amazon SNS).

questions.explanation: The correct approach for an ML engineer to capture bias metrics and display them on a dashboard after creating a bias monitoring job is to capture Amazon CloudWatch metrics from SageMaker Clarify. Amazon SageMaker Clarify is specifically designed to help detect potential bias in ML models, both during training and in post-deployment (inference). When SageMaker Clarify runs a bias monitoring job, either on- demand or as part of a continuous monitoring schedule (often orchestrated by SageMaker Model Monitor), it generates detailed bias metrics. These metrics quantify various aspects of bias, such as disparity in predicted labels or feature importance across different groups. Crucially, SageMaker Clarify is deeply integrated with Amazon CloudWatch, which is AWS's monitoring and observability service. CloudWatch automatically collects and stores these bias metrics published by SageMaker Clarify. Once these metrics are in CloudWatch, an ML engineer can easily create custom dashboards within the CloudWatch console. These dashboards provide a centralized, visual representation of the bias trends, allowing for effective monitoring and quick identification of any significant deviations or issues. CloudWatch dashboards are ideal for displaying time-series data like bias metrics, offering customizable widgets, alarms, and composite alarms to alert on critical thresholds. Let's look at why the other options are incorrect: A. Capture AWS CloudTrail metrics from SageMaker Clarify: AWS CloudTrail logs API calls and events made in your AWS account. While it records when a SageMaker Clarify job was started or completed, it does not capture the actual bias metrics generated by the job. CloudTrail is for auditing and governance, not for monitoring performance or data quality metrics. C. Capture SageMaker Model Monitor metrics from Amazon EventBridge: SageMaker Model Monitor can detect bias (among other types of drift) and send events to Amazon EventBridge when issues are detected. EventBridge is an event bus that routes events to target services to trigger actions (e.g., invoking a Lambda function or sending an SNS notification). However, EventBridge itself does not store metrics or host dashboards. While it can be part of an alerting pipeline for bias, it doesn't serve as the source for capturing metrics for dashboarding. The underlying metrics that trigger these events would still be in CloudWatch. D. Capture SageMaker Model Monitor metrics from Amazon Simple Notification Service (Amazon SNS): Amazon SNS is a publish/subscribe messaging service primarily used for sending notifications. Similar to EventBridge, it can receive notifications about bias issues detected by Model Monitor, but it does not capture or store the actual metrics, nor does it provide dashboarding capabilities. SNS is for real-time alerting, not metric storage or visualization. Therefore, Amazon CloudWatch is the dedicated service for collecting, aggregating, and visualizing metrics, making it the definitive choice for displaying SageMaker Clarify's bias metrics on a dashboard. Authoritative Links for Further Research: Amazon SageMaker Clarify: https://aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/clarify/ Monitor Models with Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor and Amazon CloudWatch: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model-monitor.html Using Amazon CloudWatch Alarms and Metrics with SageMaker: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/monitoring-cloudwatch.html Amazon CloudWatch Dashboards: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/monitoring/CloudWatch_Dashboards.html

questions.question: A company is using an Amazon SageMaker AI ML model to predict traffic accidents that potholes cause. An ML engineer has configured SageMaker Model Monitor to run as part of a SageMaker AI pipeline. In the MonitoringExecution output, the ML engineer observes several baseline_drift_check violations that are failing the pipeline. What should the ML engineer do to resolve this issue?

options.A: Retrain the model by using a new SageMaker AI training job. Check for errors by using SageMaker Debugger.
options.B: Retrain the model with new training data. Reuse the original baseline in Model Monitor.
options.C: Retrain the model with new training data. Use the new baseline in Model Monitor.
options.D: Rerun the SageMaker AI pipeline after enabling the emit_metrics option in the baseline constraints file.

questions.explanation: The issue of baseline_drift_check violations in Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor indicates that the statistical properties of the inference data being fed to the model have significantly diverged from the baseline data used to establish the original constraints and statistics. This phenomenon, known as data drift or concept drift, suggests that the real-world data distribution has changed, potentially leading to a degradation in the model's prediction accuracy. In the context of predicting traffic accidents caused by potholes, factors like seasonal changes, new road construction, different vehicle types, or improved data collection methods could alter the input features, causing this drift. To resolve this, the ML engineer must address both the model's understanding of the data and Model Monitor's reference point. Retraining the model with new training data that reflects the current data distribution is crucial. This step allows the model to learn and adapt to the updated patterns and characteristics of potholes and accident conditions. However, merely retraining isn't sufficient if the monitoring baseline remains outdated. After the model is retrained with fresh data, its expected input distribution will inherently shift to align with this new training set. Therefore, it is imperative to establish a new baseline for SageMaker Model Monitor using this updated training data (or a representative validation set from it). This new baseline will accurately reflect the statistical properties and feature distributions that the retrained model expects to see during inference. By aligning the model with the current data and updating the monitoring baseline accordingly, Model Monitor can then correctly evaluate future inference data against the model's current understanding, identifying true future drift rather than flagging legitimate shifts due to model updates. Option C directly addresses both aspects: retraining the model with new data to improve its performance and updating the baseline to accurately monitor its new behavior. Authoritative links for further research: Monitor Models for Data and Model Quality: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model- monitor.html SageMaker Model Monitor – Detect and Alert on Data Drift in Production: https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/aws/sagemaker-model-monitor-detect-and-alert-on-data-drift-in-production/

questions.question: A company uses ML models to predict whether transactions are fraudulent. The company needs to identify as many fraudulent transactions as possible. Which evaluation metric should the company use to evaluate the models to meet this requirement?

options.A: F1 score
options.B: Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC)
options.C: Precision
options.D: Recall

questions.explanation: The company's primary goal is to "identify as many fraudulent transactions as possible." This objective directly translates to minimizing the number of false negatives – actual fraudulent transactions that the model incorrectly predicts as legitimate. Recall (also known as Sensitivity or True Positive Rate) is the most appropriate evaluation metric for this requirement. Recall is calculated as the number of true positives (correctly identified fraudulent transactions) divided by the total number of actual fraudulent transactions (true positives + false negatives). A higher Recall score indicates that the model is effectively capturing a larger proportion of the actual fraudulent cases. In the context of fraud detection, missing a fraudulent transaction (a false negative) can lead to significant financial losses for the company, damage to customer trust, and reputational harm. Therefore, maximizing Recall ensures the model is optimized to minimize these costly misses. This focus is often critical in fraud scenarios, even if it means accepting a higher number of false positives (legitimate transactions incorrectly flagged as fraudulent) that would require manual review. While false positives incur operational costs, they are generally less detrimental than undetected fraud. Let's consider why the other options are less suitable: Precision measures the proportion of predicted fraudulent transactions that are actually fraudulent (True Positives / (True Positives + False Positives)). While important for minimizing unnecessary investigations, a model with high precision could still miss many actual frauds if its recall is low, thus not meeting the "as many as possible" criterion. F1 Score is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall. It is useful when there's an equal need to balance both metrics. However, the problem statement clearly indicates a strong preference for identifying as many fraudulent transactions as possible, making Recall the dominant concern. Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC) measures the model's ability to distinguish between classes across various classification thresholds. While AUC provides a good overall summary of a model's performance, it does not directly optimize for a specific business objective like minimizing false negatives at a chosen operating point. For a scenario with a clear priority on one type of error, a direct metric like Recall is more targeted and effective. Therefore, to meet the specific requirement of identifying as many fraudulent transactions as possible, the company should prioritize and evaluate its models primarily based on Recall. Authoritative Links for Further Research: AWS Documentation on Machine Learning Model Evaluation: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/machine- learning/latest/dg/evaluating_models.html Scikit-learn Classification Metrics (General ML Concepts): https://scikit- learn.org/stable/modules/model_evaluation.html#classification-metrics

questions.question: A recommendation model uses ML and calls an Amazon SageMaker AI endpoint to get recommendations. An ML engineer must ensure that the model stays available during an expected increase in user traffic. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Configure auto scaling on the SageMaker AI endpoint.
options.B: Create a new SageMaker AI endpoint. Deploy the model to the new endpoint.
options.C: Use SageMaker Neo to optimize the model for inference.
options.D: Attach an Auto Scaling group to the SageMaker AI endpoint.

questions.explanation: To ensure an Amazon SageMaker AI endpoint remains available during an expected increase in user traffic, configuring auto scaling on the endpoint is the most effective solution. SageMaker endpoints are managed inference services designed to host machine learning models, and they offer built-in integration with AWS's auto scaling capabilities. Auto scaling dynamically adjusts the number of instances supporting the endpoint based on defined metrics, such as CPU utilization, memory usage, or the number of invocations per instance. When user traffic increases, auto scaling automatically provisions more compute instances, distributing the load across them and preventing a single instance from becoming a bottleneck. Conversely, during periods of lower traffic, it scales down the number of instances, optimizing costs without compromising availability. This process leverages Amazon CloudWatch alarms to monitor the endpoint's performance metrics and the Application Auto Scaling service to execute the scaling actions. By setting target utilization levels or specific scaling policies, an ML engineer can proactively prepare the endpoint to handle variable workloads seamlessly. This ensures high availability and consistent low-latency performance, even under sudden or anticipated traffic spikes. Other options are less suitable: Creating a new SageMaker AI endpoint and deploying the model to it does not inherently address dynamic scaling; it merely creates another static endpoint, potentially for A/B testing or blue/green deployments, but doesn't automatically adjust capacity in response to traffic changes on either endpoint. Using SageMaker Neo to optimize the model for inference improves the performance and efficiency of the model on a single instance, reducing latency and potentially memory footprint. While beneficial for overall efficiency and cost, it does not dynamically scale the number of instances to handle increased concurrent requests beyond the capacity of existing instances. Attaching an Auto Scaling group to the SageMaker AI endpoint is conceptually incorrect for SageMaker. While SageMaker's auto scaling uses the Application Auto Scaling service, you configure it directly on the SageMaker endpoint resource through the SageMaker API or console, rather than attaching a traditional EC2- based Auto Scaling group in the typical sense. SageMaker abstracts this underlying mechanism for convenience. Therefore, configuring auto scaling directly on the SageMaker AI endpoint is the precise and correct mechanism to ensure continuous availability and performance during fluctuating user demand. Authoritative Links for Further Research: SageMaker Endpoint Auto Scaling: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/endpoint-auto- scaling.html Application Auto Scaling: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/autoscaling/application/userguide/what-is- application-auto-scaling.html

questions.question: A hospital wants to predict patient outcomes for the coming year. An ML engineer must improve several existing ML models that currently perform poorly. Select the correct regularization method from the following list to improve each model. Select each regularization method one time, more than one time, or not at all. (Choose three.) •L1 regularization •L2 regularization •Early stopping

An ML engineer has trained an ML model by using Amazon SageMaker AI. The ML engineer determines that the model is overfitting and that the training data contains unnecessary features. The ML engineer must reduce the overfitting and the impact of the unnecessary features. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Increase the number of training iterations. Retrain the model.
options.B: Apply L1 regularization to the training data. Retrain the model.
options.C: Decrease the number of training iterations. Retrain the model.
options.D: Use SageMaker Debugger to apply L1 regularization to the running model.

questions.explanation: The correct solution is B. Apply L1 regularization to the training data. Retrain the model. This approach directly addresses both issues identified: overfitting and the presence of unnecessary features. Detailed Justification: 1. Addressing Overfitting with L1 Regularization: Overfitting occurs when a model learns the training data too well, including its noise and irrelevant patterns, failing to generalize to new, unseen data. L1 regularization, also known as Lasso Regression, adds a penalty term to the loss function proportional to the absolute value of the magnitude of the coefficients ($\sum |w_i|$).
