This penalty encourages the model to use smaller weights, making the model simpler and less prone to memorizing noise in the training data, thereby reducing overfitting and improving its ability to generalize. 2. Addressing Unnecessary Features with L1 Regularization: A crucial characteristic of L1 regularization is its ability to perform automatic feature selection. By penalizing the absolute value of coefficients, L1 regularization tends to drive the weights of less important or unnecessary features to exactly zero. When a feature's coefficient becomes zero, it is effectively removed from the model. This directly reduces the impact of unnecessary features, simplifying the model further and contributing to better generalization. 3. Why other options are incorrect: A. Increase the number of training iterations. Retrain the model: Increasing training iterations typically gives the model more opportunities to learn the training data, often exacerbating overfitting rather than reducing it. It does not address unnecessary features directly. C. Decrease the number of training iterations. Retrain the model: While decreasing iterations (a form of early stopping) can prevent overfitting to some extent by stopping training before the model starts to memorize noise, it does not actively identify and reduce the impact of unnecessary features by driving their weights to zero. It's a less direct and comprehensive solution for the given problem. D. Use SageMaker Debugger to apply L1 regularization to the running model: Amazon SageMaker Debugger is a tool designed to monitor and identify issues during training, such as vanishing/exploding gradients, overfitting, or underfitting. However, it does not apply regularization to a running model. Regularization is a hyperparameter that must be configured as part of the model's training algorithm before the training job starts, not dynamically applied by a debugger during execution. Therefore, applying L1 regularization is the most effective and elegant solution that simultaneously tackles both the overfitting problem and the presence of unnecessary features by simplifying the model and performing inherent feature selection. Authoritative Links for Further Research: L1 Regularization (Lasso Regression): AWS Blog on Regularization in Machine Learning: https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine- learning/regularization-in-machine-learning-a-primer/ (This link might not directly be "authoritative" in the sense of a scientific paper, but it's an AWS blog, so it's relevant for AWS context and explains regularization clearly.) Scikit-learn documentation on Lasso (L1 regularization): https://scikit- learn.org/stable/modules/linear_model.html#lasso Amazon SageMaker Debugger: AWS Documentation on Amazon SageMaker Debugger: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/train-debugger.html

questions.question: An ML engineer wants to use Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler to perform preprocessing on a dataset. The ML engineer wants to use the processed dataset to train a classification model. During preprocessing, the ML engineer notices that a text feature has a range of thousands of values that differ only by spelling errors. The ML engineer needs to apply an encoding method so that after preprocessing is complete, the text feature can be used to train the model. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Perform ordinal encoding to represent categories of the feature.
options.B: Perform similarity encoding to represent categories of the feature.
options.C: Perform one-hot encoding to represent categories of the feature.
options.D: Perform target encoding to represent categories of the feature.

questions.explanation: The correct solution is B, performing similarity encoding, because it directly addresses the problem of a text feature having thousands of values that differ only by spelling errors. Similarity encoding, often implemented through fuzzy matching or embedding techniques, is designed to identify and group text values that are semantically or phonetically similar, even with minor variations like typos. In Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler, the "Similarity" transform can be used to cluster similar text values, effectively reducing high cardinality caused by misspellings and mapping them to a unified representation. This ensures that variations like "apple," "appel," and "aple" are recognized as the same underlying category, making the feature meaningful and usable for model training. Conversely, the other encoding methods are ill-suited for this specific scenario: A. Ordinal encoding assigns a unique integer to each distinct value. With thousands of misspelled unique values, it would still result in thousands of distinct integers, treating each misspelling as a separate, unrelated category and imposing an arbitrary order that doesn't exist. C. One-hot encoding creates a new binary column for each unique value. Thousands of unique misspelled values would lead to a massive number of columns, causing a severe dimensionality explosion and computational inefficiency, while still failing to group similar text. D. Target encoding replaces each category with the mean of the target variable for that category. While useful for high-cardinality features, it still treats each misspelled variation as a distinct category, failing to correct or consolidate values that are essentially the same due to typos. It doesn't solve the core problem of grouping similar values. Therefore, similarity encoding is the most appropriate method to preprocess a text feature with spelling errors, transforming it into a usable format that captures underlying commonalities for a classification model. Authoritative Links for Further Research: Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler Transforms: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/data- wrangler-transforms.html (Search for "Similarity" within the page for specific details on text similarity transformation.) Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler Overview: https://aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/data-wrangler/

questions.question: An ML engineer is training a text generation model on Amazon SageMaker AI. After several epochs, the loss function does not converge, and the model’s accuracy on the validation dataset starts to show oscillating results. The ML engineer needs to ensure that the model achieves generalization. Which solution will meet this requirement?

options.A: Increase the learning rate and decrease the mini-batch size.
options.B: Increase the learning rate as the number of epochs increases.
options.C: Decrease the learning rate and increase the mini-batch size.
options.D: Decrease the learning rate and decrease the mini-batch size.

questions.explanation: The problem describes a text generation model on Amazon SageMaker AI experiencing non-converging loss and oscillating validation accuracy, indicating instability and poor generalization. The goal is to achieve better generalization. The solution is to decrease the learning rate and increase the mini-batch size (Option C). 1. Decreasing the learning rate: A high learning rate causes the model to take excessively large steps during optimization, leading to overshooting the optimal solution and causing the loss function to oscillate or diverge rather than converge smoothly. Reducing the learning rate allows for smaller, more controlled updates, enabling the model to navigate the loss landscape more precisely and settle into a stable minimum. This directly addresses the non-convergence and oscillation. 2. Increasing the mini-batch size: Small mini-batch sizes result in noisy gradient estimates because each batch provides a high-variance approximation of the true gradient from the entire dataset. This noise can lead to an erratic optimization path, contributing to oscillations in validation accuracy and making it harder for the model to converge robustly. Larger mini-batch sizes provide a more stable and accurate estimate of the gradient, reducing variance in the updates and promoting a smoother training trajectory. 3. Combined effect for Generalization: By combining a decreased learning rate with an increased mini- batch size, the training process becomes significantly more stable and less susceptible to chaotic updates. This allows the model to converge reliably to a more robust and flatter minimum in the loss landscape. Models that converge to flatter minima are generally known to achieve better generalization performance on unseen data, as they are less sensitive to small perturbations in the input. This robust convergence is critical for ensuring the text generation model performs well beyond its training set. Why other options are incorrect: A. Increase the learning rate and decrease the mini-batch size: Both actions would worsen the instability, amplify gradient noise, and further prevent convergence, exacerbating the oscillation. B. Increase the learning rate as the number of epochs increases: This is generally counterproductive. Learning rates are typically decreased over time (learning rate decay) to fine-tune the model as it approaches convergence; increasing it would likely lead to divergence. D. Decrease the learning rate and decrease the mini-batch size: While decreasing the learning rate is beneficial, decreasing the mini-batch size reintroduces gradient noise. Although a very small learning rate might mitigate some noise, a larger batch size is generally more effective at providing stable gradient estimates and promoting smoother, more efficient convergence for generalization. For further reading: An overview of gradient descent optimization algorithms: https://ruder.io/optimizing-gradient-descent/ Understanding the impact of batch size: https://arxiv.org/abs/1804.07612

questions.question: A company uses an NFS-based data store to store data for ML training. Linux-based systems access the data store. The company needs a hybrid system to make the shared data store accessible to on-premises servers and Amazon SageMaker AI notebooks that will consume the data. File locking is required for the data producers. Which AWS storage solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Use an Amazon S3 bucket to store the data. Use Mountpoint for Amazon S3 to mount the S3 bucket to the on-premises servers and the SageMaker AI notebooks.
options.B: Use an Amazon Elastic File System (Amazon EFS) file system to store the data. Mount the file system to the on-premises servers and the SageMaker AI notebooks.
options.C: Use an Amazon FSx for Lustre file system to store the data. Mount the file system to the on-premises servers and the SageMaker AI notebooks.
options.D: Use an Amazon Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS) volume to store the data. Mount the volume to the on- premises servers and the SageMaker AI notebooks.

questions.explanation: The company's requirements specify a shared data store accessible to both on-premises servers and Amazon SageMaker AI notebooks, maintaining compatibility with an existing NFS-based system, and crucially, requiring file locking for data producers. Amazon Elastic File System (EFS) is the optimal solution because it provides a fully managed, scalable, and highly available NFSv4.1 file system. This directly addresses the need to replace an existing NFS-based data store with a cloud-native equivalent that Linux-based systems can access seamlessly. EFS inherently supports POSIX file system semantics, including advisory file locking, which is a critical requirement for data producers to ensure data consistency during concurrent writes. For hybrid accessibility, EFS file systems can be securely accessed from on-premises servers via AWS Direct Connect or a VPN connection, providing a low-latency, private network link. Simultaneously, SageMaker AI notebooks, typically residing within an Amazon Virtual Private Cloud (VPC), can mount the EFS file system directly, allowing them to consume the shared data for training. EFS is designed for shared access across multiple compute instances and services, making it perfect for this distributed ML workflow. Let's evaluate why the other options are not suitable: A. Amazon S3 with Mountpoint for Amazon S3: Amazon S3 is object storage, not a POSIX-compliant file system. While Mountpoint for Amazon S3 allows S3 buckets to be mounted as a local file system, it does not support crucial POSIX features like file locking, which is explicitly required by the problem statement. This makes it unsuitable for data producers relying on file locking. C. Amazon FSx for Lustre: Amazon FSx for Lustre is a high-performance file system optimized for compute- intensive workloads like HPC, not a general-purpose NFS replacement. While it can be shared and mounted by Linux systems, it's not NFSv4.1-based by default and doesn't directly meet the "NFS-based data store" compatibility for a straightforward migration with standard file locking expected from an NFS system without potential compatibility issues for producers. It's often an overkill for the described scenario and primarily targets throughput-intensive use cases rather than general-purpose shared file systems with specific NFS locking requirements. D. Amazon Elastic Block Store (EBS): Amazon EBS provides block storage volumes that can typically be attached to a single Amazon EC2 instance at a time (with specific exceptions like EBS Multi-Attach for limited instances in specific configurations, still requiring a cluster-aware file system on top). It cannot be simultaneously mounted as a shared file system by multiple on-premises servers and SageMaker notebooks directly without significant, complex, and generally unsupported workarounds or additional layers. Therefore, it fails the core requirement for a shared data store. In summary, Amazon EFS is the only solution that fully meets all specified requirements: an NFSv4.1-based shared data store, hybrid accessibility for on-premises and SageMaker, and native support for POSIX file locking. Authoritative Links for further research: Amazon EFS: https://aws.amazon.com/efs/ Accessing EFS from On-Premises: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/efs/latest/ug/network-access- modes.html#connect-to-efs-from-on-premises Mounting EFS to SageMaker: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/at-rest- encryption.html#notebook-instance-efs (indirectly, EFS is typically mounted to the SageMaker notebook instance via its VPC configuration) Mountpoint for Amazon S3 limitations (including file locking): https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/aws/introducing-mountpoint-for-amazon-s3-a-new-open-source-file-client/ (search for "POSIX" or "locking" in its documentation/FAQs)

questions.question: A company needs to analyze a large dataset that is stored in Amazon S3 in Apache Parquet format. The company wants to use one-hot encoding for some of the columns. The company needs a no-code solution to transform the data. The solution must store the transformed data back to the same S3 bucket for model training. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Configure an AWS Glue DataBrew project that connects to the data. Use the DataBrew interactive interface to create a recipe that performs the one-hot encoding transformation. Create a job to apply the transformation and to write the output back to an S3 bucket.
options.B: Configure an AWS Glue Data Catalog table that points to the data. Use Amazon Athena to write SQL commands to perform the one-hot encoding transformation. Configure Athena to write the query results back to an S3 bucket.
options.C: Configure an AWS Glue Data Catalog table that points to the data. Create an AWS Glue ETL interactive notebook. Use the notebook to perform the one-hot encoding transformation. Run the configured cells and write the results back to an S3 bucket.
options.D: Configure an Amazon Redshift cluster to access the data by using Redshift Spectrum. Use SQL commands to perform the one-hot encoding transformation within Amazon Redshift. Configure Amazon Redshift to write the results back to an S3 bucket.

questions.explanation: The correct solution is A. Configure an AWS Glue DataBrew project that connects to the data. Use the DataBrew interactive interface to create a recipe that performs the one-hot encoding transformation. Create a job to apply the transformation and to write the output back to an S3 bucket. Here's a detailed justification: The core requirement is a no-code solution to perform one-hot encoding on a large dataset stored in Amazon S3 in Apache Parquet format, with the transformed data being written back to S3. 1. AWS Glue DataBrew is specifically designed as a visual data preparation tool that enables data analysts and data scientists to clean and normalize data without writing any code. It offers an interactive, point-and-click interface to apply over 250 pre-built transformations, including common machine learning preprocessing steps like one-hot encoding. 2. When a user configures a DataBrew project, they connect it directly to data sources in Amazon S3. The interactive interface allows users to sample the data, visually inspect it, and then select transformations from a comprehensive library to build a "recipe." This recipe is a sequence of transformation steps recorded without writing any code. 3. One-hot encoding is a fundamental preprocessing step for categorical features in machine learning, and DataBrew provides this transformation readily. Users simply select the column(s) for one-hot encoding, and DataBrew handles the underlying logic. 4. Once the recipe is finalized, a DataBrew job can be created to apply this recipe to the entire dataset (which can be very large due to its integration with AWS Glue's scalable infrastructure). The job then writes the transformed output data back to a specified Amazon S3 bucket, fulfilling the requirement for storing data for model training. 5. In contrast, options B, C, and D do not meet the "no-code" requirement: Option B (Amazon Athena) uses SQL, which requires writing code to perform transformations, including one-hot encoding (e.g., using CASE statements or PIVOT operations, depending on complexity). Option C (AWS Glue ETL interactive notebook) explicitly states using a notebook, which typically involves writing Python (PySpark) or Scala code for ETL transformations. Option D (Amazon Redshift cluster with Redshift Spectrum) also relies on SQL commands to perform transformations within Redshift, making it a code-based solution. Therefore, DataBrew stands out as the only solution among the choices that provides a true no-code, visual, and interactive environment for data transformation, perfectly aligning with all the specified requirements for one-hot encoding and S3 integration. Authoritative Links for Further Research: AWS Glue DataBrew: https://aws.amazon.com/databrew/ AWS Glue DataBrew Features: https://aws.amazon.com/databrew/features/ DataBrew Transformations (including one-hot encoding): You can explore the documentation for a comprehensive list, often found within the AWS Glue DataBrew User Guide. A good starting point would be the "Transformations" section in the official AWS Glue DataBrew documentation.

questions.question: A company wants to migrate ML models from an on-premises environment to Amazon SageMaker AI. The models are based on the PyTorch algorithm. The company needs to reuse its existing custom scripts as much as possible on AWS. Which feature of SageMaker AI should the company use to meet these requirements?

options.A: SageMaker AI built-in algorithms
options.B: SageMaker Canvas
options.C: SageMaker JumpStart
options.D: SageMaker AI script mode

questions.explanation: Amazon SageMaker AI script mode is the most suitable feature for migrating existing PyTorch-based ML models while maximizing the reuse of custom scripts. Script mode enables developers to bring their own training and inference scripts written in popular frameworks like PyTorch, TensorFlow, or MXNet directly to SageMaker. This approach significantly simplifies the migration by eliminating the need to build and maintain custom Docker containers. SageMaker provides pre-built deep learning containers for various frameworks, which include the necessary dependencies and environment setup. Users simply point SageMaker to their custom PyTorch training script, and SageMaker handles the provisioning of instances, environment setup, execution of the script, and storage of model artifacts. For instance, when using the SageMaker Python SDK, a PyTorch Estimator is configured with the path to the custom script, along with hyper-parameters and instance configurations. This capability directly addresses the requirement to reuse existing custom scripts as much as possible, as developers only need to make minor adjustments to their PyTorch code, primarily related to input/output paths and argument parsing. The core logic of the training and inference processes remains intact. In contrast, SageMaker built-in algorithms (A) are pre-packaged solutions that accept data in specific formats and do not allow for the execution of arbitrary custom scripts. SageMaker Canvas (B) is a no-code/low-code visual interface designed for business analysts to build models without coding, which is not applicable for migrating custom PyTorch codebases. SageMaker JumpStart (C) offers pre-trained models, notebooks, and solutions for various use cases, aiding rapid deployment or model discovery; however, its primary focus is not on directly executing and migrating existing custom training scripts as efficiently as script mode. Therefore, SageMaker AI script mode is the precise solution that bridges the gap between existing custom code and SageMaker's managed ML platform, facilitating a smooth transition. Authoritative Links: SageMaker Python SDK for PyTorch: https://sagemaker.readthedocs.io/en/stable/frameworks/pytorch/sagemaker.pytorch.html Use your own training scripts with SageMaker: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/training- hackathon-use-your-own.html

questions.question: A company uses an Amazon QuickSight dashboard to track the sale prices of sneakers over time. The dashboard aggregates sale prices scraped from many retail websites. The company wants to determine which prices are unusually high outliers and to display the outliers visually. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Use a vertical bar chart to visualize the outliers. Use a calculated field in QuickSight to take the square roots of the outlier prices to generate the chart. Configure a custom AWS Lambda function to scan the data for anomalies.
options.B: Use AWS Glue DataBrew to preprocess the data. Set the REMOVE_OUTLIERS operation to eliminate data rows that include unusually high prices. Invoke an AWS Lambda function to store the removed rows in Amazon DynamoDB.
options.C: Use a vertical bar chart to visualize the outliers. Use a calculated field in QuickSight to square the outlier prices to generate the chart. Use QuickSight anomaly detection insights to determine which prices are unusually high.
options.D: Use a QuickSight filter to find the lowest 10 values for sneaker price. Assign a specific color to the 10 lowest values.

questions.explanation: Option C provides the most suitable solution for identifying and visually displaying unusually high price outliers within an Amazon QuickSight dashboard. The core requirement is to detect anomalies, specifically high outliers, and then visualize them. Firstly, QuickSight anomaly detection insights are explicitly designed for this purpose. QuickSight uses machine learning algorithms to automatically analyze time-series data, learn normal patterns, and detect unexpected deviations or outliers. This feature directly addresses the need to "determine which prices are unusually high outliers" without requiring external services like AWS Lambda for anomaly detection. Users can configure anomaly detection to look for high-value anomalies, making it ideal for identifying unusually high sneaker prices. Secondly, a vertical bar chart is an appropriate visualization tool for displaying individual data points, including identified outliers. Once QuickSight identifies the anomalous prices, these specific data points can be highlighted or presented distinctly on a bar chart to make their outlier status visually apparent. For instance, the bar representing an outlier could be colored differently, or a reference line could indicate the normal range, clearly showing which prices fall outside. The "calculated field in QuickSight to square the outlier prices to generate the chart" part, while perhaps not universally necessary for every outlier visualization, can serve a purpose. Squaring prices could be a data transformation applied to exaggerate the difference between normal and outlier values on the chart, making the high outliers stand out even more visually, especially if the distribution of prices is skewed or if the visualization needs to emphasize large deviations. It is a manipulation of the visual representation after the outlier has been identified. In contrast, other options are flawed: Option A incorrectly suggests using a custom AWS Lambda function for anomaly scanning, which is redundant given QuickSight's built-in anomaly detection capabilities. Also, taking square roots might compress differences rather than highlight high outliers. Option B is fundamentally incorrect because it suggests using AWS Glue DataBrew to remove outliers. The company wants to determine and display outliers, not eliminate them from the analysis dashboard. Removing them would defeat the purpose of tracking these specific high prices. Option D focuses on finding and coloring the lowest 10 values for sneaker prices, directly contradicting the requirement to find unusually high outliers. Therefore, QuickSight's native anomaly detection coupled with a suitable visualization like a bar chart, potentially enhanced by a calculated field for visual impact, directly addresses all aspects of the problem. Authoritative Links: Amazon QuickSight Anomaly Detection: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/quicksight/latest/user/anomaly- detection.html Building Visualizations in Amazon QuickSight (Bar Charts): https://docs.aws.amazon.com/quicksight/latest/user/creating-a-bar-chart.html

questions.question: An ML engineer is using Amazon QuickSight anomaly detection to detect very high or very low machine operating temperatures compared to normal. The ML engineer sets the Severity parameter to Low and above. The ML engineer sets the Direction parameter to All. What effect will the ML engineer observe in the anomaly detection results if the ML engineer changes the Direction parameter to Lower than expected?

options.A: Increased anomaly identification frequency and increased recall
options.B: Decreased anomaly identification frequency and decreased recall
options.C: Increased anomaly identification frequency and decreased recall
options.D: Decreased anomaly identification frequency and increased recall

questions.explanation: B is correct because restricting Direction to "Lower than expected" filters out all above-normal (high) anomalies, thereby reducing the number of detected anomalies and lowering recall for the full set of true anomalies. Reasoning: By design QuickSight’s Direction parameter constrains the hypothesis space the detector evaluates; setting it to a single tail (lower) limits detection to negative deviations only, so any true positive anomalies that are high-temperature events become systematic false negatives. With Severity unchanged at Low (a permissive threshold) the detector will still flag modest negative deviations, but the net anomaly identification frequency falls because the positive-side anomalies are excluded. Recall (sensitivity) is the fraction of true anomalies that are detected; excluding one entire direction necessarily reduces recall over the true anomaly population that includes both high and low events. Any reduction in false positives among excluded directions does not compensate for the eliminated true positives when the true anomaly distribution is two-tailed, so overall recall decreases. Why the other options are incorrect: A (Increased identification frequency and increased recall): incorrect because constraining to one direction cannot increase the count of detected two-sided true anomalies or their recall; it only removes candidates on the opposite tail. C (Increased identification frequency and decreased recall): incorrect because identification frequency cannot increase when one full class of anomalies (the upper tail) is ignored; frequency can only stay the same or decrease. D (Decreased identification frequency and increased recall): incorrect because recall measures true positives captured out of all true anomalies—removing one side reduces true positives and thus reduces (not increases) recall for a two-sided anomaly set. References:https://docs.aws.amazon.com/quicksight/latest/user/anomaly- detection.htmlhttps://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/cognitive-services/anomaly- detector/overview/overviewhttps://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/model_evaluation.html#precision-and- recall

questions.question: A company runs its ML workflows on an on-premises Kubernetes cluster. The ML workflows include ML services that perform training and inferences for ML models. Each ML service runs from its own standalone Docker image. The company needs to perform a lift and shift from the on-premises Kubernetes cluster to an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (Amazon EKS) cluster. Which solution will meet this requirement with the LEAST operational overhead?

options.A: Redesign the ML services to be configured in Kubeflow. Deploy the new Kubeflow managed ML services to the EKS cluster.
options.B: Upload the Docker images to an Amazon Elastic Container Registry (Amazon ECR) repository. Configure a deployment pipeline to deploy the images to the EKS cluster.
options.C: Migrate the training data to an Amazon Redshift cluster. Retrain the models from the migrated training data by using Amazon Redshift ML. Deploy the retrained models to the EKS cluster.
options.D: Configure an Amazon SageMaker AI notebook. Retrain the models with the same code. Deploy the retrained models to the EKS cluster.

questions.explanation: Option B provides the most straightforward "lift and shift" solution with the LEAST operational overhead because it directly leverages the existing containerization strategy. The company's ML services are already packaged as standalone Docker images. By uploading these Docker images to Amazon Elastic Container Registry (ECR), they gain a secure, scalable, and highly available repository for their container images, seamlessly integrated with AWS services. ECR acts as a direct replacement for any on-premises container registry. Subsequently, deploying these images to an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster involves configuring standard Kubernetes deployment manifests (YAML files) that reference the images in ECR. Since EKS is a managed Kubernetes service, the core Kubernetes APIs and objects are compatible with those used in the on-premises cluster. This means the existing Kubernetes deployment configurations will require minimal, if any, modifications beyond updating image pull references and potentially adjusting for AWS- specific integrations (e.g., load balancers). This approach avoids any redesign or rewrite of the ML services themselves, preserving the existing code, dependencies, and execution environment encapsulated within the Docker images. A deployment pipeline (e.g., using AWS CodePipeline, Jenkins, or GitLab CI) further automates the process of building, pushing, and deploying these images, ensuring consistency and reducing manual effort. This makes it a true lift-and-shift, minimizing the learning curve and re-engineering efforts. In contrast: Option A (Redesign with Kubeflow) requires a significant refactoring of existing ML services to fit the Kubeflow framework. This is a substantial development effort, not a lift and shift, and introduces high operational overhead for adoption and management of a new ML platform layer. Option C (Migrate data to Redshift, Retrain with Redshift ML) involves migrating data and fundamentally changing the training methodology and framework. This would necessitate rewriting the training components of the ML services, leading to a complete re-architecture of the ML pipeline, which is far from a lift and shift and incurs very high operational overhead. Option D (SageMaker AI notebook, Retrain) focuses on retraining models using SageMaker notebooks. While SageMaker is powerful, this option primarily addresses the training aspect and implies a platform shift for training, not a direct migration of existing containerized ML services (which include inference and potentially automated training jobs) as they are. It doesn't preserve the existing Docker-based service structure for operational deployment and would still require adapting the inference components for EKS separately, or shifting them entirely to SageMaker endpoints, which isn't a direct lift of the existing Docker images to EKS. Therefore, Option B directly addresses the requirement of migrating existing containerized ML services to EKS with the least changes and operational burden. Authoritative Links for Further Research: Amazon Elastic Container Registry (ECR): https://aws.amazon.com/ecr/ Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS): https://aws.amazon.com/eks/ Migrating Containerized Applications to AWS EKS: https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/containers/getting- started-with-migrating-containers-to-aws/ AWS Well-Architected Framework - Operational Excellence Lens (includes CI/CD and deployment pipelines): https://docs.aws.amazon.com/wellarchitected/latest/operational-excellence-pillar/operational- excellence.html

questions.question: An ML engineer needs to run intensive model training jobs each month that can take 48 to 72 hours to run. The training jobs can be interrupted and resumed without major issues. The ML engineer has a fixed budget and needs to optimize computing resources. Which solution will meet these requirements MOST cost-effectively?

options.A: Purchase Reserved Instances with a partial upfront payment.
options.B: Purchase On-Demand Instances with no commitment.
options.C: Purchase Amazon SageMaker AI Savings Plans.
options.D: Purchase Spot Instances that use automated checkpoints.

questions.explanation: The most cost-effective solution for intensive, interruptible model training jobs, occurring monthly with a fixed budget, is purchasing Spot Instances combined with automated checkpoints. Spot Instances allow ML engineers to bid on unused AWS compute capacity, offering significant cost savings of up to 90% compared to On-Demand prices. This directly addresses the requirement for optimizing computing resources within a fixed budget. The critical component making Spot Instances viable for these long-running jobs (48-72 hours) is the implementation of automated checkpoints. Checkpointing enables the training process to periodically save its progress and model state. If a Spot Instance is reclaimed by AWS due to a capacity need, the job can gracefully stop, and upon a new Spot Instance becoming available, it can resume training from the last saved checkpoint without major loss of progress or issues. This perfectly aligns with the job's characteristic of being interruptible and resumable. In contrast, purchasing On-Demand Instances (Option B) would be the most expensive option, failing to optimize computing resources for a fixed budget, especially for a workload that explicitly tolerates interruptions. Reserved Instances (Option A), while offering cost savings compared to On-Demand, require a 1- year or 3-year commitment and are less flexible for workloads that might fluctuate or are explicitly interruptible. They do not provide the same deep discounts as Spot Instances for suitable workloads. Amazon SageMaker AI Savings Plans (Option C) offer discounts on SageMaker usage for a committed spend, which can be beneficial for consistent, long-term workloads. However, for a specific scenario focused on maximizing cost savings for interruptible compute, Spot Instances often provide a more substantial discount than Savings Plans alone, particularly when the workload can leverage the transient nature of Spot capacity effectively. Therefore, the combination of deeply discounted Spot Instances and robust checkpointing provides the optimal balance of cost efficiency and operational resilience for this specific use case. Authoritative Links for Further Research: AWS Spot Instances: https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/spot/ Training with Amazon EC2 Spot Instances (AWS Machine Learning Blog): https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/train-machine-learning-models-with-amazon-ec2-spot- instances/ Amazon EC2 Pricing (On-Demand vs. Spot): https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/pricing/ AWS Savings Plans: https://aws.amazon.com/savingsplans/

questions.question: An ML engineer needs to use Amazon SageMaker hyperparameter tuning to reduce the training time for an ML model. Select and order the correct steps from the following list to meet this requirement. Each step should be selected one time or not at all. (Select and order three.) •Choose Bayesian optimization and increase the number of parameters. •Choose Hyperband tuning and decrease the number of parameters. •Choose random search and use a random seed of -1. •Deploy the model to a SageMaker endpoint. •Evaluate the change in training time. •Retrain the model.
options: Deploy the model to a SageMaker endpoint.
Evaluate the change in training time.

questions.question: A company develops an ML model to classify products. The model uses textual data and visual data to classify the products into a hierarchical taxonomy. An ML engineer must use specific strategies to enhance the model’s accuracy and handle class imbalances. Select the correct strategy from the following list for each use case. Select each strategy one time. (Choose five.) •Categorical cross-entropy •Fallback metric •Hierarchical loss •General oversampling •Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) for text
options: Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) for text

questions.question: A retail company is creating an AI-powered assistant for customers. The company has a large body of documentation that the assistant needs to use for general inquiries. The company wants any responses about prices to use only documentation that is less than 1 month old. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Use Amazon Q Business to develop the responses. Configure a document attribute filter so that responses about prices use only the documents from the past month.
options.B: Use Amazon Q Business to develop the responses. Configure the source attribution citation so that responses about prices use only the documents from the past month.
options.C: Segment the documents into folders based on the month of document creation. Configure Amazon Q Developer to use only the documents from the past month to develop responses about prices.
options.D: Segment the documents into folders based on the month of document creation. Grant the assistant access to only the documents from the past month for responses about prices. Use Amazon Q Developer to develop the responses.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is A because Amazon Q Business is purpose-built for creating generative AI assistants that interact with enterprise data, providing accurate and contextual responses. A key capability of Amazon Q Business is its ability to ingest data from various sources and index it with associated metadata, known as document attributes. These attributes can include information like the document creation date, last modified date, department, or content type. For the requirement of using only documentation less than 1 month old for price inquiries, Amazon Q Business allows configuring document attribute filters. This feature enables developers to specify conditions on the metadata during query time. In this scenario, a filter can be applied to restrict the retrieval of documents for price-related questions to only those where the 'creation_date' or 'last_modified_date' attribute falls within the last month. This ensures that the AI assistant prioritizes the most current pricing information while still using the broader knowledge base for general inquiries without such a strict age constraint. This method is dynamic, scalable, and leverages the inherent capabilities of Amazon Q's underlying intelligent search and retrieval mechanisms. Option B is incorrect because source attribution citation in Amazon Q Business is about showing the user which documents were used to generate a response, not about filtering which documents are considered during the retrieval phase. It's a post-generation display feature, not a pre-retrieval filtering mechanism. Options C and D are incorrect for several reasons. First, manually segmenting documents into folders based on creation month is a cumbersome, unscalable, and inefficient approach compared to using metadata. It creates significant management overhead. Second, Amazon Q Developer is primarily designed to assist developers with coding, debugging, and general development tasks, not typically for customer-facing knowledge base assistants. Amazon Q Business is the dedicated service for enterprise knowledge and customer interaction. Furthermore, granting access to folders would require complex routing logic to determine when a "price" query is made and dynamically switch access, which is not how Amazon Q efficiently handles content filtering based on query intent. In summary, Amazon Q Business with its document attribute filtering capability provides an elegant, scalable, and intelligent solution for enforcing specific data recency requirements for different types of queries within a single knowledge base. Authoritative Links: Amazon Q Business Overview: https://aws.amazon.com/q/business/ Working with Document Attributes in Amazon Q: While Amazon Q Business is a higher-level service, its underlying indexing capabilities (similar to Amazon Kendra) support document attributes and filtering. For more on metadata filtering concepts relevant to Q: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kendra/latest/dg/searching- with-filtering.html Amazon Q Developer Overview: https://aws.amazon.com/q/developer/

questions.question: An ML engineer needs to automate the rebuild and redeployment of an ML model. Updates will occur when changes are made to the model’s code base. The ML engineer must use AWS services to configure a continuous integration and continuous delivery (CI/CD) pipeline for the rebuild and redeployment. Select and order the steps from the following list to configure the CI/CD pipeline. Each step should be selected one time. (Select and order three.) •Invoke Amazon SageMaker Pipelines to run all steps required for model training and deployment. •Create a pipeline in AWS CodePipeline. Build and test containers in AWS CodeBuild. •Create a Git source code repository.
options: Invoke Amazon SageMaker Pipelines to run all steps required for model training and deployment.
Create a pipeline in AWS CodePipeline. Build and test containers in AWS CodeBuild.
Create a Git source code repository.

questions.question: An airline company deploys ML models to one dozen Amazon SageMaker AI inference endpoints. The inference endpoints must be able to handle different types of workloads in a cost-effective way. Select the correct inference option from the following list to handle each type of workload. Select each inference option one time. (Choose four.) •Asynchronous inference •Batch inference •Real-time inference •Serverless inference

A company uses Amazon SageMaker AI to support ML workflows such as model training and deployment. Select the correct registry from the following list to meet the requirements for each use case with the LEAST operational overhead. Each registry should be selected one or more times. (Choose four.)

A travel company wants to create an ML model to recommend the next airport destination for its users. The company has collected millions of data records about user location, recent search history on the company’s website, and 2,000 available airports. The data has several categorical features with a target column that is expected to have a high-dimensional sparse matrix. The company needs to use Amazon SageMaker AI built-in algorithms for the model. An ML engineer converts the categorical features by using one-hot encoding. Which algorithm should the ML engineer implement to meet these requirements?

options.A: Use the CatBoost algorithm to recommend the next airport destination.
options.B: Use the DeepAR forecasting algorithm to recommend the next airport destination.
options.C: Use the Factorization Machines algorithm to recommend the next airport destination.
options.D: Use the k-means algorithm to cluster users into groups. Map each group to the next airport destination based on user search history.

questions.explanation: The problem describes a recommendation system task where the goal is to predict the next airport destination. Key characteristics include the presence of categorical features, which are converted using one- hot encoding, leading to a high-dimensional sparse matrix for the target. The company explicitly requires using Amazon SageMaker AI built-in algorithms. Factorization Machines (FM) are particularly well-suited for recommendation systems, especially when dealing with high-dimensional and sparse datasets. The conversion of categorical features like user location and search history, along with 2,000 possible airport destinations, into one-hot encoded vectors will inevitably result in an extremely wide and sparse feature matrix. This sparsity is a natural challenge for many algorithms, but it is precisely where FMs excel. FMs can effectively model pairwise feature interactions, even when these interactions are very sparse or have never been directly observed in the training data. They achieve this by factorizing the interaction matrix into latent vectors, allowing the model to learn underlying relationships and make predictions based on shared latent factors. For instance, an FM can infer that users interested in certain types of destinations from specific locations might also be interested in other related airports, even if that exact user-airport combination hasn't been explicitly observed. This ability to generalize from sparse interactions is crucial for making personalized recommendations among a large number of options. Furthermore, Factorization Machines are available as a built-in algorithm in Amazon SageMaker, directly meeting the company's requirement. This makes them a highly efficient and scalable choice for processing millions of data records. In contrast: CatBoost is an excellent gradient boosting algorithm for tabular data, especially with categorical features, but it often works best when these features are handled internally or through less aggressive encoding than one-hot. It might struggle with the extreme sparsity and dimensionality after extensive one-hot encoding in a recommender system context compared to FMs. DeepAR is designed for time series forecasting, predicting future values based on historical time series data. While user search history has a temporal aspect, the core problem is a recommendation task, not a traditional time series prediction of a numerical value. k-means is a clustering algorithm. While clustering users can be part of a recommendation strategy, it doesn't directly address the problem of predicting a specific destination from a high-dimensional sparse interaction matrix. It would require additional logic built on top of the clustering to generate recommendations, and it wouldn't inherently leverage sparse interactions between features as effectively as FMs. Therefore, Factorization Machines provide the most direct, efficient, and appropriate solution for building a recommendation model with high-dimensional sparse data resulting from one-hot encoded categorical features, adhering to the Amazon SageMaker built-in algorithms constraint. Authoritative Links for Further Research: Amazon SageMaker Factorization Machines Algorithm: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/fact-machines.html Factorization Machines (original paper by Steffen Rendle): https://www.csie.ntu.edu.tw/~b97053/paper/Rendle2010FM.pdf

questions.question: An ML engineer is configuring auto scaling for an inference component of a model that runs behind an Amazon SageMaker AI endpoint. The ML engineer configures SageMaker AI auto scaling with a target tracking scaling policy set to 100 invocations per model per minute. The SageMaker AI endpoint scales appropriately during normal business hours. However, the ML engineer notices that at the start of each business day, there are zero instances available to handle requests, which causes delays in processing. The ML engineer must ensure that the SageMaker AI endpoint can handle incoming requests at the start of each business day. Which solution will meet this requirement?

options.A: Reduce the SageMaker AI auto scaling cooldown period to the minimum supported value. Add an auto scaling lifecycle hook to scale the SageMaker AI instances.
options.B: Change the target metric to CPU utilization.
options.C: Modify the scaling policy target value to one.
options.D: Apply a step scaling policy that scales based on an Amazon CloudWatch alarm. Apply a second CloudWatch alarm and scaling policy to scale the minimum number of instances from zero to one at the start of each business day.

questions.explanation: The core problem described is a "cold start" scenario: the Amazon SageMaker AI endpoint scales down to zero instances, and the existing target tracking policy, while effective during normal operations, fails to provision instances proactively at the start of the business day. A target tracking policy based on invocations is reactive; if there are zero instances, there are no invocations to trigger the scaling-up process, leading to delays. Here's a detailed justification for why option D is the correct solution: 1. Understanding the Failure of the Current Setup: The existing target tracking policy (100 invocations per model per minute) works by reacting to actual invocation load. If the endpoint scales down to zero instances overnight, there will be zero invocations at the start of the day. This creates a catch-22: no instances mean no invocations, and no invocations mean no trigger for the scaling policy to provision instances, causing the initial delay. 2. Addressing the Problem with Option D: Option D proposes a two-pronged approach that effectively solves this cold start issue: "Apply a step scaling policy that scales based on an Amazon CloudWatch alarm." A step scaling policy provides flexible scaling actions based on thresholds of a CloudWatch metric. While this can enhance reactive scaling during high load, its primary contribution to solving the cold start problem, in conjunction with the second part of the solution, is its ability to be triggered by various alarm types, including time-based alarms. "Apply a second CloudWatch alarm and scaling policy to scale the minimum number of instances from zero to one at the start of each business day." This is the crucial component. An Amazon CloudWatch alarm can be configured to trigger based on a schedule (similar to a cron job) at a specific time each day, corresponding to the start of business hours. When this alarm fires, it can trigger a scaling policy that explicitly increases the minimum capacity of the SageMaker endpoint's auto scaling group from zero to one (or more). This proactively ensures that at least one instance is provisioned and warmed up before the first requests arrive, eliminating the cold start delay. This method guarantees baseline availability at predictable times. 3. Why Other Options Are Incorrect: A. Reduce cooldown period, add lifecycle hook: Reducing the cooldown period primarily affects how quickly instances are terminated or new ones are launched after a scaling event. Lifecycle hooks allow for custom actions during instance state transitions. Neither of these mechanisms inherently solves the problem of scaling from zero instances proactively at a scheduled time. If there are no instances, there's no lifecycle to hook into. B. Change the target metric to CPU utilization: If there are zero instances, there will be zero CPU utilization. This change would not resolve the cold start problem, as it's still a reactive metric that requires instances to be running. C. Modify the scaling policy target value to one: Setting the target value to one invocation per minute still requires at least one invocation to trigger scaling. If there are zero instances, there will be zero invocations, and the problem persists. By combining scheduled CloudWatch alarms with scaling policies to adjust the minimum capacity, option D provides a robust and common solution for ensuring service availability at predictable times, preventing delays caused by cold starts. Authoritative Links for Further Research: SageMaker Automatic Scaling: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/endpoint-auto- scaling.html Target Tracking Scaling Policies: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/autoscaling/ec2/userguide/as-scaling- target-tracking.html Step Scaling Policies: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/autoscaling/ec2/userguide/as-scaling-step-policies.html Scheduled Scaling: While the SageMaker console for endpoint auto-scaling directly supports scheduled scaling, the underlying mechanism often involves CloudWatch events/alarms triggering scaling actions. For general EC2 Auto Scaling, scheduled scaling is explicit: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/autoscaling/ec2/userguide/schedule_future_scaling.html

questions.question: An ML engineer is using an Amazon SageMaker Studio notebook to train a neural network by creating an estimator. The estimator runs a Python training script that uses Distributed Data Parallel (DDP) on a single instance that has more than one GPU. The ML engineer discovers that the training script is underutilizing GPU resources. The ML engineer must identify the point in the training script where resource utilization can be optimized. Which solution will meet this requirement?

options.A: Use Amazon CloudWatch metrics to create a report that describes GPU utilization over time.
options.B: Add SageMaker Profiler annotations to the training script. Run the script and generate a report from the results.
options.C: Use AWS CloudTrail to create a report that describes GPU utilization and GPU memory utilization over time.
options.D: Create a default monitor in Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor and suggest a baseline. Generate a report based on the constraints and statistics the monitor generates.

questions.explanation: The most effective solution to identify the point in a training script where GPU resource utilization can be optimized, especially in an Amazon SageMaker Studio notebook environment using Distributed Data Parallel (DDP) on a multi-GPU instance, is to use SageMaker Profiler annotations. Justification for Option B: Amazon SageMaker Profiler is specifically designed to detect and diagnose resource bottlenecks during the training of machine learning models. It collects system-level metrics such as CPU, GPU, I/O, and network utilization, alongside framework-level metrics (e.g., PyTorch, TensorFlow). The key feature relevant here is the ability to add annotations to the training script. By instrumenting specific sections of the Python training script (e.g., data loading, forward pass, backward pass, optimizer steps, DDP synchronization points), the ML engineer can precisely measure the time spent in each operation. After running the annotated script, SageMaker Profiler generates a comprehensive report that visualizes these metrics and annotated sections. This report clearly highlights where time is being spent inefficiently, where GPUs might be idle awaiting data or computations, or where specific operations are causing bottlenecks. This granular insight directly addresses the requirement to "identify the point in the training script" for optimization. For instance, if the report shows high GPU idle time during a data loading phase, it indicates a data pipeline bottleneck. If the backward pass takes disproportionately long, it might point to a complex gradient computation. Why other options are incorrect: A. Use Amazon CloudWatch metrics to create a report that describes GPU utilization over time: While CloudWatch provides overall GPU utilization metrics, it offers only a high-level view. It can tell you that GPUs are underutilized, but it cannot pinpoint which specific line or section of your training script is causing the underutilization. It lacks the necessary granularity for script-level optimization. C. Use AWS CloudTrail to create a report that describes GPU utilization and GPU memory utilization over time: AWS CloudTrail is an auditing and logging service that records API calls and events made in your AWS account. It is used for governance, compliance, and operational auditing, not for monitoring real-time resource utilization or profiling training job performance. This option is fundamentally misaligned with the problem statement. D. Create a default monitor in Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor and suggest a baseline. Generate a report based on the constraints and statistics the monitor generates: Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor is designed to detect data drift, model quality drift, and bias in deployed machine learning models during their inference phase. It is entirely irrelevant to optimizing a training script during the training phase. Therefore, SageMaker Profiler with its annotation capability is the purpose-built and most effective tool to gain the deep, script-specific insights needed to optimize GPU resource utilization in this scenario. Authoritative Links: Amazon SageMaker Profiler: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/train-debugger.html Debug and Profile Training Jobs with SageMaker Debugger (includes Profiler): https://aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/debugger/ Amazon CloudWatch: https://aws.amazon.com/cloudwatch/ AWS CloudTrail: https://aws.amazon.com/cloudtrail/ Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor: https://aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/model-monitor/

questions.question: An ML engineer at an insurance company trains a regression model to predict the number of insurance policy sales each month. After training the model, the ML engineer uses Amazon SageMaker AI to deploy the model for inference. The ML engineer wants to monitor the model predictions to detect whether the production data distribution differs from the training data distribution when there are changes in customer behaviors. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Determine whether there is drift in the data quality.
options.B: Determine whether there is drift in the model quality.
options.C: Determine whether there is drift in the model bias.
options.D: Determine whether there is drift in the feature attribution.

questions.explanation: The correct solution is to determine whether there is drift in the data quality because this directly addresses the requirement of detecting if the production data distribution differs from the training data distribution. This phenomenon is broadly known as data drift. Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor offers robust capabilities to detect various types of drift. Its "Data Quality" monitoring feature is specifically designed to analyze the statistical properties and constraints of incoming inference data and compare them against a baseline established from the training dataset. This baseline captures the expected distribution, ranges, and types of features seen during model training. When an ML engineer monitors for data quality drift, SageMaker Model Monitor calculates statistics (like mean, median, standard deviation for numerical features, or frequency counts for categorical features) and checks for constraint violations in the real-time or batch inference data. It then compares these against the baseline statistics and constraints. Significant deviations indicate that the distribution of the production input data has changed relative to the data the model was trained on. In the given scenario, "changes in customer behaviors" would manifest as shifts in the input features provided to the regression model (e.g., changes in demographic patterns, purchase history, or other predictors). These shifts would alter the statistical distribution of the production data, which data quality monitoring is perfectly suited to detect. Other options are not the primary solution for this specific problem: B. Model Quality drift involves monitoring performance metrics (e.g., RMSE for regression) by comparing predictions against actual ground truth labels, which are often delayed or unavailable at inference time. While data drift can cause model quality degradation, the requirement specifically asks to monitor data distribution changes, not direct model performance degradation. C. Model Bias drift focuses on fairness, checking for disparities in model predictions or errors across different sensitive groups. This is a crucial aspect of responsible AI but does not directly address the general deviation of overall data distribution. D. Feature Attribution drift monitors how the importance or influence of different features on the model's predictions changes over time. While related to features, it doesn't directly measure the distributional shifts of the features themselves, but rather how the model's internal logic might be shifting or if different features are becoming more or less influential. Therefore, "Data Quality" monitoring is the most direct and appropriate mechanism within Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor to detect when the production data distribution diverges from the training data distribution, indicating changes in customer behavior have impacted the input data. Authoritative Links for Further Research: Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor – Detect Drift: https://aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/model-monitor/ Monitor Models for Data and Model Quality, Bias, and Explainability: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model-monitor.html Analyze Monitoring Results: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model-monitor-analyze.html

questions.question: A company is developing an internal cost-estimation tool that uses an ML model in Amazon SageMaker AI. Users upload high-resolution images to the tool. The model must process each image and predict the cost of the object in the image. The model also must notify the user when processing is complete. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Store the images in an Amazon S3 bucket. Deploy the model on SageMaker AI. Use batch transform jobs for model inference. Use an Amazon Simple Queue Service (Amazon SQS) queue to notify users.
options.B: Store the images in an Amazon S3 bucket. Deploy the model on SageMaker AI. Use an asynchronous inference strategy for model inference. Use an Amazon Simple Notification Service (Amazon SNS) topic to notify users.
options.C: Store the images in an Amazon Elastic File System (Amazon EFS) file system. Deploy the model on SageMaker AI. Use batch transform jobs for model inference. Use an Amazon Simple Queue Service (Amazon SQS) queue to notify users.
options.D: Store the images in an Amazon Elastic File System (Amazon EFS) file system. Deploy the model on SageMaker AI. Use an asynchronous inference strategy for model inference. Use an Amazon Simple Notification Service (Amazon SNS) topic to notify users.

questions.explanation: The problem describes a scenario where users upload high-resolution images to an internal tool. An ML model in Amazon SageMaker must process each image to predict a cost, and then notify the user upon completion. This requires handling large input files, potentially long processing times per image, and a mechanism for individual user notification. Let's break down why Option B is the optimal solution: 1. Image Storage (Amazon S3 bucket): High-resolution images are typically large files. Amazon S3 is a highly scalable, durable, and cost-effective object storage service perfect for storing vast amounts of unstructured data like images. SageMaker services are deeply integrated with S3, making it the natural choice for input data storage. Using Amazon EFS (as in options C and D) for this purpose would be less suitable; EFS is a shared file system more geared towards scenarios requiring POSIX file system access across multiple compute instances, and generally incurs higher costs and complexity for simple object storage compared to S3. 2. Model Deployment (SageMaker AI): The question explicitly states that the ML model is in Amazon SageMaker AI, so deploying it on SageMaker is a given and consistent across all options. 3. Inference Strategy (Asynchronous Inference): This is a critical component. High-resolution images imply potentially long processing times for the ML model. Asynchronous inference (Amazon SageMaker Asynchronous Inference) is specifically designed for scenarios involving large payloads (up to 1 GB), long processing times (up to 15 minutes), and requests that do not require an immediate, synchronous response. Users upload their images to S3, trigger an asynchronous inference request, and don't need to wait for the result. SageMaker handles queuing, processing, and storing the output in S3. Batch transform jobs (as in options A and C) are typically used for processing a large dataset (a batch) at once, often for offline predictions where individual real-time responses aren't required. While it can process files from S3, it's less suited for individual uploads requiring per-item notifications upon completion of each request. The user would submit a collection of images and get results for the entire batch, not necessarily an individual notification for each image as it completes. 4. User Notification (Amazon Simple Notification Service - SNS topic): After an asynchronous inference job completes, SageMaker can publish completion events to a specified SNS topic or SQS queue. Amazon SNS is a managed messaging service that supports publishing messages to various subscribers, including email, SMS, mobile push notifications, HTTP/S endpoints, and other AWS services. For direct "user notification," SNS is ideal because it allows for immediate push-based delivery to multiple types of endpoints a user might subscribe to (e.g., email or SMS). Amazon SQS (as in options A and C) is a message queuing service primarily used for decoupling application components, where consumers poll the queue for messages. While it could be used, it would require an additional component to poll SQS and then push notifications to the user. SNS provides a more direct and efficient mechanism for "notifying the user." In summary, Option B leverages S3 for scalable storage, SageMaker Asynchronous Inference for handling large image payloads and long processing times efficiently without blocking the user, and SNS for directly pushing completion notifications to users. This combination perfectly addresses all stated requirements. Authoritative Links: Amazon SageMaker Asynchronous Inference: https://aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/model- hosting/asynchronous-inference/ Amazon S3: https://aws.amazon.com/s3/ Amazon SNS: https://aws.amazon.com/sns/

questions.question: A healthcare company uses an Amazon SageMaker AI endpoint to host a model that predicts patient readmission risk to hospitals. The company wants to predict patient readmissions with high accuracy and is willing to tolerate false positives. The current model performance has degraded over the previous year. The company trains and deploys a new model as a shadow variant for testing on live traffic from hospitals. The company monitors the performance of the new model for a month. During the month of testing, the shadow variant has a higher recall than the existing model but has a lower precision. What should the company do next?

options.A: Promote the shadow variant to full production.
options.B: Extend the shadow testing period to capture more data. Monitor the new model to determine whether precision improves.
options.C: Use a blue/green deployment strategy to allocate a small percentage of traffic to the shadow variant to reduce model errors.
options.D: Disable the shadow variant and roll back to the main variant.

questions.explanation: The company's primary objective is to predict patient readmissions with "high accuracy" and a stated willingness to "tolerate false positives." This preference indicates a higher priority for minimizing false negatives (missing actual readmissions) over minimizing false positives (incorrectly flagging a patient as high risk). In machine learning terms, this translates to prioritizing recall over precision when there's a trade-off. The new model, deployed as a shadow variant, has been tested for a month on live traffic. Its performance shows a higher recall than the existing model. This means the new model is more effective at identifying patients who will actually be readmitted, thus reducing dangerous false negatives. While it has lower precision, implying more false positives, the company has explicitly stated its willingness to tolerate these. Given that the existing model's performance has degraded, and the new model demonstrably aligns better with the company's stated priorities (high recall, tolerance for false positives), promoting the shadow variant to full production is the logical next step. The month-long monitoring period on live traffic provides sufficient evidence for this decision, as the key performance characteristics are clear and align with the business goal. Option A (Promote the shadow variant to full production): This aligns directly with the company's stated preference for high recall and tolerance for false positives. The shadow testing has confirmed the new model's superiority in the desired metric. Option B (Extend the shadow testing period...): Extending the testing period is unlikely to fundamentally change the model's precision unless the data distribution changes or the model itself is retrained/re-tuned. The current performance characteristics are known and acceptable given the company's requirements. Option C (Use a blue/green deployment strategy...): Blue/green or canary deployments are strategies for how to roll out a model, not which model to choose. The decision to deploy the new model has already been implicitly made by evaluating its performance. A shadow deployment is a form of A/B testing where the new variant processes 100% of live traffic without affecting responses, which is a robust way to evaluate before a full rollout. Option D (Disable the shadow variant and roll back to the main variant): The main variant's performance has degraded, making this an unacceptable option. The new model offers improved performance in the critical area of recall, which is essential for patient safety. Therefore, promoting the new model is the correct action as it directly addresses the company's needs and observed performance. Authoritative Links for further research: 1. Understanding Precision and Recall: Google's Machine Learning Crash Course provides an excellent foundational explanation: https://developers.google.com/machine-learning/crash- course/classification/precision-and-recall 2. A/B Testing and Shadow Deployments in AWS SageMaker: AWS blogs often describe these deployment strategies: https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/a-b-testing-ml-models-in- amazon-sagemaker-endpoints/

questions.question: A company is using Amazon SageMaker to deploy a new version of its ML model. Select the correct SageMaker traffic shifting strategy from the following list for each use case. Each traffic shifting strategy should be selected one time. (Choose three.) •All at once traffic shifting •Canary traffic shifting •Linear traffic shifting

A company needs to combine data from multiple sources. The company must use Amazon Redshift Serverless to query an AWS Glue Data Catalog database and underlying data that is stored in an Amazon S3 bucket. Select and order the correct steps from the following list to meet these requirements. Select each step one time or not at all. (Select and order three.) •Attach the IAM role to the Redshift cluster. •Attach the IAM role to the Redshift namespace. •Create an external database in Amazon Redshift to point to the Data Catalog schema. •Create an external schema in Amazon Redshift to point to the Data Catalog database. •Create an IAM role for Amazon Redshift to use to access only the S3 bucket that contains underlying data. •Create an IAM role for Amazon Redshift to use to access the Data Catalog and the S3 bucket that contains underlying data.
options: Attach the IAM role to the Redshift cluster.
Attach the IAM role to the Redshift namespace.
Create an external database in Amazon Redshift to point to the Data Catalog schema.
Create an external schema in Amazon Redshift to point to the Data Catalog database.
Create an IAM role for Amazon Redshift to use to access only the S3 bucket that contains underlying data.
Create an IAM role for Amazon Redshift to use to access the Data Catalog and the S3 bucket that contains underlying data.

questions.question: An airline company uses an ML model to adjust ticket prices based on demand. The model runs on Amazon SageMaker real-time endpoints. During previous deployments, the model failed to scale quickly enough when website traffic increased, which caused delays in price adjustments. An ML engineer needs to configure auto scaling for the SageMaker endpoints to respond rapidly to traffic changes. The solution must use target tracking scaling policies. Which configuration will be MOST responsive to sudden changes in traffic?

options.A: Configure auto scaling based on the SageMaker AI InvocationsPerInstance standard metric. Configure 10- second interval resolution, and set the default 300-second scale-in cooldown period.
options.B: Configure auto scaling based on the SageMaker AI InvocationsPerInstance metric. Configure high-resolution 10-second intervals, and set a 600-second scale-in cooldown period.
options.C: Configure auto scaling based on the SageMaker InvocationsPerInstance standard metric. Configure 10- second intervals resolution, and set a 600-second scale-in cooldown period.
options.D: Configure auto scaling based on the SageMaker InvocationsPerInstance metric. Configure high-resolution 10- second intervals, and set the default 300-second scale-in cooldown period.

questions.explanation: To ensure the ML model on Amazon SageMaker real-time endpoints responds rapidly to sudden traffic changes, especially increases, auto scaling needs to be configured optimally. The solution requires using target tracking scaling policies. Here's a detailed breakdown of why option B is the most responsive configuration: 1. Metric Selection: All options correctly identify SageMaker InvocationsPerInstance (or SageMaker AI InvocationsPerInstance, which refers to the same underlying metric) as the basis for auto scaling. This metric is ideal for adjusting endpoint capacity based on the per-instance request load, ensuring that as demand increases, new instances are added to maintain a target load. 2. Metric Resolution: The responsiveness of auto scaling is heavily dependent on how quickly changes in the metric can be detected. High-resolution 10-second intervals (Options B and D) are crucial for rapid detection. Standard CloudWatch metrics typically have a 1-minute resolution. Enabling "high-resolution" metrics allows data points to be published at finer granularities, down to 1-second or 10-second intervals. This means the auto scaling policy can evaluate traffic changes and initiate scaling actions much faster than with standard 1-minute resolution, which is essential for responding rapidly to sudden spikes in website traffic. Options A and C, which mention "10-second interval resolution" without specifying "high-resolution," might imply standard 1-minute resolution collected every 10 seconds or simply lack the explicit optimization for rapid detection that "high-resolution" provides. Therefore, high-resolution metrics (B and D) are superior for responsiveness. 3. Scale-in Cooldown Period: This parameter dictates how long Application Auto Scaling waits after a scale-in activity completes before initiating another scale-in activity. A longer scale-in cooldown period (600 seconds, or 10 minutes, in Options B and C) is beneficial for maintaining responsiveness in environments with volatile traffic. When traffic surges, instances are scaled out. If traffic temporarily dips but is likely to surge again soon (as is common for e-commerce or airline websites), a longer cooldown prevents instances from being terminated prematurely. By keeping the scaled-out instances provisioned for a longer duration, the system remains ready to handle subsequent rapid traffic increases without incurring the latency of provisioning new instances from scratch. This significantly contributes to the overall responsiveness and availability of the service during fluctuating demand. A shorter, default 300-second (5-minute) cooldown (Options A and D) risks prematurely scaling in, potentially leading to performance degradation and delays if another traffic spike occurs shortly after. Combining these factors: Options A and C are less optimal due to their potentially lower metric resolution compared to "high- resolution." Between Options B and D, both offer the crucial "high-resolution 10-second intervals." However, Option B's 600-second scale-in cooldown period provides a strategic advantage for responsiveness. It ensures that once capacity is scaled out to meet high demand, it is retained for a longer period, preparing the system for subsequent, rapid traffic surges and minimizing potential delays from re-provisioning. This longer cooldown period is a key differentiator for maintaining readiness and responding "MOST rapidly" to continuous or fluctuating high traffic. Therefore, Option B provides the optimal combination of rapid metric detection and sustained capacity readiness to be MOST responsive to sudden traffic changes. Authoritative Links for Further Research: Scale Amazon SageMaker Models: This AWS documentation explains how to configure auto scaling for SageMaker endpoints, including the metrics and parameters involved.https://docs.aws.amazon/sagemaker/latest/dg/sagemaker-auto-scaling.html CloudWatch Metrics for Amazon SageMaker: Details the available metrics for SageMaker endpoints, including InvocationsPerInstance.https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/monitoring- cloudwatch.html High-Resolution Metrics: Explains the concept of high-resolution metrics in CloudWatch and their benefits for faster monitoring and scaling.https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/monitoring/cloudwatch_metrics.html#HighResolutionMetrics Target Tracking Scaling Policies for Application Auto Scaling: While often shown for EC2 Auto Scaling, the principles of target tracking, cooldown periods, and their impact on responsiveness apply broadly across AWS services using Application Auto Scaling.https://docs.aws.amazon.com/autoscaling/application/userguide/application-auto-scaling-target- tracking.html
