questions.question: An ML engineer is collecting data to train a classification ML model by using Amazon SageMaker AI. The target column can have two possible values: Class A or Class B. The ML engineer wants to ensure that the number of samples for both Class A and Class B are balanced, without losing any existing training data. The ML engineer must test the balance of the training data. Which solution will meet this requirement?

options.A: Use SageMaker Clarify to check for class imbalance (CI). If the value is equal to 0, then use random undersampling in SageMaker Data Wrangler to balance the classes.
options.B: Use SageMaker Clarify to check for class imbalance (CI). If the value is greater than 0, then use synthetic minority oversampling technique (SMOTE) in SageMaker Data Wrangler to balance the classes.
options.C: Use SageMaker JumpStart to generate a class imbalance (CI) report. If the value is greater than 0, then use random undersampling in SageMaker Studio to balance the classes.
options.D: Use SageMaker JumpStart to generate a class imbalance (CI) report. If the value is equal to 0, then use synthetic minority oversampling technique (SMOTE) in SageMaker Studio to balance the classes.

questions.explanation: Option B is the correct solution because it accurately identifies the appropriate AWS services and techniques to address class imbalance without discarding valuable training data. First, Amazon SageMaker Clarify is the dedicated service within SageMaker designed for bias detection, including the detection of class imbalance in datasets. It provides metrics like Class Imbalance (CI) that quantify the disparity between the number of samples for different classes. Therefore, using SageMaker Clarify to check for CI is the most direct and recommended approach for this task. Second, the condition "If the value is greater than 0" correctly indicates that an imbalance exists and requires intervention. A CI value of 0 would mean the classes are already perfectly balanced, rendering further action unnecessary. Third, the core requirement is to balance classes without losing any existing training data. This is where Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE) becomes crucial. SMOTE addresses class imbalance by creating synthetic examples for the minority class, rather than simply duplicating existing minority samples. It generates new data points by interpolating between existing minority class instances, effectively increasing the representation of the underrepresented class without removing any data from the majority class. This directly fulfills the constraint of not losing existing training data, a significant advantage over methods like random undersampling, which discard majority class samples. Finally, Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler is an excellent choice for implementing SMOTE. Data Wrangler is a fully managed service that allows ML engineers to aggregate, prepare, and transform data for machine learning with a visual interface. It includes built-in transformations specifically for handling imbalanced datasets, such as SMOTE and various undersampling techniques, making it efficient and user-friendly for this data preparation step. Using Data Wrangler ensures that the data balancing process is integrated seamlessly into the SageMaker ML workflow. In contrast, other options fall short: SageMaker JumpStart is primarily for pre-built solutions and models, not for generating bias reports like Clarify. Random undersampling (Options A, C) explicitly violates the "without losing existing training data" requirement. Acting when CI equals 0 (Options A, D) is illogical as it implies balancing an already balanced dataset. Authoritative Links for Further Research: Amazon SageMaker Clarify: AWS Documentation: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/clarify-bias-pre-training-report.html Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler: AWS Documentation: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/data-wrangler.html Blog Post on Imbalance Handling in Data Wrangler: https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/handle- imbalanced-data-for-your-machine-learning-models-with-amazon-sagemaker-data-wrangler/ SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique): Original Paper (N.V. Chawla et al., 2002): https://jair.org/index.php/jair/article/view/10317 (While not AWS specific, it's the foundational research for SMOTE).

questions.question: An ML engineer is building a logistic regression model to predict customer churn for subscription services. The ML engineer is using a dataset that contains two string variables: location and job_seniority_level. The location variable has 3 distinct values, and the job_seniority_level variable has over 10 distinct values. The ML engineer must perform preprocessing on the variables. Which solution will meet this requirement?

options.A: Apply tokenization to location. Apply ordinal encoding to job_seniority_level.
options.B: Apply one-hot encoding to location. Apply ordinal encoding to job_seniority_level
options.C: Apply binning to location. Apply standard scaling to job_seniority_level.
options.D: Apply one-hot encoding to location. Apply standard scaling to job_seniority_level.

questions.explanation: B is correct because one-hot preserves the nominal nature of the 3-valued location variable while ordinal encoding compactly and correctly represents the intrinsic rank structure of a multi-level seniority feature without exploding dimensionality. Logistic regression requires numeric inputs but encoding must respect variable measurement scale: nominal categories should not be given artificial order, and ordinal categories should preserve rank. One-hot for location creates orthogonal binary indicators that avoid imposing order and maintain interpretability in a low- cardinality case. job_seniority_level with >10 distinct, naturally-ordered levels is better served by ordinal encoding because it (a) preserves monotonic rank information, (b) keeps feature dimensionality small (mitigating sparsity and overfitting risks), and (c) yields coefficients interpretable as marginal changes per seniority step under the logistic link. Compact ordinal coding also interacts more predictably with regularization and convergence in convex solvers than a very high-dimensional one-hot expansion. Scaling binary one-hot columns is unnecessary; ordinal-encoded integers can be rescaled if numerical stability or regularization balance is required, but rescaling does not substitute for correct categorical treatment. Critical evaluation of other choices: A — Incorrect: Tokenization is for unstructured text (subword/word tokens) and is inappropriate for a low- cardinality categorical like location; it would create meaningless token artifacts. C — Incorrect: Binning is a numeric-to-discrete technique and makes no sense for a nominal location; applying standard scaling to a categorical seniority label treats discrete categories as continuous magnitude without preserving ordinal semantics. D — Incorrect: One-hot for location is correct, but standard scaling of a categorical seniority variable misuses a continuous transform on discrete labels, potentially distorting rank relationships and not addressing cardinality trade-offs that motivate ordinal encoding. Referenceshttps://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/preprocessing.html#encoding-categorical- featureshttps://scikit- learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.preprocessing.OneHotEncoder.htmlhttps://scikit- learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.preprocessing.OrdinalEncoder.html

questions.question: An ML engineer needs to use Amazon SageMaker to develop an ML solution for a company. The solution will use streaming video from cameras to count the number of people who walk past the company’s store every day. Select and order the steps from the following list to implement the first version of the algorithm. Each step should be selected one time. (Select and order three.) •Choose a built-in algorithm or pre-trained model. •Decide the data input format and apply data augmentation if necessary. •Determine if the challenge is a classification, detection, or segmentation problem.
options: Decide the data input format and apply data augmentation if necessary.
Determine if the challenge is a classification, detection, or segmentation problem.

questions.question: A company needs to deploy a custom-trained classification ML model on AWS. The model must make near real- time predictions with low latency and must handle variable request volumes. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Create an Amazon SageMaker AI batch transform job to process inference requests in batches.
options.B: Use Amazon API Gateway to receive prediction requests. Use an Amazon S3 bucket to host and serve the model.
options.C: Deploy an Amazon SageMaker AI endpoint. Configure auto scaling for the endpoint.
options.D: Launch AWS Deep Learning AMIs (DLAMI) on two Amazon EC2 instances. Run the instances behind an Application Load Balancer.

questions.explanation: C is correct because an Amazon SageMaker real-time endpoint provides managed, low-latency inference with integrated autoscaling to handle variable request volumes. Real-time SageMaker endpoints run containerized models for synchronous predict calls, delivering millisecond-to-sub-second latency depending on instance choice and model complexity, and they support both provisioned and serverless hosting to meet latency SLAs. SageMaker endpoints integrate with Application Auto Scaling and CloudWatch metrics to scale instance count or provisioned concurrency automatically, enabling cost-efficient elasticity under variable traffic. The managed service also covers model packaging, container lifecycle, multi-model/version deployment, and VPC/IAM networking controls, reducing operational burden and risk relative to self-managed compute. Structured evaluation of other options: Option A — Batch Transform: optimized for high-throughput, asynchronous batch jobs with significant startup/queue latency; unsuitable for near real-time, low-latency predictions. Option B — API Gateway + S3: S3 is object storage and cannot execute inference; API Gateway would still need a compute backend (Lambda/ECS/SageMaker) to serve low-latency predictions, so this architecture alone does not meet the requirement. Option D — DLAMI on EC2 + ALB: feasible but forces heavy operational management (OS/AMI/framework patching, manual or custom autoscaling, container/process orchestration) and increases complexity and latency risks versus a managed SageMaker endpoint. Reference: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/deploy-model.html" target="_blank">https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/deploy-model.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/endpoint-auto-scaling.html" target="_blank">https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/endpoint-auto-scaling.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/batch-transform.html " target="_blank">https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/batch-transform.html

questions.question: A company runs a neural network model and retrains the model when the performance degrades. The company uses a training job that uses Amazon SageMaker AI distributed data parallelism (DDP). The training job takes several hours to run. The company wants to decrease the required time for the training job. Which solution will meet this requirement?

options.A: Increase the number of epochs.
options.B: Increase the number of neurons in the hidden layers.
options.C: Increase the number of layers.
options.D: Increase the number of instances.

questions.explanation: The correct solution to decrease the required time for a training job using Amazon SageMaker AI distributed data parallelism (DDP) is D. Increase the number of instances. Amazon SageMaker's distributed data parallelism (DDP) is designed to accelerate model training by distributing the computational workload across multiple computing nodes (instances) and GPUs. In a DDP setup, each instance receives a distinct subset of the overall training data (a mini-batch). Each instance then independently performs a forward pass and computes gradients based on its assigned mini-batch. After local gradient computation, these gradients are aggregated across all participating instances using efficient communication primitives like all-reduce operations. This aggregation ensures that all instances maintain a synchronized view of the model's weights. By increasing the number of instances, the total amount of data processed concurrently per step increases significantly. This means more mini-batches are processed in parallel, effectively reducing the time it takes to complete one epoch of training. Consequently, the entire training job, which consists of multiple epochs, finishes much faster. This approach leverages the principles of distributed computing and horizontal scaling, where adding more computational resources (instances) directly translates to a reduction in the overall wall-clock training time for compute-intensive tasks like neural network training. For a job that takes "several hours," scaling out by adding more instances is the most direct and effective method to achieve faster completion when using a distributed training framework like DDP. Conversely, options A, B, and C would likely increase training time: A. Increase the number of epochs: This would require the model to iterate over the entire dataset more times, directly increasing the total training duration. B. Increase the number of neurons in the hidden layers: This increases the model's complexity and the number of parameters, leading to more computations per training step and thus longer training times. C. Increase the number of layers: Similar to increasing neurons, adding more layers makes the model deeper and more computationally expensive per training step, also increasing the training time. Therefore, to decrease the training time, the focus must be on increasing the parallel processing capability, which is achieved by adding more instances in a distributed training setup. Authoritative Links for Further Research: Amazon SageMaker Distributed Data Parallelism:https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/distributed-training-data-parallel.html Amazon SageMaker Training Instance Types:https://aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/pricing/ (refer to the ML compute instance section)

questions.question: An ML engineer decides to use Amazon SageMaker AI automated model tuning (AMT) for hyperparameter optimization (HPO). The ML engineer requires a tuning strategy that uses regression to slowly and sequentially select the next set of hyperparameters based on previous runs. The strategy must work across small hyperparameter ranges. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Grid search
options.B: Random search
options.C: Bayesian optimization
options.D: Hyperband

questions.explanation: Answer: C – Bayesian optimization Why Bayesian optimization is the best fit Sequential, surrogate-model driven search – Bayesian optimization builds a probabilistic surrogate (typically a Gaussian Process) of the objective function and selects the next hyper-parameter configuration that maximizes an acquisition function. This naturally “explores” promising regions while “exploiting” known good areas, allowing a slow, incremental refinement of the search space. Efficient for small hyper-parameter ranges – When the search space is limited, Bayesian optimization can achieve near-optimal performance with far fewer trials than exhaustive or random methods, because each new trial is chosen to provide the most information about the optimum. Built-in model-based guidance – The algorithm updates its belief about the objective after each run, making it ideal for scenarios where each training job is expensive (e.g., SageMaker HPO jobs) and you want to minimize the number of experiments. Compatibility with SageMaker AMT – SageMaker’s Automated Model Tuning supports Bayesian optimization as a native strategy, exposing it directly in the AlgorithmSpecification → TrainingInputMode → HyperParameterAlgorithm configuration. Why the other options are less suitable A. Grid search – Exhaustively evaluates every point in a Cartesian product of the hyper-parameter values. It scales poorly with even modestly sized ranges, leading to a combinatorial explosion of trials, which is wasteful when the goal is a slow, sequential approach. B. Random search – Samples hyper-parameter combinations at random. While it can be more efficient than grid search for some problems, it provides no mechanism to focus on promising regions and does not guarantee a sequential, data-driven refinement. D. Hyperband – Uses a bandit-based allocation of resources across configurations, prioritizing “cheap” trials early and terminating poor performers. It is designed for early-stopping and resource-aware scenarios, not specifically for a slow, sequential exploration within a tightly bounded hyper-parameter space. Conclusion Bayesian optimization aligns precisely with the requirement for a sequential, regression-based selection of hyper-parameters over a small, well-defined range, making it the optimal choice for SageMaker AMT in this context. References SageMaker Automatic Model Tuning – Hyperparameter Optimization Strategies: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/automatic-model-tuning.html#automatic-model-tuning- strategies Bayesian Optimization in Amazon SageMaker Hyperparameter Tuning: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/hyperparameter-interpretation.html#bayesian- optimization These links are official AWS documentation that confirm the described behavior and capabilities.

questions.question: A healthcare company wants to detect irregularities in patient vital signs that could indicate early signs of a medical condition. The company has an unlabeled dataset that includes patient health records, medication history, and lifestyle changes. Which algorithm and hyperparameter should the company use to meet this requirement?

options.A: Use the Amazon SageMaker AI XGBoost algorithm. Set max_depth to greater than 100 to regulate tree complexity.
options.B: Use the Amazon SageMaker AI k-means clustering algorithm. Set k to determine the number of clusters.
options.C: Use the Amazon SageMaker AI DeepAR algorithm. Set epochs to the number of training iterations.
options.D: Use the Amazon SageMaker AI Random Cut Forest (RCF) algorithm. Set num_trees to greater than 100.

questions.explanation: Justification The problem requires anomaly detection on an unlabeled dataset that contains heterogeneous health-record features. Random Cut Forest (RCF) is a native SageMaker algorithm built specifically for unsupervised anomaly detection; it models data as a forest of random trees and uses the depth of a point’s path to assign an anomaly score. Setting num_trees > 100 increases the forest’s capacity to capture subtle patterns while maintaining robustness to noise, which is appropriate for high-dimensional medical data. Why the other options are less suitable XGBoost (A) – a supervised gradient-boosted tree model; it needs labeled outcomes to learn a predictive objective and cannot directly produce anomaly scores. Raising max_depth > 100 would over-fit and does not align with the unsupervised requirement. k-means clustering (B) – groups similar records but does not provide a dedicated anomaly metric; choosing k only defines clusters, not abnormal instances, making it ill-suited for detecting rare vital-sign irregularities. DeepAR (C) – a forecasting model that predicts future values; it is designed for time-series prediction, not for flagging anomalous observations in a static feature set. Therefore, the Random Cut Forest algorithm with a sufficient number of trees best meets the requirement of detecting early irregularities in patient vital signs from unlabeled health data. References Random Cut Forest (RCF) algorithm – Amazon SageMaker documentation: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/random-cut-forest.html Using RCF for anomaly detection – SageMaker blog: https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/using- random-cut-forest-for-anomaly-detection-in-amazon-sagemaker/

questions.question: A company is training an image classification model on Amazon SageMaker AI to differentiate between images of cats and dogs. The company is using images from a third-party source. The company needs to label the images of cats and dogs by adding the correct label to a metadata summary file that is attached to each image. The company wants to ensure that the dataset includes an equal number of images of cats and dogs. Which solution will meet this requirement?

options.A: Use SageMaker Clarify to check for class imbalance (CI). If the value is close to 1, then the dataset is balanced.
options.B: Use SageMaker Clarify to check for class imbalance (CI). If the value is close to 0, then the dataset is balanced.
options.C: Use SageMaker Data Wrangler to check for difference in proportions of labels (DPL). If the value is close to 1, then the dataset is balanced.
options.D: Use SageMaker Data Wrangler to check for difference in proportions of labels (DPL). If the value is close to -1, then the dataset is balanced.

questions.explanation: Technical justification SageMaker Clarify’s Class Imbalance (CI) metric is designed specifically to quantify the balance between classes in a classification dataset. The CI score ranges from 0 to 1, where 0 indicates perfect balance (i.e., the observed class distribution matches the desired equal-frequency distribution) and values approaching 1 indicate increasing imbalance. Therefore, to verify that the cat-dog dataset contains an equal number of each class, the team should check that the CI value is close to 0. This directly satisfies the requirement of an equal class count. Why the other options are unsuitable Option A – Incorrect interpretation; a CI value close to 1 signals high imbalance, not balance. Option C & D – SageMaker Data Wrangler is a visual data-exploration and transformation tool; it does not provide a formal imbalance metric. The “difference in proportions of labels (DPL)” is not a standard CI measure, and a value of -1 has no meaning for balance assessment. Using Data Wrangler for this purpose would not reliably guarantee an equal number of cats and dogs. Implementation approach 1. Export the metadata summary file (e.g., a CSV with image_path, label). 2. Load the dataset into a SageMaker notebook and invoke sagemaker.clarify.CleanDataQualityJob with the label column specified as the target feature. 3. Review the generated Data Quality Report → Class Imbalance section; a CI score near 0 confirms the dataset meets the equal-class requirement. References Amazon SageMaker Clarify Data Quality documentation: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/clarify-data-quality.html Blog post on detecting class imbalance with SageMaker Clarify: https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine- learning/how-to-use-amazon-sagemaker-clarify-to-detect-data-bias-and-data-quality-issues/

questions.question: An ML engineer at an entertainment company is refining an ML model to predict audience preferences. As the model learns from data, the ML engineer notices that the model’s performance peaks early and then begins to gradually decline. The ML engineer must prevent the performance degradation after initial success. Which solution will meet this requirement?

options.A: Increase the number of layers.
options.B: Implement early stopping.
options.C: Add more neurons to each layer to capture complex patterns.
options.D: Examine model bias and variance to understand performance issues.

questions.explanation: Technical justification Correct choice – B. Implement early stopping Early stopping monitors a validation metric (e.g., validation loss or accuracy) during training and halts the training job once that metric stops improving or begins to degrade. In the scenario the model’s performance rises quickly, then slowly declines – a classic sign of over-fitting. By stopping at the epoch with the highest validation performance, the model is preserved in its best-generalizing state, preventing further degradation. This technique is directly supported by AWS services (e.g., SageMaker Training Jobs with EarlyStoppingConfig or built-in algorithms that expose an early-stopping hook), making it a practical, AWS-native solution. Why the other options are less suitable A. Increase the number of layers – Adding depth raises model capacity, which can exacerbate over-fitting and will not inherently stop the performance drop; it may even accelerate it. C. Add more neurons to each layer – Similar to increasing layers, this expands capacity without addressing the root cause (over-fitting) and can worsen the decline rather than halt it. D. Examine model bias and variance – While diagnosing bias-variance is valuable for long-term model health, it is an analytical step, not an immediate corrective action. It does not automatically prevent performance degradation during training. References Early stopping configuration in Amazon SageMaker: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/early-stopping.html Understanding over-fitting and regularization in AWS Machine Learning: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/machine-learning/latest/dg/overfitting.html

questions.question: An ML engineer is tuning an image classification model that shows poor performance on one of two available classes during prediction. Analysis reveals that the images whose class the model performed poorly on represent an extremely small fraction of the whole training dataset. The ML engineer must improve the model’s performance. Which solution will meet this requirement?

options.A: Optimize for accuracy. Use image augmentation on the less common images to generate new samples.
options.B: Optimize for F1 score. Use image augmentation on the less common images to generate new samples.
options.C: Optimize for accuracy. Use Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) on the less common images to generate new samples.
options.D: Optimize for F1 score. Use Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) on the less common images to generate new samples.

questions.explanation: Technical justification The problem is a severe class imbalance where the minority class occupies only a tiny portion of the training data, leading to low recall (or high false-negative rate) for that class. In image classification, the primary business metric is often the ability to correctly detect the minority class, not merely overall accuracy; therefore the evaluation metric should reflect class-balanced performance. Option B proposes optimizing for the F1 score and applying image augmentation to the under-represented class. F1 combines precision and recall, making it sensitive to improvements on the minority class. Image augmentation creates diverse synthetic samples from the scarce images, expanding the minority distribution without artificially inflating majority examples, which would not help the recall component. This combination directly addresses the imbalance while preserving realistic data characteristics for deep-learning pipelines. Why the other options are inferior A – Optimizing only for accuracy encourages the model to favor the majority class, worsening minority-class performance. C – SMOTE is designed for tabular data; applying it to raw images is not valid and would produce meaningless feature vectors. D – Although F1 is appropriate, SMOTE cannot be directly used on images; augmenting images is the correct technique, not SMOTE. Conclusion – The only solution that aligns the optimization objective with the class-imbalance nature of the problem and uses a feasible augmentation strategy is B. References AWS Documentation – Model Evaluation Metrics for Imbalanced Data: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/evaluation-metrics.html Scikit-learn Documentation – F1 Score: https://scikit- learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.metrics.f1_score.html

questions.question: A healthcare analytics company wants to segment patients into groups that have similar risk factors to develop personalized treatment plans. The company has a dataset that includes patient health records, medication history, and lifestyle changes. The company must identify the appropriate algorithm to determine the number of groups by using hyperparameters. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Use the Amazon SageMaker AI XGBoost algorithm. Set max_depth to control tree complexity for risk groups.
options.B: Use the Amazon SageMaker k-means clustering algorithm. Set k to specify the number of clusters.
options.C: Use the Amazon SageMaker AI DeepAR algorithm. Set epochs to determine the number of training iterations for risk groups.
options.D: Use the Amazon SageMaker AI Random Cut Forest (RCF) algorithm. Set a contamination hyperparameter for risk anomaly groups.

questions.explanation: Why option B is the correct choice The problem is an unsupervised segmentation task: the goal is to discover a set of patient groups (clusters) that share similar risk-factor patterns. SageMaker k-means is a native, scalable clustering algorithm that directly controls the number of groups via the hyperparameter k. By setting k to the desired number of clusters, the algorithm partitions the data into exactly that many groups, which matches the requirement to “identify the appropriate algorithm to determine the number of groups by using hyperparameters.” The algorithm also handles high-dimensional numeric data (e.g., health-record vectors) efficiently and provides cluster assignments that can be fed into downstream personalized-treatment workflows. Why the other options are not appropriate A – XGBoost is a supervised gradient-boosted tree method. It requires labeled outcomes (e.g., risk of disease) to train a predictive model; it does not produce clusters or let you set a hyperparameter to dictate the number of groups. max_depth influences tree complexity, not the count of groups. C – DeepAR is a forecasting model for time-series data. It predicts future values and uses epochs to control training iterations, which has no relation to defining discrete patient groups. D – Random Cut Forest (RCF) is an anomaly-detection algorithm. Its contamination hyperparameter estimates the proportion of anomalous points, not the total number of distinct clusters. It is suited for identifying outliers rather than segmenting the population into equal-sized risk groups. Conclusion Only the SageMaker k-means algorithm directly satisfies the requirement of determining the number of patient groups through a controllable hyperparameter (k). References Amazon SageMaker k-means algorithm documentation: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/k-means.html Understanding the k parameter in clustering: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/k- means.html#k-means-algorithm-parameters

questions.question: An ML engineer needs to develop an AI assistant by using Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) in Amazon Bedrock. The company stores a collection of PDF text files in an Amazon S3 bucket. The ML engineer must create an Amazon Bedrock knowledge base to process the PDF files and to store the processed files in a vector store. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Use the latest version of Amazon Titan Text Premier to perform document embedding.
options.B: Use the latest version of Mistral 7B Instruct to perform instruction tuning.
options.C: Use the latest version of Anthropic Claude Sonnet to perform instruction tuning.
options.D: Use Cohere Embed Multilingual to perform document embedding.

questions.explanation: Why option D is the correct choice Cohere Embed Multilingual is a dedicated embedding model that converts textual content (including PDF-extracted text) into dense vector representations. In Amazon Bedrock, the knowledge-base feature relies on a vector store that must be populated with embeddings generated by a supported embedding model. Cohere Embed Multilingual is one of the officially supported models for this purpose. It works out-of-the-box with Bedrock’s knowledge-base ingestion pipeline, handling large PDF collections stored in S3 without requiring additional preprocessing steps. The model is multilingual, robust to varied document formats, and provides high-quality vectors that preserve semantic meaning, which is essential for retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). Why the other options are not suitable A. Amazon Titan Text Premier – This is a large language model for text generation, not an embedding model. Bedrock’s knowledge-base requires an embedding generator; Titan Premier cannot produce the vectors needed for semantic search. B. Mistral 7B Instruct – Although a powerful instruction-tuned LLM, Mistral 7B is not a dedicated embedding model and is not listed as a supported embedding source in Bedrock’s knowledge-base configuration. C. Anthropic Claude Sonnet – Like Titan Premier, Claude Sonnet is a generative LLM. It does not provide the embedding functionality required to index PDF content into a vector store within Bedrock. Implementation flow (high-level) 1. Extract text from PDFs in S3 (e.g., using AWS Glue or Lambda). 2. Ingest the extracted chunks into a Bedrock knowledge-base. 3. Select Cohere Embed Multilingual as the embedding model during knowledge-base creation. 4. Bedrock automatically generates vectors and stores them in the integrated vector store (e.g., Pinecone, OpenSearch Serverless, or Amazon Aurora). 5. The RAG pipeline can then retrieve relevant vectors at runtime and augment prompts for the chosen generative model (e.g., Claude, Titan, etc.). References Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases – Supported embedding models: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/knowledge-bases.html#knowledge-bases- embeddings Cohere Embed Multilingual documentation: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/embedding-models.html#embedding-cohere-embed- multilingual

questions.question: An ML engineer needs to build a processing pipeline to identify and remove personally identifiable information (PII) from petabytes of unstructured data. The ML engineer will use the processed data to train ML models in Amazon SageMaker AI. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Use the Apache Spark-based serverless engine from AWS Glue interactive sessions. Use the Detect PII transform feature to identify and remove the PII data.
options.B: Use AWS Glue Data Wrangler within Amazon SageMaker Canvas to detect and remove the PII.
options.C: Use the Amazon SageMaker Clarify API to detect and mask the PII data.
options.D: Use the DetectEntities API action in Amazon Comprehend to identify and remove the PII data.

questions.explanation: Why option A is the correct choice Scalable, server-less processing – AWS Glue interactive sessions run on an Apache Spark engine that can automatically scale to petabyte-scale workloads, making them suitable for massive unstructured data stores. Built-in PII detection and masking – Glue provides a Detect PII transform that identifies personally identifiable information (names, emails, SSNs, etc.) and can replace or redact those fields in a single pass, eliminating the need for custom code. Native integration with downstream SageMaker training – The output of the Glue job can be written directly to an S3 location that SageMaker can ingest, preserving the same data format and schema required for model training. Fully managed and cost-effective – As a serverless service, Glue handles cluster provisioning, patching, and scaling, reducing operational overhead and aligning with the “pay-as-you-go” model expected in a certification scenario. Why the other options are less suitable Option B – Glue Data Wrangler in SageMaker Canvas Canvas is a low-code data-preparation UI aimed at analysts, not a batch-processing engine. It lacks the distributed Spark foundation needed for petabyte-scale jobs and does not expose a programmatic Detect PII transform for automated pipelines. Option C – SageMaker Clarify API Clarify is purpose-built for model explainability and bias detection; it does not provide PII identification or masking capabilities, nor does it operate on raw data stores at the scale required. Option D – Amazon Comprehend DetectEntities API While Comprehend can detect PII entities, the DetectEntities operation is designed for individual request-level inference, not for bulk, petabyte-scale data processing. Running it at that scale would be operationally expensive and would not integrate seamlessly with a Glue-based ETL pipeline. References AWS Glue interactive sessions: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/interactive-sessions.html Glue transforms – Detect PII: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/transforms.html#transforms-pii

questions.question: A company develops a recommendation model and hosts the model on an Amazon SageMaker AI endpoint. The model uses the SageMaker AI endpoint to perform near real-time inference to deliver personalized product recommendations to customers based on browsing history, purchase records, and in-app user interactions. After a major marketing campaign, the company observes a sharp drop in the model’s performance. The company needs a solution to proactively monitor, detect, and validate model performance before future marketing campaigns. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Use SageMaker Clarify to analyze changes in feature distribution. Configure SageMaker Model Monitor for near real-time input validation.
options.B: Use Amazon CloudWatch dashboards to monitor endpoint metrics. Use SageMaker Model Monitor to track feature attribution.
options.C: Use SageMaker Clarify for bias detection. Set up Amazon CloudWatch alarms to monitor model latency.
options.D: Use SageMaker Model Monitor to monitor constraints. Use Amazon CloudWatch Logs Insights to analyze error patterns.

questions.explanation: Why option A is the best solution SageMaker Clarify + Model Monitor directly addresses the root cause of performance drop after a marketing campaign: changes in the data that the model sees in production. Clarify can detect covariate shift by comparing the distribution of input features (e.g., browsing history, purchase records) between training and inference data, while Model Monitor automatically captures feature-level statistics and alerts when drift exceeds a threshold. Together they enable proactive monitoring (continuous drift detection) and validation (automated statistical tests) without manual data pulls. The setup can be integrated with an endpoint-level inference pipeline, providing near-real-time alerts before a campaign’s traffic arrives, allowing the team to retrain or adjust the model ahead of time. Why the other options are less suitable B. CloudWatch dashboards only show endpoint operational metrics (latency, error rates) and cannot evaluate feature-level data drift. Model Monitor alone can track drift but the answer deliberately pairs it with a less-relevant CloudWatch use case, missing the Clarify component that explicitly measures input-feature distribution changes. C. Bias detection with Clarify is unrelated to performance degradation caused by campaign-driven data shifts; CloudWatch alarms on latency do not validate model quality or detect feature drift. D. Model Monitor can enforce constraints, but CloudWatch Logs Insights is mainly for log-level troubleshooting (e.g., error messages). It does not provide statistical assessment of feature distribution changes, making the combination too generic for proactive model-performance monitoring. Bottom line: Option A delivers a complete, end-to-end monitoring pipeline that specifically detects input-feature drift (the likely cause of the performance drop) and can trigger automated validation and alerts before future campaigns. References SageMaker Model Monitor documentation – https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/monitoring.html SageMaker Clarify documentation – https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/clarify-model.html

questions.question: A company is using Amazon SageMaker AI to create a classification model to categorize the company’s sales performance for each month of the previous 20 years on a scale from 1 to 5. The dataset includes fields for month, sales region, regional aggregate sales, and the number of stores in each sales region. The company notices that during two months of every year, the aggregate sales values are unexpectedly high. The company performs one- hot encoding on all non-numerical features in the training and validation datasets. The company uses the training dataset to train the classification model. When the company evaluates the model against the validation dataset, the results are less accurate than expected. The company must improve the model’s accuracy on the validation dataset. Which solution will meet this requirement?

options.A: Remove records that include outliers across all features.
options.B: Use a stratified split on the month and sales region features.
options.C: Perform normalization on the aggregate sales feature.
options.D: Perform normalization on the aggregate sales feature for each sales region.

questions.explanation: Why option B is the best choice The validation performance is low because the train-validation split does not preserve the distribution of the key categorical variables month and sales-region. When certain months or regions are under-represented in the validation set, the model has never (or rarely) seen those patterns during training, leading to poor accuracy on them. A stratified split on month and sales-region guarantees that each fold contains a proportional representation of every month-region combination, ensuring that the validation set reflects the same class balance and temporal-regional patterns present in the full data. This directly improves the model’s ability to generalize to all months and regions, especially the two months per year that currently cause high aggregate sales and are likely under-sampled. Why the other options are less suitable A – Remove outlier records – Outliers may actually correspond to the high-sales months the company wants the model to learn. Deleting them discards valuable signal and can bias the model toward “normal” patterns, worsening performance on the rare high-sales cases. C – Normalize aggregate sales globally – Global scaling does not address the underlying class-imbalance issue caused by uneven month/region representation. It may help optimization but will not increase validation accuracy for the under-represented groups. D – Normalize aggregate sales per region – While region-specific scaling can reduce magnitude bias, it still does not ensure that each validation fold contains all months and regions in the correct proportion. The root cause—imbalanced split—remains unaddressed. Implementation tip Use sklearn.model_selection.StratifiedShuffleSplit (or SageMaker’s built-in data splitting utilities) with a stratify parameter on both month and sales-region to create train/validation splits that preserve the original distribution. References Stratified sampling for train-validation splits – AWS SageMaker documentation on data preparation: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/data-processing.html#data-processing-stratified-split Handling categorical features and class imbalance – Amazon SageMaker built-in algorithms guide: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/algorithmic-frameworks.html#algorithmic-frameworks- classification#algorithmic-frameworks-classification-sampling

questions.question: A company is creating an ML model to identify defects in a product. The company has gathered a dataset and has stored the dataset in TIFF format in Amazon S3. The dataset contains 200 images in which the most common defects are visible. The dataset also contains 1,800 images in which there is no defect visible. An ML engineer trains the model and notices poor performance in some classes. The ML engineer identifies a class imbalance problem in the dataset. What should the ML engineer do to solves this problem?

options.A: Use a few hundred images and Amazon Rekognition Custom Labels to train a new model.
options.B: Undersample the 200 images in which the most common defects are visible.
options.C: Oversample the 200 images in which the most common defects are visible.
options.D: Use all 2,000 images and Amazon Rekognition Custom Labels to train a new model.

questions.explanation: Technical justification The dataset is highly imbalanced: 200 images depict the dominant defect class, while 1,800 images belong to the “no-defect” class. This skew causes the model to over-fit to the majority class and results in poor recall for the minority defect class. Option C – Oversample the 200 defect images directly addresses the imbalance by increasing the representation of the minority class in the training set. Oversampling (e.g., using duplicate samples or synthetic generation such as SMOTE) balances the class distribution, allowing the model to learn characteristics of the defect pattern without discarding any data. This approach preserves all 2,000 images, maintains the original label fidelity, and can improve both precision and recall for the defect class, which is critical for defect-detection use cases. Option A and D propose re-training with Amazon Rekognition Custom Labels, but the core issue is not the labeling service; it is the skewed label distribution. Re-training with a larger labeled set would not resolve the imbalance unless the underlying sampling strategy changes, making these options indirect and unnecessary. Option B – Undersample the dominant “no-defect” images would reduce the majority class to match the 200 defect images. While this balances the classes, it discards 1,600 useful images, potentially degrading overall model performance and wasting data that could improve generalization. Oversampling is preferred when the total dataset is relatively small and all samples are valuable. Conclusion: Oversampling the minority defect class is the most appropriate corrective action for the identified class-imbalance problem. References Handling class imbalance in Amazon SageMaker: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/class-- imbalance.html Amazon Rekognition Custom Labels documentation: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/rekognition/latest/dg/custom-labels.html

questions.question: An ML engineer is building an ML model in Amazon SageMaker AI. The ML engineer needs to load historical data directly from Amazon S3, Amazon Athena, and Snowflake into SageMaker AI. Which solution will meet this requirement?

options.A: Use AWS Glue DataBrew to import the data into SageMaker AI.
options.B: Build a pipeline in SageMaker Pipelines to process the data. Use AWS DataSync to load the processed data into SageMaker AI.
options.C: Create a feature store in SageMaker Feature Store. Use an Apache Spark connector to Feature Store to access the data.
options.D: Use SageMaker Data Wrangler to query and import the data.

questions.explanation: Why option D is the optimal choice SageMaker Data Wrangler provides a visual, low-code interface that can directly query external data sources such as Amazon S3, Amazon Athena, and Snowflake. It automatically generates the necessary AWS Glue connectors and Spark-based ingestion pipelines, handling schema inference, data type conversion, and incremental loads without requiring the engineer to write custom ETL code. The imported data can be saved directly to an S3 bucket that SageMaker training jobs consume, or it can be persisted in a Feature Store for downstream model development, satisfying the “load historical data directly into SageMaker AI” requirement in a single step. Because Data Wrangler is tightly integrated with the SageMaker ecosystem, the resulting datasets are immediately usable by downstream SageMaker services (e.g., training, processing, model deployment) with minimal additional configuration. Why the other options are less suitable Option A – AWS Glue DataBrew: DataBrew is primarily a data-preparation service for cleaning and enriching data; it does not provide a native, end-to-end pipeline that loads data into SageMaker AI. Additional steps would be needed to move the processed data into SageMaker, making it indirect and more complex. Option B – SageMaker Pipelines + AWS DataSync: Building a custom pipeline in Pipelines and then using DataSync adds unnecessary layers of orchestration. DataSync is designed for large-scale file movement, not for interactive querying of Athena or Snowflake, and this approach would require separate processing jobs before data can be consumed by SageMaker, increasing latency and operational overhead. Option C – Feature Store with Apache Spark connector: While the Feature Store can store feature data, accessing external sources like Athena and Snowflake via a Spark connector requires the engineer to provision and manage Spark clusters, configure connectors, and manually handle data ingestion. This is more engineering effort than the out-of-the-box querying capabilities offered by Data Wrangler. Conclusion Option D leverages a purpose-built, integrated tool (SageMaker Data Wrangler) that directly connects to the required data sources, automates ingestion, and makes the data immediately available for SageMaker workflows, making it the most efficient and least-custom solution for the stated requirement. References SageMaker Data Wrangler – Importing data from external sources: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/data-wrangler-import.html Feature Store integration with external data sources: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/features-store-data.html#feature-store-data-sources

questions.question: An ML engineer has developed a custom scikit-learn script to transform tabular data to train a regression model. The ML engineer wants to use Amazon SageMaker AI to prepare data and to train and evaluate the model. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Create an AWS Lambda function to run the custom script. Load the processed data into SageMaker AI to train the model.
options.B: Use the SKLearnProcessor class within the SageMaker Python SDK to run the custom script.
options.C: Apply the HuggingFaceProcessor class within the SageMaker Python SDK to run the custom script.
options.D: Use AWS Glue ETL custom recipes to perform data transformations. Load the processed data into SageMaker AI to train the model.

questions.explanation: Why option B is the optimal choice Native integration with custom scikit-learn code – SKLearnProcessor lets you specify your own training script, environment, and dependencies, executing it on an Amazon-managed ML compute instance (e.g., ml.m5.xlarge). End-to-end data preparation – You can chain preprocessing steps (e.g., scaling, encoding) directly inside the script or use SageMaker Processing Jobs, keeping the workflow inside the same SDK. Managed training & evaluation – The processor automatically provisions a training job, captures the output model artifact, and can trigger a SageMaker Training job for regression, simplifying experiment tracking. Cost-effective and scalable – No need to manage separate Lambda invocations or external ETL services; resources are spun up only for the duration of the processing/training job. Why the other options are less suitable A – Lambda + SageMaker AI – Lambda has a 15-minute timeout and limited CPU/memory, making it impractical for data-intensive preprocessing or model training. It also requires manual orchestration of SageMaker jobs. C – HuggingFaceProcessor – This class is purpose-built for Hugging Face model fine-tuning, not for generic scikit-learn pipelines; using it would add unnecessary complexity and licensing overhead. D – AWS Glue ETL + SageMaker – Glue recipes are optimized for large-scale data lake ETL, not for lightweight tabular transformations tied to a single model. They introduce extra services, data movement, and operational overhead compared to the direct SKLearnProcessor approach. References SKLearnProcessor documentation – https://sagemaker.readthedocs.io/en/stable/amazon_sagemaker_training.html#sklearnprocessor SageMaker Python SDK overview – https://sagemaker.readthedocs.io/en/stable/overview.html These links provide the official AWS documentation confirming the capabilities and usage patterns of SKLearnProcessor for custom scikit-learn workflows in SageMaker.

questions.question: A company develops an ML model to classify products. The model uses textual data and visual data to classify the products into a hierarchical taxonomy. An ML engineer must use specific strategies to enhance the model’s accuracy and handle class imbalances. Select the correct strategy from the following list for each use case. Select each strategy one time. (Choose five.) •Categorical cross-entropy •Fallback metric •Hierarchical loss •General oversampling •Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) for text
options: Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) for text

questions.question: A company is developing an application that reads animal descriptions from user prompts and generates images based on the information from the prompts. The application reads a message from an Amazon Simple Queue Service (Amazon SQS) queue. Then the application uses Amazon Titan Image Generator on Amazon Bedrock to generate an image based on the information in the message. Finally, the application removes the message from SQS queue. Which IAM permissions should the company assign to the application’s IAM role? (Choose two.)

options.A: Allow the bedrock:InvokeModel action for the Amazon Titan Image Generator resource.
options.B: Allow the bedrock:Get* action for the Amazon Titan Image Generator resource.
options.C: Allow the sqs:ReceiveMessage action and the sqs:DeleteMessage action for the SQS queue resource.
options.D: Allow the sqs:GetQueueAttributes action and the sqs:DeleteMessage action for the SQS queue resource.
options.E: Allow the sagemaker:PutRecord* action for the Amazon Titan Image Generator resource.

questions.explanation: Correct choices: A and C Why they are required A – bedrock:InvokeModel – The application must call the Amazon Titan Image Generator on Bedrock to turn the prompt into an image. InvokeModel is the only IAM action that authorizes a request to run a Bedrock model; Get* (option B) only permits reading metadata and cannot invoke the model. C – sqs:ReceiveMessage + sqs:DeleteMessage – After a prompt arrives in the SQS queue, the consumer must be able to read the message (ReceiveMessage) and then remove it once processing is finished (DeleteMessage). These two actions are sufficient for the full “read-process-delete” cycle; no additional SQS permissions are needed. Why the other options are not appropriate B – bedrock:Get – Get only allows reading information about a model (e.g., model details) and cannot be used to actually invoke the model. Without InvokeModel, the image cannot be generated. D – sqs:GetQueueAttributes + sqs:DeleteMessage – GetQueueAttributes retrieves queue-level attributes but is not needed to consume messages. The consumer must still receive the message, which requires sqs:ReceiveMessage; omitting it prevents the application from obtaining the prompt. E – sagemaker:PutRecord* – This action is used to send records to Amazon SageMaker Edge or Ground Station endpoints and has no relevance to invoking a Bedrock model or interacting with SQS, so it does not fulfil the required permissions. Resulting IAM policy (conceptual) Permission bedrock:InvokeModel on the Titan Image Generator resource. Permissions sqs:ReceiveMessage and sqs:DeleteMessage on the specific SQS queue used for prompts. These two permissions together enable the application to pull prompts from SQS, call the Titan Image Generator, and cleanly remove processed messages. References Amazon Bedrock inference permissions – InvokeModel Amazon SQS IAM actions – ReceiveMessage and DeleteMessage

questions.question: An ML engineer is setting up a continuous integration and continuous delivery (CI/CD) pipeline for an ML workflow in Amazon SageMaker AI. The pipeline needs to automate model re-training, testing, and deployment whenever new data is uploaded to an Amazon S3 bucket. New data files are approximately 10 GB in size. The ML engineer wants to track model versions for auditing. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Use AWS CodePipeline. Amazon S3, and AWS CodeBuild to retrain and deploy the model automatically and to track model versions.
options.B: Use SageMaker Pipelines with the SageMaker Model Registry to orchestrate model training and version tracking.
options.C: Create an AWS Lambda function to re-train and deploy the model. Use Amazon EventBridge to invoke the Lambda function. Reference the Lambda logs to track model versions.
options.D: Use SageMaker AI notebook instances to manually re-train and deploy the model when needed. Reference AWS CloudTrail logs to track model versions.

questions.explanation: Why option B is the optimal solution End-to-end orchestration – Amazon SageMaker Pipelines provides a native, server-less pipeline engine that can ingest a new data file in an S3 bucket, trigger a training step, run validation/evaluation jobs, and automatically promote the model to a production endpoint. Built-in model versioning – The pipeline can be wired directly to the SageMaker Model Registry, which stores each model artifact with immutable version metadata (model name, version number, parameters, input schema, and lineage). This satisfies the auditing requirement without needing custom logging or external artifact stores. Scalable processing of 10 GB files – SageMaker Jobs can read large objects from S3 without additional processing layers; CodeBuild or Lambda-based approaches would struggle with the memory and timeout constraints for 10 GB payloads. CI/CD best practices – Pipelines support parameterization, conditional execution, and artifact passing, enabling the typical CI/CD stages (build → test → deploy) in a single, repeatable workflow. Operational simplicity – All components (compute, storage, registry) are managed by SageMaker, reducing operational overhead and ensuring consistency across stages. Why the other options are less suitable Option A (AWS CodePipeline + CodeBuild) – While CodePipeline can glue together S3, CodeBuild, and SageMaker, it lacks native model-registry integration and built-in validation steps tailored for ML. Version tracking would require custom solution building, and CodeBuild is not optimized for large training data processing. Option C (Lambda + EventBridge) – Lambda has a 15-minute timeout and 10 GB-plus memory isn’t feasible; it cannot reliably handle 10 GB data loads or long-running training jobs. Moreover, model versioning is derived only from log entries, which is error-prone and not auditable in the same way as a registry. Option D (Manual SageMaker notebooks + CloudTrail) – This approach defeats the purpose of a fully automated CI/CD pipeline; it requires human initiation for each retraining cycle. Auditing relies on CloudTrail logs, which do not provide structured model version metadata. References SageMaker Pipelines – AWS Documentation https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/pipelines.html SageMaker Model Registry – AWS Documentation https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model-registry.html

questions.question: An ML engineer needs to monitor the behavior of an ML model and track bias drift. The ML engineer must detect issues from an Amazon SageMaker Al endpoint so the ML engineer can take actions to address any identified issues. The ML engineer has collected Amazon SageMaker Ground Truth labels that show correct results for a set of test data. Select the AWS service feature from the following list to correctly answer each of the following questions. Select each service feature one time. •Amazon CloudWatch alarms •Amazon CloudWatch metrics •Amazon S3 bucket •Amazon SageMaker Clarify

An ML engineering team is spread across multiple locations. When the lead ML engineer opens an Amazon SageMaker Al notebook, the ML engineer does not see the latest merged notebook made by other team members from a Git repository. The lead ML engineer must see the latest SageMaker AI notebook updates. Which solution will meet this requirement?

options.A: Run the !git pull origin master command.
options.B: Run the !git commit command.
options.C: Run the !git push origin master command.
options.D: Run the !git branch command.

questions.explanation: Technical justification The problem is that the lead ML engineer is not seeing the most recent notebook changes that were merged into the shared Git repository. To retrieve those changes from a remote repository into the local notebook environment, the correct Git operation is pull: !git pull origin master fetches the latest commits from the origin remote’s master branch and merges them into the current local branch. This ensures the local notebook’s workspace reflects the most recent state of the repository, exactly what the lead engineer needs. Running !git commit only creates a new commit; it does not bring in changes from the remote, so it cannot resolve the stale-file problem. !git push origin master sends local commits to the remote; it does not affect what the local workspace sees, and would actually publish any uncommitted changes rather than retrieve updates. !git branch merely lists or manages branches; it does not fetch or merge any code, so it cannot update the notebook content. Therefore, only option A directly addresses the need to synchronize the local SageMaker AI notebook with the latest repository contents. References AWS SageMaker AI Notebooks – Git integration: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/developerguide/notebooks-git-integration.html Git Basics – Pull command: https://git-scm.com/docs/git-pull

questions.question: An ML engineer is preparing data for binary classification modeling. The ML engineer runs an Amazon SageMaker Clarify processing job. The bias report shows that the difference in proportions of labels (DPL) score is 0.4. The ML engineer wants the occurrence of labels across classes to be equal. The ML engineer decides to use SageMaker Data Wrangler to balance the data. What should the ML engineer do to most of the data to meet these requirements?

options.A: Use random oversampling to increase the DPL score.
options.B: Use random undersampling to increase the DPL score
options.C: Use random oversampling to reduce the DPL score.
options.D: Use random undersampling to reduce the DPL score.

questions.explanation: Technical justification A DPL (Disparate Impact Proportion) of 0.4 indicates that the minority class appears in only 40 % of the records, far from a balanced 50 / 50 distribution. To increase the proportion of the minority label (i.e., make class frequencies more equal), the dataset must be augmented with additional instances of the under-represented class. Random oversampling does exactly this: it duplicates or creates synthetic samples of the minority class, thereby raising its share in the overall label distribution and reducing the DPL score (the gap shrinks). Consequently, Option C – “Use random oversampling to reduce the DPL score.” directly achieves the desired outcome. Why the other options are unsuitable Option A – Random oversampling does not increase the DPL score; it diminishes the disparity, so the wording is contradictory. Option B – Random undersampling removes majority-class samples, which would increase the imbalance if applied to an already skewed dataset, again moving the DPL further away from the target of equality. Option D – Undersampling also reduces the DPL score only when the majority class is disproportionately large, but here the goal is to bring the minority class up to parity, not to strip away majority examples. Therefore, the optimal preprocessing step is to oversample the minority label, which lowers the DPL metric and moves the class distribution toward balance. References SageMaker Clarify documentation – Bias detection and metrics: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/clarify-bias.html SageMaker Data Wrangler – Data preprocessing and balancing: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/data-wrangler.html#data-wrangler-oversampling

questions.question: An ML engineer is using Amazon SageMaker AI to train a deep learning model. During testing, the model shows high variance. The ML engineer needs to adjust a hyperparameter to improve generalization without significantly increasing training time. Which solution will MOST improve the model generalization?

options.A: Increase the number of layers in the neural network.
options.B: Reduce the learning rate of the optimizer.
options.C: Increase the L2 regularization parameter
options.D: Increase the batch size significantly.

questions.explanation: Correct option: C – Increase the L2 regularization parameter Why option C improves generalization: Adding L2 regularization penalizes large weights, encouraging smaller, smoother parameter values and reducing over-fitting, which directly combats high variance. The penalty is applied during each gradient update, so its effect is immediate; no extra epochs, data movement, or large batch reshaping are required, keeping training time essentially unchanged. SageMaker’s built-in algorithms (e.g., XGBoost, SageMaker-provided containers for TensorFlow/PyTorch) all expose an L2 or weight_decay hyperparameter that can be tuned without redesigning the network architecture. Why the other options are less suitable: A. Increase the number of layers – Deepening the network adds capacity, which typically increases variance and can further degrade generalization unless accompanied by substantial regularization; it also raises computational cost and training time. B. Reduce the learning rate – A smaller learning rate may improve stability but does not directly reduce model complexity or variance; convergence may require more steps, potentially extending training time without guaranteeing better generalization. D. Increase the batch size significantly – Larger batches lower gradient noise, which can make optimization smoother but usually increase effective model capacity and may not improve generalization; moreover, very large batches can reduce the stochastic regularization effect that helps prevent over-fitting. Conclusion: Adjusting the L2 regularization parameter is the most targeted way to curb high variance while preserving training speed, making it the optimal choice for improving generalization in a SageMaker-hosted deep learning job. References Amazon SageMaker Hyperparameter Tuning – Regularization https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/hyperparameter-sweep.html#hyperparameter-sweep- regularization Deep Learning Regularization Techniques (Official AWS Blog) https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/deep-learning-regularization-techniques-and-aws- sagemaker/

questions.question: An ML engineer is running a hyperparameter tuning job in Amazon SageMaker AI. The hyperparameter tuning job will train an XGBoost model on a tabular dataset that has millions of rows. Each individual training job is configured to use up to 8 GPU instances, which leads to high costs if too many jobs run concurrently. The ML engineer wants to ensure that no more than five training jobs run in parallel at any time while still exploring all hyperparameter configurations. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Set the resource limit for the MaxNumberOfTrainingJobs parameter to 5 in the hyperparameter tuning job.
options.B: Set the resource limit for the MaxParallelTrainingJobs parameter to 5 in the hyperparameter tuning job.
options.C: Set the stopping condition for the MaxRuntimeInSeconds parameter to 5 in the hyperparameter tuning job.
options.D: Set the resource limit for the MaxNumberOfTrainingJobs parameter to 5 inside the script in the hyperparameter training job.

questions.explanation: Technical justification Option B – Set MaxParallelTrainingJobs to 5 The MaxParallelTrainingJobs parameter of a SageMaker Hyperparameter Tuning job directly controls the maximum number of concurrent training jobs that the service will launch. By setting it to 5, the engineer guarantees that no more than five training jobs (each potentially using up to 8 GPUs) run in parallel, thereby limiting total GPU consumption while still allowing the optimizer to explore all configured hyperparameter combinations. This is the purpose-built mechanism for throttling parallelism in SageMaker tuning. Option A – MaxNumberOfTrainingJobs MaxNumberOfTrainingJobs defines the total number of training jobs that will ever be started for a tuning job, not the number running at the same time. It does not limit concurrency, so up to 8 GPU jobs could still execute in parallel, potentially violating the cost constraint. Option C – MaxRuntimeInSeconds This parameter sets an upper bound on the runtime of each individual trial, not on parallel execution. Adjusting it to 5 seconds would terminate training prematurely and does not address the concurrency issue. Option D – Resource limit inside the script Imposing a limit within the Python script would affect only the runtime behavior of that trial; it does not communicate to SageMaker’s scheduler to prevent other jobs from launching. Consequently, the platform could still spin up additional concurrent jobs, bypassing the desired cap. Conclusion Only MaxParallelTrainingJobs provides a native, declarative control to cap simultaneous training executions, making Option B the appropriate solution for constraining up to five parallel GPU-based hyperparameter tuning jobs while still exploring all configurations. References SageMaker Hyperparameter Tuning –Parameter Summary Control Parallelism –MaxParallelTrainingJobs Documentation

questions.question: A company is developing an ML model to forecast future values based on time series data. The dataset includes historical measurements collected at regular intervals and categorical features. The model needs to predict future values based on past patterns and trends. Which algorithm and hyperparameters should the company use to develop the model?

options.A: Use the Amazon SageMaker AI XGBoost algorithm. Set the scale_pos_weight hyperparameter to adjust for class imbalance.
options.B: Use k-means clustering with k to specify the number of clusters.
options.C: Use the Amazon SageMaker AI DeepAR algorithm with matching context_length and prediction_length hyperparameters.
options.D: Use the Amazon SageMaker AI Random Cut Forest (RCF) algorithm with contamination to set the expected proportion of anomalies.

questions.explanation: Justification Correct option – C. Amazon SageMaker AI DeepAR is a managed algorithm for time-series forecasting that uses a probabilistic auto-regressive model. It provides dedicated hyperparameters context_length (how many past time steps are used as input) and prediction_length (how many steps to predict ahead). These parameters can be tuned to match the interval of the historical measurements and the horizon the business needs, while the algorithm natively handles seasonal patterns, trend components, and optionally user-provided categorical covariates. Option A – XGBoost with scale_pos_weight. XGBoost is a gradient-boosted decision-tree framework primarily designed for tabular classification/regression tasks. Although it can be used for forecasting with engineered lag features, it is not a purpose-built time-series algorithm. The scale_pos_weight parameter addresses class imbalance in binary classification, which is irrelevant for continuous-valued forecasts and therefore unnecessary for this use case. Option B – k-means clustering. k-means clusters data points without learning a predictive model. It does not produce future values and cannot capture temporal dependencies or trends. Hence it is unsuitable for forecasting. Option D – Random Cut Forest with contamination. RCF is a semi-supervised anomaly-detection algorithm that flags outliers based on forest-based path length. While it can set an anomaly proportion via contamination, it does not generate forecasts. Its purpose is detection, not prediction of future measurements. Conclusion – DeepAR is the only algorithm among the choices that directly supports multivariate time-series forecasting with configurable context and prediction horizons, making it the optimal fit for the described requirement. References DeepAR Overview and Documentation: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/deepar.html DeepAR API Reference (Hyperparameters): https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/APIReference/API_AlgorithmSpecification.html#sagemaker- AlgorithmSpecification-ExerciseMode Comparison of Amazon Forecasting Algorithms: https://aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/forecasting/algorithmic- approaches/ (under “Built-in algorithms”).

questions.question: A company uses an Amazon EMR cluster to run a data ingestion process for an ML model. An ML engineer notices that the processing time is increasing. Which solution will reduce the processing time MOST cost-effectively?

options.A: Use Spot Instances to increase the number of primary nodes.
options.B: Use Spot Instances to increase the number of core nodes.
options.C: Use Spot Instances to increase the number of task nodes.
options.D: Use On-Demand Instances to increase the number of core nodes.

questions.explanation: Why option C is the most cost-effective solution Task nodes run only application-specific workloads (mappers/reducers) that are fully parallelizable; they can be added in large numbers without increasing the cluster’s control-plane complexity. Spot Instances for task nodes can be up to 90 % cheaper than On-Demand, providing the highest compute capacity per dollar and allowing many concurrent tasks to finish sooner. Adding more task nodes directly reduces the per-task latency, thereby decreasing overall processing time for the ingestion pipeline. Why the other options are less suitable A – Increase primary nodes with Spot: the primary node hosts the Hadoop/YARN services and is required to run continuously; it cannot be cost-effectively replaced by Spot, and adding more primary nodes does not accelerate data-processing tasks. B – Increase core nodes with Spot: core nodes store data in HDFS and must persist across job failures; Spot disruption would cause data loss or job failures, making Spot inappropriate for persistent workloads. D – On-Demand for core nodes: while On-Demand eliminates interruption risk, it is significantly more expensive than Spot; using On-Demand for core nodes yields higher cost without the same parallel-processing benefit that Spot task nodes provide. Result – Adding Spot-priced task nodes (option C) maximizes parallel execution at the lowest incremental cost, delivering the greatest reduction in processing time per dollar spent. References Amazon EMR pricing – Spot Instances: https://aws.amazon.com/emr/pricing/ EMR cluster configuration – Using Spot Instances for task nodes: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/emr/latest/ManagementGuide/emr-spot-instances.html

questions.question: An ML engineer is designing an AI-powered traffic management system to adjust traffic lights during predicted congestion. The system must use near real-time inference to generate predictions to help prevent traffic collisions. The system must use a batch processing pipeline to perform historical analysis of the predictions to continuously refine and improve the model. The historical analysis will take several hours to evaluate how well the predictions correlate with actual outcomes. The system must be able to scale inference endpoints appropriately to meet demand. Which combination of solutions will meet these requirements? (Choose two.)

options.A: Use Amazon SageMaker real-time inference endpoints. Configure the endpoints to scale automatically based on a target tracking scaling policy that uses the metric ConcurrentInvocationsPerinstance.
options.B: Configure reserved concurrency for AWS Lambda functions to process streaming data. Use Lambda SnapStart to connect the Lambda functions to Amazon SageMaker real-time endpoints to support near real- time traffic predictions.
options.C: Configure an Amazon SageMaker Processing job for batch analysis of historical prediction data. Use Amazon EventBridge to schedule the job to run daily. Allow several hours for in-depth analysis to refine and improve the traffic management model.
options.D: Use an Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling group to host containers to support the batch analysis of historical prediction data. Configure scaling based on Amazon CloudWatch metrics to analyze historical traffic patterns and model performance over multiple hours.
options.E: Use an AWS Lambda function to perform the historical analysis. Use Amazon EventBridge to invoke the Lambda function.

questions.explanation: Chosen solutions: A and C A – Deploy a SageMaker real-time inference endpoint that automatically scales with a target-tracking policy (e.g., ConcurrentInvocationsPerInstance metric). This provides the required near-real-time response latencies for traffic-light adjustments and handles variable demand without manual provisioning. C – Use a SageMaker Processing job for batch analysis of historical prediction outcomes. The job can be scheduled daily with EventBridge, runs in its own compute environment, and can consume several hours to evaluate model performance and refine the algorithm. Processing jobs are purpose-built for large-scale, low-frequency analytics and integrate natively with SageMaker, eliminating the need to manage provisioning or scaling manually. Why other options are less suitable B – Lambda SnapStart is designed for low-latency invocations of latency-sensitive functions, not for orchestrating near-real-time inference against a SageMaker endpoint. Using Lambda as a bridge adds an unnecessary layer of latency and complexity, and Lambda’s execution limits are not ideal for the several-hour batch workloads described. D – An EC2 Auto Scaling group can host containers, but it requires ongoing infrastructure management (e.g., AMI selection, lifecycle scripts) and is less efficient for the batch-only analysis that can be executed more simply with a SageMaker Processing job. It also does not inherit the built-in integration with SageMaker model artifacts and datasets, increasing operational overhead. E – A single Lambda function is unsuitable for a multi-hour batch process; Lambda’s maximum timeout is only 15 minutes (unless using async concurrency extensions, which still lack the necessary compute and memory for heavy data-processing). Moreover, Lambda lacks native scheduling capabilities comparable to EventBridge-driven Processing jobs. Thus, combining A (auto-scaling real-time inference) with C (scheduled batch Processing for deep model refinement) satisfies the near-real-time inference requirement, the need for extensive historical analysis, and the scalability constraints of the system. References SageMaker Real-Time Inference Scaling – https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/inference- realtime-endpoints-autoscaling.html SageMaker Processing Jobs with EventBridge Scheduler – https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/batch-processing.html#batch-processing-scheduling
