questions.question: A company wants to launch a new website feature that predicts home prices based on user-supplied home attributes. The attributes include location, square footage, and number of bedrooms and bathrooms. An ML engineer has trained a regression model by using the Amazon SageMaker AI XGBoost algorithm. The model performs well with training data. However, the model significantly underperforms when it is validated against real- world data. Which solution will improve the model’s validation score with the LEAST implementation effort?

options.A: Create a larger training dataset that includes more real-world data. Retrain the model.
options.B: Increase the value of the num_round hyperparameter.
options.C: Change the eval_metric hyperparameter from Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) to Error.
options.D: Increase the value of the lambda hyperparameter.

questions.explanation: Why option D is the best choice Regularization impact – In XGBoost the lambda (L2) parameter controls the magnitude of the penalty on leaf weights. Raising its value adds stronger regularization, which typically reduces variance and improves generalization on unseen data. Minimal implementation overhead – The change can be made directly in the model’s hyper-parameter configuration without retraining, acquiring new data, or altering the evaluation metric. It is a single-line adjustment in the SageMaker training job. Directly addresses under-performance – When a model that fits the training set well fails on real-world validation, it often means the model is not sufficiently constrained from over-fitting. Increasing lambda corrects this with the smallest operational change. Why the other options are less suitable A – Larger training set – Collecting additional real-world data requires substantial data-engineering effort (ingestion, labeling, validation) before a new model can be trained. It does not guarantee a better validation score and involves higher operational cost. B – Increase num_round – Adding more boosting rounds typically increases model complexity. If the current model is already under-performing on validation, more trees are likely to exacerbate over-fitting rather than improve generalization. C – Switch eval_metric to “Error” – Changing the evaluation metric only alters how the metric is reported; it does not affect the model’s training dynamics or its predictive performance. Using “Error” instead of RMSE provides no technical advantage for improving validation accuracy. Result – Raising the lambda regularization parameter is the simplest, most direct way to boost validation performance with the least implementation effort. References XGBoost Parameters: https://xgboost.readthedocs.io/en/stable/parameter.html#parameter-lambda Amazon SageMaker XGBoost Documentation: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/xgboost.html

questions.question: A company is developing an ML model for a customer. The company will train the model on data that is in an Amazon S3 bucket in the customer’s AWS account, named Account A. The company will use Amazon SageMaker AI training jobs in a separate AWS account, named Account B. The company requires cross-account access to the S3 bucket to train the model. The company defines an S3 bucket policy and an IAM policy to allow reads to the S3 bucket that contains the training data. Which additional steps will meet these requirements?

options.A: Create the S3 bucket policy in Account Attach the IAM policy to an IAM role that SageMaker AI uses in Account A.
options.B: Create the S3 bucket policy in Account A. Attach the IAM policy to an IAM role that SageMaker AI uses in Account B.
options.C: Create the S3 bucket policy in Account B. Attach the IAM policy to an IAM role that SageMaker AI uses in Account A.
options.D: Create the S3 bucket policy in Account B. Attach the IAM policy to an IAM role that SageMaker AI uses in Account B.

questions.explanation: Why option B is the correct solution The S3 bucket that stores the training data resides in Account A, so the bucket policy must be defined in that account to grant permission to the external principal. SageMaker training runs in Account B. To access the bucket, the role that SageMaker uses in Account B must have an IAM policy that explicitly allows the required actions (e.g., s3:GetObject, s3:ListBucket) on the bucket. By attaching that IAM policy to an IAM role in Account B and referencing that role in the SageMaker training configuration, the service can assume the role and retrieve the data without needing direct access to Account A’s IAM resources. Adding the bucket policy in Account A to the SageMaker execution role (or a custom role) in Account B satisfies AWS’s principle of least privilege and follows the standard cross-account access pattern: bucket-level permissions are granted from the data-owner account, and the consuming account uses an IAM role to consume them. Why the other options are unsuitable Option A attaches the IAM policy to a role in Account A. The role would execute in the data-owner account, but SageMaker runs jobs in Account B and cannot assume an Account A role unless explicitly granted cross-account permissions, which adds complexity and is unnecessary when a role already exists in Account B. Option C creates the bucket policy in Account B, which is irrelevant because bucket policies are scoped to the account that owns the bucket. Account B does not control the bucket in Account A, so any policy defined there would have no effect. Option D both creates a bucket policy in Account B and attaches the IAM policy to a role also in Account B. This does not address the fact that the target bucket lives in Account A; without a bucket policy in Account A granting access, the role in Account B would have no permission to read the data. Required steps (summarized) 1. In Account A: Add a bucket policy to the training-data S3 bucket that allows the specific IAM role (or the SageMaker service principal) in Account B to s3:GetObject and s3:ListBucket on the bucket/prefix. 2. In Account B: Create an IAM policy that mirrors the permissions needed to read the bucket. Attach that policy to an IAM role (e.g., SageMakerExecutionRole) that will be used by SageMaker training jobs. Reference this role in the SageMaker training job configuration (via --role-arn). These actions together establish the necessary cross-account access while adhering to AWS security best practices. References S3 Bucket Policy – Using Bucket Policies to Grant Access Across Accounts IAM Roles for Amazon SageMaker

questions.question: A company is performing A/B testing on a model that provides product recommendations. The company has deployed two versions of the model and is showing each version of the model to 50% of users randomly. Which metric should the company use to evaluate whether users act on a recommendation?

options.A: The conversion rates between the two versions of the model
options.B: The number of recommendations provided to each user
options.C: The model accuracy on held-out test data
options.D: The latency of model inference

questions.explanation: Justification Option A – Conversion rates between the two versions The conversion rate (e.g., purchases or add-to-cart actions) directly measures user response to a recommendation. In an A/B experiment the goal is to determine which model drives more actual engagements, so comparing the conversion rates of the two treatment groups provides the most relevant performance indicator. Option B – Number of recommendations provided to each user This metric only reflects the breadth of the recommendation list, not whether users act on it. A model could show many items without generating any clicks or purchases, so it is insufficient for evaluating recommendation adoption. Option C – Model accuracy on held-out test data Accuracy on a static test set is a offline metric that does not capture real-world user behavior. In an online A/B test the critical question is whether live users act on the recommendations, which cannot be inferred from accuracy alone. Option D – Latency of model inference Latency is important for user experience, but it is not a measure of whether users act on the recommendation. It should be monitored separately, not used as the primary success metric for conversion impact. Chosen metric – The conversion rate between the two model versions (Option A) directly quantifies the business outcome of interest—user adoption of the recommendations. References AWS Machine Learning Foundations – A/B Testing: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/machine- learning/latest/developerguide/ab-testing.html Amazon SageMaker Hyperparameter Tuning – Evaluation Metrics: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/hyperparameter-evaluation.html

questions.question: A company is using Amazon SageMaker AI to build an ML model to predict customer behavior. The company needs to explain the bias in the model to an auditor. The explanation must focus on demographics data of the customers. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Use SageMaker Clarify to generate a bias report. Send the report to the auditor.
options.B: Use AWS Glue DataBrew to create a job to detect drift in the model's data quality. Send the job output to the auditor.
options.C: Use Amazon Quick Suite (previously known as Amazon QuickSight) integration with SageMaker AI to generate a bias report from Quick Suite. Send the report to the auditor.
options.D: Use Amazon CloudWatch metrics from the SageMaker AI namespace to create a bias dashboard. Share the dashboard with the auditor.

questions.explanation: Why option A is the most appropriate SageMaker Clarify is purpose-built to produce explainability artifacts for SageMaker-trained models, including bias detection and mitigation with respect to protected attributes such as gender, age, or geography. It ingests model artifacts (inference specifications, training data schemas, feature importance, etc.) and automatically generates a bias report that quantifies disparate impact, lifts, and fairness metrics. The report can be exported in a neutral format (HTML, PDF, JSON) and attached to audit documentation, fulfilling the requirement to explain bias to an auditor in a formal, reproducible manner. Why the other options are inadequate B – AWS Glue DataBrew drift detection focuses on data-quality drift (schema changes, missingness, statistical drift) rather than bias evaluation. It does not produce a bias-specific report tied to demographic attributes, so it cannot satisfy the auditor’s demand for a demographic-level fairness analysis. C – QuickSight integration offers dashboards and visual analytics but lacks built-in bias-analysis capabilities. Generating a bias report directly from QuickSight would require custom calculations and custom visualizations, which is not a managed, auditable solution out-of-the-box. D – CloudWatch metrics expose operational metrics (latency, invocations, errors) from the SageMaker namespace. They do not contain fairness or bias measurements and therefore cannot be used to justify bias explanations for an auditor. Summary Only SageMaker Clarify provides a native, automated bias-reporting workflow that can be directly communicated to an auditor, aligning with the audit requirement to focus on demographic data bias. References SageMaker Clarify Documentation – Detecting and Mitigating Bias SageMaker Clarify User Guide – Generating Bias Reports

questions.question: A digital media entertainment company needs real-time video content moderation to ensure compliance during live streaming events. Which solution will meet these requirements with the LEAST operational overhead?

options.A: Use Amazon Rekognition and AWS Lambda to extract and analyze the metadata from the videos’ image frames.
options.B: Use Amazon Rekognition and a large language model (LLM) hosted on Amazon Bedrock to extract and analyze the metadata from the videos’ image frames.
options.C: Use Amazon SageMaker AI to extract and analyze the metadata from the videos’ image frames.
options.D: Use Amazon Transcribe and Amazon Comprehend to extract and analyze the metadata from the videos’ image frames.

questions.explanation: Justification (bullet format) Option A – Rekognition + Lambda Uses Amazon Rekognition Video which provides pre-trained, fully-managed moderation labels (e.g., explicit content, violence, hate symbols) with no model management overhead. Results can be returned instantly via the service’s synchronous DetectModerationLabels API or via asynchronous jobs. AWS Lambda can ingest the video stream, invoke the API, and forward any violations to downstream systems (e.g., alerts, moderation queues) – a fully serverless, pay-per-use architecture that requires virtually no operational maintenance. Option B – Rekognition + LLM on Bedrock Introduces a large language model for metadata extraction and moderation, which adds extra steps (prompt engineering, inference latency, model versioning). Higher operational overhead because you must manage inference resources on Bedrock, monitor model drift, and handle model scaling costs. Option C – SageMaker AI SageMaker requires model training, deployment, endpoint management, and scaling configuration. Even when using pre-trained utilities, the platform’s operational footprint (instances, autoscaling policies, monitoring) exceeds the minimal overhead demanded for real-time moderation. Option D – Transcribe + Comprehend These services focus on speech-to-text and text-analysis and do not provide dedicated video-moderation labels. To infer moderation from image frames you would still need custom computer-vision code or an additional Rekognition step, effectively shifting the complexity back to a self-managed pipeline, increasing operational load. Conclusion: Option A delivers real-time, fully-managed moderation with zero model-maintenance and a serverless execution model, making it the solution with the least operational overhead. References Amazon Rekognition Video – Moderation Labels: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/rekognition/latest/dev/video- moderation.html AWS Lambda – Serverless Compute: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/introduction.html

questions.question: An ML engineer is working on a project to predict customer churn. The dataset contains customer information and is stored in an Amazon S3 bucket. A few rows have blank values in the monthly spend column. The ML engineer needs to replace the blank values by using the mean value of the column. The replacement must be performed as part of an automated data preparation pipeline. What should the ML engineer do to prepare the data?

options.A: Use Amazon Athena with a SQL query that uses the AVG function and an UPDATE operation to replace the missing values.
options.B: Use AWS Glue DataBrew with the impute missing values transformation to replace the missing values.
options.C: Use Amazon Quick Suite (previously known as Amazon QuickSight) with the Calculated Field feature to replace the missing values.
options.D: Use AWS Glue with the DropColumns transformation to replace the missing values.

questions.explanation: Technical justification Option B – AWS Glue DataBrew impute missing values transformation DataBrew is a fully managed, low-code data-preparation service that can read data directly from S3, automatically compute statistics such as the column mean, and replace nulls with that value. The imputation can be expressed as a reusable recipe step, allowing the same logic to be embedded in an automated pipeline (e.g., triggered by an S3 → Glue Job or Step Functions workflow). The transformation is atomic, repeatable, and does not require custom coding or additional read-modify-write steps, making it the most appropriate choice for a production-grade, automated churn-prediction pipeline. Option A – Athena with AVG and UPDATE Athena is a read-only query engine; it cannot perform in-place updates to S3 objects. An UPDATE operation is not supported, so the missing-value replacement would have to be materialized elsewhere, breaking the automated-pipeline requirement. Using Athena for this purpose adds unnecessary complexity and latency compared with purpose-built data-preparation services. Option C – Amazon QuickSight calculated field QuickSight is a business-intelligence visualization tool. Calculated fields apply only to visualizations and do not persist changes to the underlying dataset. They are not designed for batch data-cleansing or integration into ETL/ELT pipelines. Relying on QuickSight would not provide a repeatable, programmatic replacement of missing values across the entire dataset. Option D – AWS Glue DropColumns transformation The DropColumns operation removes entire columns from the data flow; it does not compute or fill missing values. Using it to “replace” blank entries is functionally incorrect and would discard the monthly-spend column entirely, losing valuable predictive information. Conclusion AWS Glue DataBrew’s impute-missing-values transformation directly satisfies the need to fill nulls with the column mean in a reusable, pipeline-friendly manner, making option B the best solution. References AWS Glue DataBrew – Impute missing values: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/databrew/latest/userguide/transforms-impute.html AWS Glue DataBrew – Overview and use cases: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/databrew/latest/userguide/what-is-databrew.html

questions.question: A company is using Amazon SageMaker AI to train a face detection ML model. The company uses the model to ensure that only authorized employees are present on the company premises. The facial data used to train the model must be encrypted. The company must manage the encryption control. Only authorized employees must be able to view the data. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Use SageMaker AI IAM roles and policies to control access. Use AWS Key Management Service (AWS KMS) for encryption.
options.B: Use AWS PrivateLink for SageMaker AI to control access. Use AWS Key Management Service (AWS KMS) for encryption.
options.C: Use AWS PrivateLink for SageMaker AI to control access. Use VPC only mode for encryption.
options.D: Use SageMaker AI IAM roles and policies to control access. Use AWS CloudTrail for encryption.

questions.explanation: Justification Encryption control must be managed by the customer – Only AWS KMS lets you create, rotate, and audit customer-managed keys (CMKs) and enforce key policies that restrict use to specific IAM principals. This satisfies the requirement that the company “must manage the encryption control” and that “only authorized employees must be able to view the data.” Access to training data is governed by identity – SageMaker AI IAM roles and policies can restrict who can read the dataset, launch training jobs, and access the resulting model artifacts. By tying data access to IAM, the company ensures that only vetted employees or service roles can retrieve the encrypted facial images. KMS provides the required at-rest encryption – When the dataset is stored in S3 (or an EFS mount), the bucket can be configured with default CMK-based server-side encryption. The key policy can be scoped to the IAM roles that correspond to authorized employees, guaranteeing that decryption occurs only for permitted principals. Why option A is the only viable answer Option B introduces AWS PrivateLink, which is useful for private connectivity to SageMaker endpoints but does not provide encryption control or access-based decryption; it adds unnecessary complexity. Option C mentions “VPC only mode for encryption,” which is not a recognized encryption mechanism in SageMaker; VPC isolation does not encrypt data. Option D suggests using CloudTrail for encryption, yet CloudTrail is a logging service and cannot encrypt data at rest. Therefore, the combination of IAM-based access control with customer-managed KMS encryption (option A) directly fulfills both the encryption-control and authorized-view requirements. References AWS Key Management Service (KMS) – Encryption and Key Policies https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/concepts.html Amazon SageMaker Security – IAM Roles and Encryption https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/security-iam.html These documents illustrate how to configure KMS-encrypted S3 buckets and restrict access through IAM policies in SageMaker, confirming that option A meets the security and compliance goals.

questions.question: A company is evaluating options to deploy an ML model that will be used to make predictions. The model performs image classification on photos that customers upload. The application that will use the model needs the model to operate with low latency. The company has limited capacity to manage infrastructure and needs to minimize costs. How should the company deploy the model to meet these requirements?

options.A: Deploy the model on Amazon EC2 instances behind an Application Load Balancer.
options.B: Deploy the model to Amazon SageMaker AI real-time inference endpoints.
options.C: Deploy the model on Amazon Elastic Container Service (Amazon ECS) containers that run on AWS Fargate.
options.D: Deploy the model on Kubernetes nodes on an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (Amazon EKS) cluster.

questions.explanation: Why the correct choice is best Low latency & managed service – Amazon SageMaker real-time inference endpoints are fully managed, auto-scaled, and automatically provisioned to deliver sub-second response times, which directly addresses the low-latency requirement. Minimal operational overhead – SageMaker handles the underlying compute, scaling, and patching, allowing the company to focus on model serving rather than infrastructure management. Cost-effective scaling – Pricing is per-invocation and per-instance-hour; the service can scale down to zero when idle, minimizing costs for limited workloads. Integrated monitoring & security – Built-in CloudWatch metrics, auto-scaling policies, and IAM-based access control simplify compliance and observability without extra tooling. Why the other options are less suitable Option A (EC2 + ALB) – Requires manual capacity planning, load-balancer configuration, and scaling scripts; higher operational burden and higher idle cost if traffic is variable. Option C (ECS on Fargate) – Still involves container orchestration, task-definition management, and networking setup; scaling latency is slower and cost is driven by long-running tasks even during low usage. Option D (EKS) – Introduces a full Kubernetes control plane, operational complexity, and typically higher management overhead; not ideal for teams with limited infrastructure capacity. References Amazon SageMaker Real-Time Inference: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/realtime- inference.html Amazon SageMaker Pricing Overview: https://aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/pricing/

questions.question: An ML engineer uses A/B testing to dynamically select recommendation models. The models are deployed on Amazon SageMaker AI endpoints. The ML engineer needs to monitor system metrics such as latency, call volume, and HTTP status codes when the endpoints are invoked. Which solution will meet these requirements with the LEAST operational overhead?

options.A: Use AWS X-Ray tracing to monitor SageMaker AI endpoints.
options.B: Configure Amazon CloudWatch dashboards with AWS Lambda log processors.
options.C: Enable AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) Access Analyzer to track SageMaker AI endpoint metrics.
options.D: Deploy AWS Trusted Advisor checks on SageMaker AI endpoint instances.

questions.explanation: Correct option: B. Configure Amazon CloudWatch dashboards with AWS Lambda log processors Why this choice is optimal Built-in metric collection – SageMaker inference endpoints automatically emit logs in CloudWatch Logs; a Lambda function can subscribe to these logs and emit custom CloudWatch metrics (latency, request volume, HTTP status, error types) without any SDK-level instrumentation. Zero-code for alerting – Once the Lambda publishes metrics, native CloudWatch Alarms and dashboards can be created to visualise latency, call volume, and status codes. This provides real-time monitoring with virtually no ongoing operational tasks. Scalable & serverless – The Lambda runs automatically each time new log events appear, eliminating the need to manage dedicated monitoring clusters or schedule jobs, thus minimizing operational overhead. Full visibility of container-level health – By parsing the structured logs (JSON format) the Lambda can extract metrics such as request duration, response status, and error codes, delivering the precise observability required for A/B testing. Why the other options are less suitable A. Use AWS X-Ray tracing – X-Ray is geared toward distributed tracing of HTTP calls across services, not for aggregating endpoint-level metrics like latency histograms or error counts. It adds tracing overhead and does not directly surface the requested system metrics. C. Enable IAM Access Analyzer – Access Analyzer reviews IAM policies for unauthorized resource access; it provides no performance-related telemetry and cannot report latency, volume, or HTTP status information. D. Deploy Trusted Advisor checks – Trusted Advisor offers cost, security, fault-tolerance, and performance recommendations for AWS resources, but it does not deliver continuous, granular metric dashboards for SageMaker endpoints. Result: Option B delivers a serverless, low-maintenance pipeline to collect and visualise the exact metrics needed, making it the least-operational-overhead solution. References CloudWatch Logs subscription to Lambda: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/logs/SubscriptionFilters.html Building dashboards and alarms with CloudWatch metrics: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/monitoring/cloudwatch-dashboards.html

questions.question: An ML engineer at a credit card company used Amazon SageMaker AI to build and deploy a new ML model. The model was trained on a copy of transactions from the production environment. The copy of transactions contained only a few fraudulent transactions. After deployment to production, the model is underperforming. What should the ML engineer do to improve the model’s performance?

options.A: Retrain the model with a different built-in algorithm that is available in SageMaker AI.
options.B: Use random undersampling in SageMaker Data Wrangler to randomly reduce the majority class samples. Retrain the model.
options.C: Use Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) in SageMaker Data Wrangler to generate synthetic minority samples. Retrain the model.
options.D: Use random oversampling in SageMaker Data Wrangler to randomly duplicate minority samples. Retrain the model.

questions.explanation: Why option C is the correct choice SMOTE ( Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique ) creates synthetic examples of the minority (fraud) class rather than simply duplicating existing cases. By generating new, statistically-plausible fraud records, the model receives richer minority information, which reduces the risk of over-fitting that can occur with naïve random oversampling and preserves more of the original majority data than random undersampling. In the context of SageMaker AI, Data Wrangler provides a built-in SMOTE node that can be incorporated into an end-to-end ML pipeline, allowing the engineer to preprocess the training data before model training without leaving the SageMaker environment. This approach directly addresses the imbalanced nature of the fraud detection problem, leading to a more balanced decision boundary and typically higher recall of fraudulent transactions—critical for a credit-card fraud model. Why the other options are less suitable A – Switch to a different built-in algorithm – The algorithm choice is secondary to the data-balancing issue. Even a powerful algorithm will underperform if the training set remains heavily skewed toward the majority class. B – Random undersampling – Randomly dropping majority-class samples discards potentially useful information and can lead to loss of discriminative patterns; it also reduces the total number of training instances, which may degrade overall performance. D – Random oversampling – Simply duplicating minority instances creates exact copies, which the model can over-fit to and does not improve the ability to generalize to new fraud cases. Implementation note The engineer should add a SMOTE step in the Data Wrangler preprocessing workflow, tune the oversampling ratio to achieve an appropriate class balance, and then retrain the model (e.g., using XGBoost, SageMaker Linear Learner, or another estimator) on the balanced dataset. References SMOTE documentation (Imbalanced-learn library) – https://imbalanced- learn.org/stable/over_sampling.html#smote Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler overview – https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/data- wrangler.html (contains information on using built-in data transformation nodes such as SMOTE)

questions.question: An ML engineer is importing a custom model from the Hugging Face Hub into Amazon Bedrock. The ML engineer wants to use the model with the Amazon Bedrock API in an agentic AI application. Which combination of steps will meet these requirements? (Choose two.)

options.A: Import the model for deployment by using Amazon Bedrock On-Demand mode.
options.B: Convert the model to a quantized format. Import the quantized model after conversion.
options.C: Import the model directly by using the Amazon SageMaker AI SDK in the agentic AI application.
options.D: During the model import process, configure an auto-scaling policy before using the model in the agentic AI application.
options.E: In the import job, provide the complete Amazon S3 URI that points to the Hugging Face model files. Ensure that the S3 URI is in the same AWS account as the Amazon Bedrock import job.

questions.explanation: Why the correct combination (B + E) is appropriate E – Provide the complete Amazon S3 URI that points to the Hugging Face model files and ensure the URI resides in the same AWS account as the Bedrock import job. Bedrock can only import custom models that are accessible via an S3 location owned by the importing account; this step makes the model files available for ingestion. B – Convert the model to a quantized format (e.g., INT8) before import and upload the quantized artifact to S3. Quantization reduces model size and latency, satisfies Bedrock’s size and precision constraints, and enables efficient inference when the model is later served through the Bedrock API. Why the other options are not suitable A – Import using Amazon Bedrock On-Demand mode – On-Demand mode is intended for running already-deployed models; it does not affect the import process itself and does not meet the requirement to prepare the model for Bedrock. C – Import the model directly by using the Amazon SageMaker AI SDK – SageMaker is a separate ML service; Bedrock requires model artifacts to be staged in S3 for import, not loaded via SageMaker SDK within an application. D – Configure an auto-scaling policy during import – Auto-scaling applies to inferencing resources after deployment, not to the model-import step, and therefore does not meet the prerequisite of making the model available in Bedrock. C (already mentioned) – Importing through SageMaker bypasses Bedrock’s import workflow and does not satisfy the “agentic AI application” requirement tied to Bedrock’s runtime. Any other questions? References Model import from S3 for Amazon Bedrock: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/model- import.html Model quantization for Bedrock: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/quantize- models.html

questions.question: A company is developing a classification model to predict profitability. The company wants to incorporate new unstructured data that the company bought from another company. The company needs data access to be restricted to only the company’s data science team to maintain data security. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Store the new data in an encrypted Amazon S3 bucket. Create an IAM role for the data science team. Use IAM policies to allow access only by using the new IAM role.
options.B: Load the new data into an Amazon RDS database with public access disabled. Share the connection credentials with the data science team in an email message.
options.C: Upload the new data into a feature group in Amazon SageMaker Feature Store. Enable all Amazon SageMaker AI users.
options.D: Store the new data in a local file on each data scientist’s laptop to ensure that only that specific data scientists can access the data.

questions.explanation: Why Option A is the optimal solution Fine-grained access control – An IAM role scoped to the data-science group can be attached to an encrypted S3 bucket, ensuring that only the designated team members (and any services they use) can read the objects. Data-level encryption – S3 server-side encryption (e.g., SSE-KMS) protects the data at rest, meeting security-by-design requirements without additional infrastructure. Least-privilege principle – IAM policies can restrict actions to s3:GetObject (and optionally s3:ListBucket) for the bucket, preventing write or administrative actions from the team. Scalable and managed – S3 handles massive file sizes and integrates natively with downstream processing frameworks (e.g., SageMaker, EMR), allowing the ML pipeline to ingest the data seamlessly. Why the other options are unsuitable Option B – RDS is relational and not intended for large unstructured files; disabling public access does not automatically enforce team-only access, and sharing credentials via email violates credential-management best practices. Option C – Enabling all SageMaker AI users broadens access far beyond the data-science team, contravening the requirement for strict restriction. Option D – Storing data locally on individual laptops fragments the dataset, prevents reproducible pipelines, and introduces inconsistent security controls across devices. References Amazon S3 Security Best Practices: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/security-best- practices.html Managing IAM Policies for Amazon S3 Access: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/access- policy-conditions.html These sources provide the underlying design guidance for implementing secure, role-based access to S3 buckets in a data-science workflow.

questions.question: A company is developing ML models by using PyTorch and TensorFlow estimators with Amazon SageMaker AI. An ML engineer configures the SageMaker AI estimator and now needs to initiate a training job that uses a training dataset. Which SageMaker AI SDK method can initiate the training job?

options.A: fit method
options.B: create_model method
options.C: deploy method
options.D: predict method

questions.explanation: Technical Justification The fit method of a SageMaker AI Estimator (or its subclasses such as PyTorchEstimator and TensorFlowEstimator) is explicitly designed to launch a training job on Amazon SageMaker. It takes care of creating the training container, uploading the input data, configuring the job parameters (instance type, role, hyper-parameters, etc.) and submitting the job to the service. Once the job completes, the resulting model artifact is stored in the default S3 output location, ready for model registration or deployment. create_model only registers the trained model in SageMaker Model Registry; it does not start any execution. It is used after training to define a model artifact that can later be deployed. deploy creates a real-time inference endpoint from a registered model; it is unrelated to the training workflow and therefore cannot initiate a training job. predict performs inference against an already-deployed endpoint or batch transform job; it never runs training. Hence, among the listed SDK methods, only the fit method initiates a training job for PyTorch and TensorFlow estimators within SageMaker AI. References SageMaker Python SDK – Estimator.fit(): https://sdk.aws.amazon.com/sageMaker/latest/python- reference/estimators.html#fit SageMaker SDK – Training Jobs Overview: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/sdk- training.html#sdk-training-jobs

questions.question: An ML engineer is preparing a dataset of medical records to train an ML model. The model will predict the likelihood of patients developing a specific disease. The dataset includes numeric features and categorical features. The ML engineer will train the model by using a custom container that accesses data stored in an Amazon Elastic File System (Amazon EFS) file system. How should the ML engineer prepare the dataset to train the model?

options.A: Select a random sample of records. Encode the categorical features. Load the pre-processed data into the file system.
options.B: Perform data validation to check for missing or invalid values. Encode the categorical features. Scale the numeric features. Select a random sample of records.
options.C: Scale the numeric features. Select a random sample of records for the initial model training. Load the pre- processed data into the file system.
options.D: Compress the data files. Remove duplicate records. Load the pre-processed data into the file system.

questions.explanation: Justification for the correct choice (Option B) Data validation must be performed before any transformation so that missing, corrupted, or out-of-range values are identified and handled. This prevents the model from learning artefacts that could degrade predictive performance on unseen patients. Encoding of categorical features is required because most supervised algorithms operate only on numeric representations; proper encoding (e.g., one-hot or ordinal) preserves the semantics of the categories while avoiding unintended ordinal assumptions. Scaling of numeric features (standardization or min-max normalization) is essential when the algorithm is sensitive to feature magnitude—common in gradient-based models and many distance-based classifiers. Scaling also ensures that numeric and encoded features contribute comparably to the model’s learned weights. Random sampling of records provides an unbiased training set and helps to mitigate over-representation of any sub-population, which is critical when dealing with medical data that may be skewed by diagnosis codes or referral patterns. All pre-processed artefacts can then be loaded into Amazon EFS, where the custom container can mount the file system at runtime. This approach guarantees that every component of the pipeline (validation, encoding, scaling, sampling) is reproducible and that the container consumes a clean, well-structured dataset. Why the other options are less suitable Option A lacks a systematic data-quality check and any feature scaling. Without validation the model may ingest invalid records; without scaling, numeric features could dominate the learned representation, leading to unstable training. Option C performs scaling and sampling but omits explicit validation and encoding steps. In medical datasets, missing values and unencoded categories are common; ignoring them can cause downstream errors or biased models. Option D focuses only on compression and deduplication. While these operations reduce storage footprint, they do not address the core preprocessing needs—validation, encoding, scaling, or sampling—that are required for a reliable, reproducible training dataset. References AWS Documentation – Amazon Elastic File System (EFS) – File System Mounting AWS Documentation – Machine Learning Pipeline Best Practices – Data Preparation and Feature Engineering

questions.question: An ML engineer used Amazon SageMaker Studio to train a neural network. The neural network logs its information into TensorBoard and uses stochastic gradient descent (SGD) as the optimizer. The ML engineer reviewed training graphs and discovered that the accuracy was not increasing and the loss was decreasing very slowly. The ML engineer needs to improve the model’s performance without increasing the total training time. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.A: Increase the initial learning rate.
options.B: Decrease the initial learning rate.
options.C: Increase the total number of epochs.
options.D: Decrease the total number of epochs.

questions.explanation: Why option A – Increase the initial learning rate – is the best choice A higher learning rate accelerates the parameter updates in stochastic gradient descent, allowing the optimizer to traverse the loss landscape more quickly. When loss is only decreasing slowly while accuracy plateaus, it usually indicates that the current learning rate is too low to make sufficient progress toward a better region of the parameter space. Raising the initial learning rate can therefore move the model out of a “flat” region faster without requiring additional epochs or extra training time. This adjustment keeps the total training wall-clock time unchanged, satisfying the requirement to improve performance within the same time budget. Why the other options are less suitable B. Decrease the initial learning rate would make updates even smaller, further slowing loss reduction and making the stagnant accuracy problem worse. C. Increase the total number of epochs does extend training time, but the scenario explicitly forbids increasing total training time; it would consume more compute resources without addressing the underlying rate issue. D. Decrease the total number of epochs would cut training earlier, likely yielding an even poorer model and would not help the accuracy plateau. Thus, the only solution that directly tackles the slow loss descent while keeping overall training duration fixed is to increase the initial learning rate. References Learning Rate Best Practices in SageMaker Training – AWS documentation on tuning the learning rate for faster convergence. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/hyperparameter-tuning.html TensorBoard Integration with Amazon SageMaker – Guide to visualizing training metrics and diagnosing issues such as slow loss reduction. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/using- tensorboard.html

questions.question: A company wants to use large language models (LLMs) that are supported by Amazon Bedrock to develop a chat interface for the company’s internal technical documentation. The company stores the documentation as dozens of text files that are several megabytes in total size. The company updates the text files often. Which solution will meet these requirements MOST cost-effectively?

options.A: Create a new LLM on Amazon Bedrock. Train the new LLM on the original dataset and the company documentation. Make the new model available in Bedrock for calls from the chat interface.
options.B: Integrate the company documentation with Amazon Bedrock guardrails. Invoke the guardrails for all Amazon Bedrock calls from the chat interface.
options.C: Use all the text files to fine tune a model in Amazon Bedrock. Use the fine-tuned model to process user prompts.
options.D: Upload all the text files to an Amazon Bedrock knowledge base. Use the knowledge base to provide context when the chat interface makes calls to Amazon Bedrock.

questions.explanation: Why option D is the most cost-effective solution Knowledge-base approach leverages native Bedrock features – uploading documents to an Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Base enables semantic search and retrieval without custom model training or fine-tuning, eliminating the compute cost of training and inference on a dedicated model. Supports frequent updates – new or revised text files can be re-indexed into the knowledge base on-the-fly, so the system automatically reflects the latest documentation without retraining. Scales with usage – the Bedrock runtime charges per token for retrieval and generation; only the portion of the knowledge base needed for a query is accessed, keeping operational costs proportional to actual usage. Reduced engineering effort – no need to manage model weights, training pipelines, or infrastructure for fine-tuned models; the knowledge-base handles context injection automatically for the chat interface. Why the other options are less suitable Option A – Train a new LLM on the documentation Requires substantial compute for training and ongoing inference, incurring high fixed and variable costs. Training must be refreshed whenever the documentation changes, adding operational overhead and cost. Option B – Integrate Bedrock guardrails only Guardrails protect against unsafe outputs but do not provide any mechanism for grounding the model in the company’s documentation. The chat interface would still generate answers without context from the internal files, so it does not meet the requirement of using the documentation as a source of truth. Option C – Fine-tune a model on the text files Fine-tuning still involves training cycles, storage of a custom model, and periodic re-training to keep up with updates, leading to higher ongoing expenses. Maintenance of a fine-tuned model is more complex than simply updating a searchable knowledge base, especially when the source material changes frequently. Conclusion A Bedrock Knowledge Base delivers the required contextual grounding, automatic handling of frequent document updates, and pay-per-use pricing, making it the most cost-effective and operationally efficient choice. References Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases – Overview: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/knowledge-bases.html Pricing for Amazon Bedrock – Token-based cost model: https://aws.amazon.com/bedrock/pricing/

questions.question: An ML engineer is using AWS CodeDeploy to deploy new versions of containers that perform inference to an Amazon Elastic Container Service (Amazon ECS) compute platform. The ML engineer needs to define a deployment configuration to shift traffic to the updated ECS task set. The deployment configuration must shift 10% of traffic to the updated containers in the first increment. The remaining 90% of traffic must shift to the updated containers within 10 to 15 minutes. Which deployment configuration will meet these requirements?

options.A: CodeDeployDefault.LambdaLinear10PercentEvery10Minutes
options.B: CodeDeployDefault.ECSAllAtOnce
options.C: CodeDeployDefault.ECSCanary10Percent15Minutes
options.D: CodeDeployDefault.LambdaCanary10Percent15Minutes

questions.explanation: Why option C is the correct choice ECSCanary10Percent15Minutes is explicitly designed for Amazon ECS blue/green (CodeDeploy) deployments where the traffic shift starts with a small percentage and then proceeds after a configurable wait time. The name indicates 10 % of traffic is moved to the new task set first, then after 15 minutes the remaining 90 % is shifted—exactly matching the required incremental rollout and timing constraints. This configuration is part of the built-in ECS deployment strategies in CodeDeploy, so it can be referenced directly in the deployment group settings without custom scripts. Why the other options are unsuitable CodeDeployDefault.LambdaLinear10PercentEvery10Minutes – Targets AWS Lambda functions, not ECS task sets, and its pacing (every 10 minutes) does not provide a single 10-to-15-minute window for the bulk shift. CodeDeployDefault.ECSAllAtOnce – Deploys the new task set to 100 % of traffic immediately, violating the 10 % incremental start requirement. CodeDeployDefault.LambdaCanary10Percent15Minutes – Like the LambdaLinear variant, it applies to Lambda and not to ECS, so it cannot be used for container task rollouts. Therefore, ECSCanary10Percent15Minutes is the only built-in deployment configuration that fulfills the specified traffic-shift pattern for ECS. References AWS CodeDeploy deployment configurations for Amazon ECS: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/codedeploy/latest/userguide/undeploy-ecs.html#ecs-deployment- configurations Blue/green deployment strategy for ECS with traffic shifting: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/codedeploy/latest/userguide/deploy-ecs-code-deploy.html#ecs-code-deploy- bluegreen-strategy

questions.question: A company uses an ML model to recommend videos to users. The model is deployed on Amazon SageMaker AI. The model performed well initially after deployment, but the model’s performance has degraded over time. Which solution can the company use to identify model drift in the future?

options.A: Create a monitoring job in SageMaker Model Monitor. Then create a baseline from the training dataset.
options.B: Create a baseline from the training dataset. Then create a monitoring job in SageMaker Model Monitor.
options.C: Create a baseline by using a built-in rule in SageMaker Clarify. Monitor the drift in Amazon CloudWatch.
options.D: Retrain the model on new data. Compare the retrained model's performance to the original model’s performance.

questions.explanation: Why option B is the best choice Sequential workflow required: To detect drift, you first define a statistical baseline that represents the expected distribution of input features (or target labels) in the production environment. In SageMaker Model Monitor this baseline is created from the original training dataset. Continuous monitoring: Once the baseline exists, you can launch a monitoring job that periodically compares the live inference data stream with the baseline. When statistical thresholds are crossed, the job raises alerts, automatically surfacing drift for investigation. Built-in integration: SageMaker Model Monitor natively supports drift detection on both feature-drift and predictor-drift (including label drift) without requiring custom code or external services. Alerts can be routed to CloudWatch Events, SNS, or Lambda for automated remediation pipelines. Scalability and operationalization: The monitoring job runs on a managed infrastructure, scales with your traffic, and integrates seamlessly with the existing SageMaker deployment, fitting the exam-level expectation of a production-ready solution. Why the other options are less suitable Option A – “Create a monitoring job … then create a baseline…” The monitoring job must reference an already-created baseline; otherwise the system cannot compute drift metrics. Starting with the job first leads to inaccurate or non-functional monitoring and does not align with the documented workflow in the SageMaker documentation. Option C – “Create a baseline by using a built-in rule in SageMaker Clarify. Monitor the drift in Amazon CloudWatch.” SageMaker Clarify is designed for bias assessment, feature importance, and explainability, not for continuous drift detection. Model drift requires a dedicated monitoring job that compares data distributions over time; CloudWatch metrics alone cannot provide the statistical comparison needed for drift detection. Option D – “Retrain the model on new data. Compare the retrained model's performance to the original model’s performance.” Retraining is a remediation step taken after drift has been identified. It does not help in identifying drift proactively and therefore does not satisfy the question’s requirement of “identifying model drift in the future.” Key takeaway: The correct approach is to establish a baseline from the training data and then launch a monitoring job in SageMaker Model Monitor, which is precisely what option B describes.
